Consecutive gradient differences are used as a cheap proxy for local geometric change, together with a step-normalized secant curvature indicator to control the correction strength. The framework is plug-and-play, computationally efficient, and broadly compatible with existing optimizers, without explicitly forming second-order matrices. Experiments on diverse PDE benchmarks show consistent improvements in convergence speed, training stability, and solution accuracy over standard optimizers and strong baselines, including on the high-dimensional heat equation, Gray--Scott system, Belousov--Zhabotinsky system, and 2D Kuramoto--Sivashinsky system.

AI.tldr: A lightweight curvature-aware optimization framework for PINNs that augments first-order optimizers with adaptive secant-based corrections to account for local geometry, improving convergence, stability, and accuracy on challenging PDEs.
AI.motivation: PINNs encounter slow convergence and instability due to anisotropic, rapidly varying loss landscapes; there is a need for a computationally cheap, model-agnostic curvature-aware approach.
AI.method: Use consecutive gradient differences as a cheap proxy for local geometry change; combine with a step-normalized secant curvature indicator to modulate correction strength; plugin to existing optimizers without forming second-order matrices; lightweight and efficient.
AI.result: Experiments on various PDE benchmarks (high-dimensional heat equation, Gray–Scott, Belousov–Zhabotinsky, 2D Kuramoto–Sivashinsky) show consistent improvements in convergence speed, stability, and solution accuracy over standard optimizers and baselines.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework is effective, broadly compatible, and computationally efficient, offering practical gains for training PINNs on challenging PDEs.

title: Long-Term Memory for VLA-based Agents in Open-World Task Execution
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have demonstrated significant potential for embodied decision-making; however, their application in complex chemical laboratory automation remains restricted by limited long-horizon reasoning and the absence of persistent experience accumulation. Existing frameworks typically treat planning and execution as decoupled processes, often failing to consolidate successful strategies, which results in inefficient trial-and-error in multi-stage protocols. In this paper, we propose ChemBot, a dual-layer, closed-loop framework that integrates an autonomous AI agent with a progress-aware VLA model (Skill-VLA) for hierarchical task decomposition and execution. ChemBot utilizes a dual-layer memory architecture to consolidate successful trajectories into retrievable assets, while a Model Context Protocol (MCP) server facilitates efficient sub-agent and tool orchestration. To address the inherent limitations of VLA models, we further implement a future-state-based asynchronous inference mechanism to mitigate trajectory discontinuities. Extensive experiments on collaborative robots demonstrate that ChemBot achieves superior operational safety, precision, and task success rates compared to existing VLA baselines in complex, long-horizon chemical experimentation.

AI.tldr: ChemBot presents a dual-layer, memory-backed closed-loop framework for long-horizon chemical experimentation using Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, improving planning-execution integration, experience consolidation, and execution via a progress-aware Skill-VLA, MCP orchestration, and async future-state inference.
AI.motivation: Address limited long-horizon reasoning and lack of persistent experience in VLA-based lab automation by tightly coupling planning, action, and memory, enabling reuse of successful strategies across multi-stage protocols.
AI.method: A two-layer architecture with a progress-aware Skill-VLA for hierarchical task decomposition; a dual-layer memory system to store retrievable successful trajectories; a Model Context Protocol (MCP) server to orchestrate sub-agents and tools; a future-state-based asynchronous inference mechanism to reduce trajectory discontinuities.
AI.result: Experiments on collaborative robots show ChemBot achieves higher safety, precision, and task success rates than existing VLA baselines in complex, long-horizon chemical experiments.
AI.conclusion: ChemBot demonstrates that integrating memory consolidation, context-aware planning, and asynchronous inference with VLA models yields robust, safer, and more reliable long-horizon lab automation, with potential applicability beyond chemistry.

title: Bilevel Optimization of Agent Skills via Monte Carlo Tree Search
summary: Agent \texttt{skills} are structured collections of instructions, tools, and supporting resources that help large language model (LLM) agents perform particular classes of tasks. Empirical evidence shows that the design of \texttt{skills} can materially affect agent task performance, yet systematically optimizing \texttt{skills} remains challenging. Since a \texttt{skill} comprises instructions, tools, and supporting resources in a structured way, optimizing it requires jointly determining both the structure of these components and the content each component contains. This gives rise to a complex decision space with strong interdependence across structure and components. We therefore represent these two coupled decisions as \texttt{skill} structure and component content, and formulate \texttt{skill} optimization as a bilevel optimization problem. We propose a bilevel optimization framework in which an outer loop employs Monte Carlo Tree Search to determine the \texttt{skill} structure, while an inner loop refines the component content within the structure selected by the outer loop. In both loops, we employ LLMs to assist the optimization procedure. We evaluate the proposed framework on an open-source Operations Research Question Answering dataset, and the experimental results suggest that the bilevel optimization framework improves the performance of the agents with the optimized \texttt{skill}.

AI.tldr: Bilevel optimization framework for LLM agent skills: outer loop uses Monte Carlo Tree Search to design skill structure; inner loop optimizes component content; LLMs assist in both loops; validated on an open-source Operations Research Question Answering dataset, showing performance gains.
AI.motivation: Skill design for LLM agents involves coupled decisions on structure (which tools/instructions/resources) and content (their details). This coupled space is large and interdependent, making joint optimization challenging; existing single-loop or unimodal approaches may underperform.
AI.method: Formulate skill optimization as a bilevel problem: outer loop uses Monte Carlo Tree Search to explore and select skill structures; inner loop refines the content within the chosen structure. Both loops leverage LLMs to assist optimization (e.g., generation, evaluation, refinement). Evaluation conducted on an open-source Operations Research Question Answering dataset.
AI.result: The framework yields improved agent performance with optimized skills compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: A bilevel optimization framework effectively couples structure search with content refinement for skill design in LLM agents. MCTS efficiently navigates the discrete structure space, while the inner loop optimizes content; results suggest performance gains and point to broader applicability and future refinements.

title: CLIMB: Controllable Longitudinal Brain Image Generation using Mamba-based Latent Diffusion Model and Gaussian-aligned Autoencoder
summary: Latent diffusion models have emerged as powerful generative models in medical imaging, enabling the synthesis of high quality brain magnetic resonance imaging scans. In particular, predicting the evolution of a patients brain can aid in early intervention, prognosis, and treatment planning. In this study, we introduce CLIMB, Controllable Longitudinal brain Image generation via state space based latent diffusion model, an advanced framework for modeling temporal changes in brain structure. CLIMB is designed to model the structural evolution of the brain structure over time, utilizing a baseline MRI scan and its acquisition age as foundational inputs. Additionally, multiple conditional variables, including projected age, gender, disease status, genetic information, and brain structure volumes, are incorporated to enhance the temporal modeling of anatomical changes. Unlike existing LDM methods that rely on self attention modules, which effectively capture contextual information from input images but are computationally expensive, our approach leverages state space, a state space model architecture that substantially reduces computational overhead while preserving high-quality image synthesis. Furthermore, we introduce a Gaussian-aligned autoencoder that extracts latent representations conforming to prior distributions without the sampling noise inherent in conventional variational autoencoders. We train and evaluate our proposed model on the Alzheimers Disease Neuroimaging Initiative dataset, consisting of 6,306 MRI scans from 1,390 participants. By comparing generated images with real MRI scans, CLIMB achieves a structural similarity index of 0.9433, demonstrating notable improvements over existing methods.

AI.tldr: CLIMB: a state-space latent diffusion model for controllable longitudinal brain MRI generation using baseline scans and rich conditioning (age, gender, disease status, genetics, brain volumes); achieves high structural fidelity (SSIM 0.9433 on ADNI) with reduced compute compared to attention-heavy LDMs.
AI.motivation: Modeling temporal evolution of brain structure is clinically valuable for early intervention, prognosis, and treatment planning. Conventional latent diffusion models rely on self-attention, which is computationally expensive; there is a need for efficient, scalable architectures that can incorporate multimodal, longitudinal conditioning.
AI.method: CLIMB uses a state-space latent diffusion framework conditioned on a baseline MRI, baseline acquisition age, projected age, gender, disease status, genetic information, and brain structure volumes. It replaces self-attention with a state-space architecture to reduce computation. A Gaussian-aligned autoencoder enforces latent representations to match prior distributions without sampling noise typical of variational autoencoders. Trained and evaluated on ADNI (6,306 scans from 1,390 participants).
AI.result: The model achieves a structural similarity index (SSIM) of 0.9433 between generated and real MRI scans, indicating strong fidelity in modeling longitudinal brain changes and improvements over existing methods.
AI.conclusion: CLIMB offers a scalable, accurate framework for longitudinal brain image synthesis with controllable conditioning, enabling better forecasting of brain evolution for clinical planning and intervention. The approach shows promise for broader application to other longitudinal medical imaging tasks.

title: Python library supporting Discrete Variational Formulations and training solutions with Collocation-based Robust Variational Physics Informed Neural Networks (DVF-CRVPINN)
comment: Python library, Robust Variational Physics-Informed Neural Networks, Collocation Methods, Robust loss, Stokes Equations, Laplace problem
summary: We explore the possibility of solving Partial Differential Equations (PDEs) using discrete weak formulations. We propose a programming environment for defining a discrete computational domain, introducing discrete functions defined over a set of points, constructing discrete inner products, and introducing discrete weak formulations employing Kronecker delta test functions. Building on this setup, we propose a discrete neural network representation, training the solution function defined over a discrete set of points and employing discrete finite difference derivatives in the automatic differentiation procedures. As a challenging computational model example, we focus on Stokes equations in two-dimensions, defined over a discrete set of points. We train the solution using the discrete weak residual and the Adamax algorithm with discrete automatic differentiation of the discrete gradients. Despite introducing the python environment, we also provide a rigorous mathematical formulation based on discrete weak formulations, proving the well-posedness and robustness of the loss function. The solution of the discrete weak formulations is based on neural network training employing a robust loss function that is related to the true error. In this way, we have a robust control of the numerical error during the training of the neural networks. Besides the Stokes formulation, we also explain the functionality of the proposed library using the Laplace problem formulation.

AI.tldr: A framework for solving PDEs via discrete weak formulations using neural networks, with a discrete domain, discrete functions on a point set, discrete inner products, and Kronecker-delta test functions; demonstrates on 2D Stokes and Laplace, with robust loss tied to true error and mathematical well-posedness proofs.
AI.motivation: To enable robust, well-posed, neural-network-based solvers for PDEs by formulating them in a discrete weak setting that can leverage automatic differentiation and discrete calculus.
AI.method: Define a discrete computational grid/point set and discrete functions; construct discrete inner products; formulate discrete weak forms using Kronecker delta test functions; represent the solution with a neural network defined on the point set; compute discrete FD derivatives via automatic differentiation; train with the discrete weak residual using Adamax; provide rigorous mathematical formulation proving well-posedness; apply to Stokes in 2D and Laplace as demonstrations.
AI.result: Demonstrates feasibility of solving PDEs in a discrete weak formulation with neural nets; achieves robust control of numerical error through a loss tied to the true error; provides a Python-based environment and accompanying theoretical guarantees.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers a principled, robust approach to PDE solving via discrete weak formulations and neural networks, with theoretical guarantees and applicability to multiple PDEs beyond Stokes and Laplace; opens avenues for discrete calculus-based neural solvers and robust error control.

title: Fuzzy Logic Theory-based Adaptive Reward Shaping for Robust Reinforcement Learning (FARS)
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) often struggles in real-world tasks with high-dimensional state spaces and long horizons, where sparse or fixed rewards severely slow down exploration and cause agents to get trapped in local optima. This paper presents a fuzzy logic based reward shaping method that integrates human intuition into RL reward design. By encoding expert knowledge into adaptive and interpreable terms, fuzzy rules promote stable learning and reduce sensitivity to hyperparameters. The proposed method leverages these properties to adapt reward contributions based on the agent state, enabling smoother transitions between fast motion and precise control in challenging navigation tasks. Extensive simulation results on autonomous drone racing benchmarks show stable learning behavior and consistent task performance across scenarios of increasing difficulty. The proposed method achieves faster convergence and reduced performance variability across training seeds in more challenging environments, with success rates improving by up to approximately 5 percent compared to non fuzzy reward formulations.

AI.tldr: A fuzzy-logic reward shaping approach improves RL in high-dimensional, long-horizon tasks by incorporating expert knowledge into adaptive, interpretable reward terms, yielding more stable learning and modest gains in performance for challenging drone racing tasks.
AI.motivation: RL struggles with sparse/fixed rewards, high-dimensional state spaces, and long horizons, leading to slow exploration and local optima. Incorporating human intuition via fuzzy reward shaping aims to stabilize learning and reduce hyperparameter sensitivity.
AI.method: Encode expert knowledge into adaptive fuzzy rules to shape rewards based on agent state. The method uses interpretable fuzzy terms to modulate reward contributions, enabling smoother transitions between fast motion and precise control in navigation tasks.
AI.result: Simulation on autonomous drone racing benchmarks shows stable learning and robust performance across increasingly difficult scenarios. The approach achieves faster convergence, reduced training variance across seeds, and up to ~5% improvement in success rate over non-fuzzy reward formulations.
AI.conclusion: Fuzzy reward shaping with interpretable rules enhances RL stability and performance in challenging, high-dimensional navigation tasks, offering more reliable learning and modest gains in success rates.

title: The World Leaks the Future: Harness Evolution for Future Prediction Agents
summary: Many consequential decisions must be made before the relevant outcome is known. Such problems are commonly framed as \emph{future prediction}, where an LLM agent must form a prediction for an unresolved question using only the public information available at the prediction time. The setting is difficult because public evidence evolves while useful supervision arrives only after the question is resolved, so most existing approaches still improve mainly from final outcomes. Yet final outcomes are too coarse to guide earlier factor tracking, evidence gathering and interpretation, or uncertainty handling. When the same unresolved question is revisited over time, temporal contrasts between earlier and later predictions can expose omissions in the earlier prediction process; we call this signal \emph{internal feedback}. We introduce \emph{Milkyway}, a self-evolving agent system that keeps the base model fixed and instead updates a persistent \emph{future prediction harness} for factor tracking, evidence gathering and interpretation, and uncertainty handling. Across repeated predictions on the same unresolved question, \emph{Milkyway} extracts internal feedback and writes reusable guidance back into the harness, so later predictions on that question can improve before the outcome is known. After the question is resolved, the final outcome provides a \emph{retrospective check} before the updated harness is carried forward to subsequent questions. On FutureX and FutureWorld, Milkyway achieves the best overall score among the compared methods, improving FutureX from 44.07 to 60.90 and FutureWorld from 62.22 to 77.96.

AI.tldr: Milkyway is a self-evolving agent that uses a persistent future-prediction harness to improve unresolved-question predictions by extracting internal feedback from repeated inquiries; outcomes are used for retrospective checks; achieves leading scores on FutureX and FutureWorld.
AI.motivation: Public evidence evolves over time and useful supervision arrives only after questions are resolved. Relying on final outcomes is insufficient for guiding early evidence gathering, and comparing predictions across time provides signals of omissions (internal feedback). The work proposes a reusable learning signal to improve predictions without altering the base model.
AI.method: A fixed-base-model agent is augmented with a persistent 'future prediction harness' that tracks factors, evidence gathering, interpretation, and uncertainty handling. Across repeated predictions on the same unresolved question, the harness is updated with internal feedback extracted from earlier predictions. Later predictions use the updated harness to improve before resolution. After the outcome is known, a retrospective check refines the harness for subsequent questions. Evaluation is conducted on FutureX and FutureWorld, showing performance gains.
AI.result: Repeated predictions yield substantial improvements: FutureX 44.07 -> 60.90; FutureWorld 62.22 -> 77.96, with Milkyway achieving the best overall score among compared methods.
AI.conclusion: Internal feedback mechanisms enable a self-evolving prediction process that improves early-stage reasoning and uncertainty handling without changing the base model. The harness accumulates reusable guidance across questions, enabling better factor tracking and evidence interpretation.

title: AdaVFM: Adaptive Vision Foundation Models for Edge Intelligence via LLM-Guided Execution
summary: Language-aligned vision foundation models (VFMs) enable versatile visual understanding for always-on contextual AI, but their deployment on edge devices is hindered by strict latency and power constraints. We present AdaVFM, an adaptive framework for efficient on-device inference of language-aligned VFMs that dynamically adjusts computation based on scene context and task complexity. Our key insight is that the effect of model size reduction on performance is task-dependent in vision applications, motivating a runtime-adaptive execution strategy. AdaVFM integrates neural architecture search (NAS) into the language-aligned VFM backbone to enable lightweight subnet execution during runtime. A multimodal large language model (LLM) deployed on the cloud enables runtime control with a context-aware agent. This synergy allows efficient model adaptation under diverse conditions while maintaining strong accuracy. Extensive experiments on zero-shot classification and open-vocabulary segmentation demonstrate that AdaVFM achieves state-of-the-art accuracy-efficiency trade-offs, surpassing prior baselines by up to $7.9\%$ in acc@1 on IN1K and $5.2\%$ mIoU on ADE20K over the best models of comparable VFM sizes. For models with similar accuracy, AdaVFM further reduces average FLOPs by up to $77.9\%$.

AI.tldr: AdaVFM uses runtime-adaptive subnet execution for language-aligned vision foundation models on edge, guided by NAS-integrated backbones and a cloud-based context-aware LLM controller, achieving strong accuracy-efficiency trade-offs in zero-shot classification and open-vocabulary segmentation.
AI.motivation: Edge devices require low latency and power efficiency. Model size reductions affect performance in a task-dependent way in vision tasks, motivating adaptive computation strategies.
AI.method: Integrate neural architecture search into the VFM backbone to enable lightweight subnet execution at runtime. A cloud-based multimodal LLM acts as a context-aware controller to guide adaptation based on scene context and task, enabling dynamic adjustment of computation. Evaluations on zero-shot classification (IN1K) and open-vocabulary segmentation (ADE20K) against baselines.
AI.result: AdaVFM achieves state-of-the-art accuracy-efficiency trade-offs, surpassing prior baselines by up to 7.9 percentage points in acc@1 on IN1K and 5.2 percentage points in mIoU on ADE20K for models of comparable VFM sizes. For models with similar accuracy, average FLOPs are reduced by up to 77.9%.
AI.conclusion: Runtime-adaptive execution enabled by NAS-integrated backbones and cloud LLM control provides efficient, accurate language-aligned VFMs suitable for edge deployment across diverse conditions.

title: Hallucination as Trajectory Commitment: Causal Evidence for Asymmetric Attractor Dynamics in Transformer Generation
comment: 21 pages, 12 figures, 8 tables. Code and data: https://github.com/akarlaraytu/trajectory-commitment
summary: We present causal evidence that hallucination in autoregressive language models is an early trajectory commitment governed by asymmetric attractor dynamics. Using same-prompt bifurcation, in which we repeatedly sample identical inputs to observe spontaneous divergence, we isolate trajectory dynamics from prompt-level confounds. On Qwen2.5-1.5B across 61 prompts spanning six categories, 27 prompts (44.3%) bifurcate with factual and hallucinated trajectories diverging at the first generated token (KL = 0 at step 0, KL > 1.0 at step 1). Activation patching across 28 layers reveals a pronounced causal asymmetry: injecting a hallucinated activation into a correct trajectory corrupts output in 87.5% of trials (layer 20), while the reverse recovers only 33.3% (layer 24); both exceed the 10.4% baseline (p = 0.025) and 12.5% random-patch control. Window patching shows correction requires sustained multi-step intervention, whereas corruption needs only a single perturbation. Probing the prompt encoding itself, step-0 residual states predict per-prompt hallucination rate at Pearson r = 0.776 at layer 15 (p < 0.001 against a 1000-permutation null); unsupervised clustering identifies five regime-like groups (eta^2 = 0.55) whose saddle-adjacent cluster concentrates 12 of the 13 bifurcating false-premise prompts, indicating that the basin structure is organized around regime commitments fixed at prompt encoding. These findings characterize hallucination as a locally stable attractor basin: entry is probabilistic and rapid, exit demands coordinated intervention across layers and steps, and the relevant basins are selected by clusterable regimes already discernible at step 0.

AI.tldr: Hallucination in autoregressive LMs appears as a fast, probabilistic entry into an locally stable attractor basin, with exit requiring cross-layer coordination; evidence comes from same-prompt bifurcation, causal activation patching, and prompt-encoding regime analysis across Qwen2.5-1.5B prompts.
AI.motivation: To causally characterize the trajectory-level dynamics of hallucinations, separating prompt-level confounds from trajectory dynamics and identifying the structural factors (basins/regimes) that govern when and how hallucinations occur.
AI.method: Same-prompt bifurcation sampling on 61 prompts across six categories (Qwen2.5-1.5B); causal activation patching across 28 layers; window patching for intervention; probing step-0 residual states for prompt encoding; unsupervised clustering to identify regimes; statistical tests comparing perturbations against baselines and controls.
AI.result: Approximately 44.3% of prompts bifurcate into divergent factual vs. hallucinated trajectories at the first token; KL divergence is 0 at step 0 and >1.0 at step 1. Hallucination-causing activations injected into correct trajectories corrupt output in 87.5% of trials (layer 20); reverse patching recovers in 33.3% (layer 24); both exceed baseline 10.4% and random patch control 12.5% (p = 0.025). Sustained multi-step intervention is required for correction, while a single perturbation suffices to corrupt. Step-0 residuals predict per-prompt hallucination rate (r = 0.776 at layer 15); unsupervised clustering yields five regimes (eta^2 = 0.55); the saddle-adjacent cluster concentrates 12 of 13 bifurcating prompts (false premises). This suggests a basin structure organized by prompt-encoding regimes with fast entry and slower exit.
AI.conclusion: Hallucination behaves as a locally stable attractor basin in the model's trajectory space, with regime-encoded prompt features determining basin selection; exit from the basin requires coordinated, multi-layer interventions, indicating targeted mitigation strategies should focus on cross-layer dynamics and prompt-encoding regime shifts.

title: From Seeing to Simulating: Generative High-Fidelity Simulation with Digital Cousins for Generalizable Robot Learning and Evaluation
summary: Learning robust robot policies in real-world environments requires diverse data augmentation, yet scaling real-world data collection is costly due to the need for acquiring physical assets and reconfiguring environments. Therefore, augmenting real-world scenes into simulation has become a practical augmentation for efficient learning and evaluation. We present a generative framework that establishes a generative real-to-sim mapping from real-world panoramas to high-fidelity simulation scenes, and further synthesize diverse cousin scenes via semantic and geometric editing. Combined with high-quality physics engines and realistic assets, the generated scenes support interactive manipulation tasks. Additionally, we incorporate multi-room stitching to construct consistent large-scale environments for long-horizon navigation across complex layouts. Experiments demonstrate a strong sim-to-real correlation validating our platform's fidelity, and show that extensively scaling up data generation leads to significantly better generalization to unseen scene and object variations, demonstrating the effectiveness of Digital Cousins for generalizable robot learning and evaluation.

AI.tldr: Generative real-to-sim mapping with semantic/geometric editing to synthesize diverse high-fidelity simulation scenes from real panoramas, enabling large-scale, long-horizon environments for robust robot learning.
AI.motivation: Reducing the cost and effort of real-world data collection for robust robot policy learning by augmenting real-world data into realistic simulated scenes.
AI.method: A real-to-sim pipeline that converts real panoramas into high-fidelity simulation scenes, applies semantic and geometric editing to create diverse cousin scenes, leverages high-quality physics engines and assets, and uses multi-room stitching to assemble consistent large-scale environments for long-horizon tasks.
AI.result: Demonstrates strong sim-to-real correlation validating fidelity; scaling data generation improves generalization to unseen scene and object variations; supports generalizable robot learning and evaluation with Digital Cousins.
AI.conclusion: Digital Cousins provide a scalable, faithful platform for sim-to-real augmentation, enabling robust learning and evaluation across varied scenes and long-horizon tasks.

title: LLM Reasoning Is Latent, Not the Chain of Thought
summary: This position paper argues that large language model (LLM) reasoning should be studied as latent-state trajectory formation rather than as faithful surface chain-of-thought (CoT). This matters because claims about faithfulness, interpretability, reasoning benchmarks, and inference-time intervention all depend on what the field takes the primary object of reasoning to be. We ask what that object should be once three often-confounded factors are separated and formalize three competing hypotheses: H1, reasoning is primarily mediated by latent-state trajectories; H2, reasoning is primarily mediated by explicit surface CoT; and H0, most apparent reasoning gains are better explained by generic serial compute than by any privileged representational object. Reorganizing recent empirical, mechanistic, and survey work under this framework, and adding compute-audited worked exemplars that factorize surface traces, latent interventions, and matched budget expansions, we find that current evidence most strongly supports H1 as a default working hypothesis rather than as a task-independent verdict. We therefore make two recommendations: the field should treat latent-state dynamics as the default object of study for LLM reasoning, and it should evaluate reasoning with designs that explicitly disentangle surface traces, latent states, and serial compute.

AI.tldr: Latent-state dynamics should be the default object of study for LLM reasoning, not surface chain-of-thought (CoT); disentangle latent trajectories, surface traces, and serial compute to properly assess reasoning; current evidence most strongly supports latent-state mediation (H1).
AI.motivation: Clarify what constitutes the primary object of LLM reasoning, separating confounded factors (faithfulness, interpretability, benchmarks, interventions) to reconcile divergent claims.
AI.method: Reorganize recent empirical, mechanistic, and survey work under a latent-state framework; introduce compute-audited exemplars that factorize surface traces, latent interventions, and matched budget expansions; evaluate across designs that disentangle latent states from surface traces and serial compute.
AI.result: Review evidence across literature suggests that latent-state trajectories (H1) are the default working hypothesis rather than a task-independent verdict; CoT or generic serial compute explanations lack consistent primacy.
AI.conclusion: Treat latent-state dynamics as the default object of study for LLM reasoning and design experiments that explicitly disentangle surface traces, latent states, and serial compute in evaluation.

title: SIMMER: Cross-Modal Food Image--Recipe Retrieval via MLLM-Based Embedding
summary: Cross-modal retrieval between food images and recipe texts is an important task with applications in nutritional management, dietary logging, and cooking assistance. Existing methods predominantly rely on dual-encoder architectures with separate image and text encoders, requiring complex alignment strategies and task-specific network designs to bridge the semantic gap between modalities. In this work, we propose SIMMER (Single Integrated Multimodal Model for Embedding Recipes), which applies Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM)-based embedding models, specifically VLM2Vec, to this task, replacing the conventional dual-encoder paradigm with a single unified encoder that processes both food images and recipe texts. We design prompt templates tailored to the structured nature of recipes, which consist of a title, ingredients, and cooking instructions, enabling effective embedding generation by the MLLM. We further introduce a component-aware data augmentation strategy that trains the model on both complete and partial recipes, improving robustness to incomplete inputs. Experiments on the Recipe1M dataset demonstrate that SIMMER achieves state-of-the-art performance across both the 1k and 10k evaluation settings, substantially outperforming all prior methods. In particular, our best model improves the 1k image-to-recipe R@1 from 81.8\% to 87.5\% and the 10k image-to-recipe R@1 from 56.5\% to 65.5\% compared to the previous best method.

AI.tldr: SIMMER introduces a single unified multimodal model using MLLM-based embeddings (VLM2Vec) to bridge food images and recipe texts, achieving state-of-the-art cross-modal retrieval on Recipe1M (1k and 10k).
AI.motivation: Dual-encoder cross-modal retrieval for images and structured recipe text faces alignment complexity and may require task-specific designs; a single encoder could simplify architecture and improve robustness, especially to incomplete inputs.
AI.method: Use VLM2Vec as a unified encoder with prompt templates tailored to recipes (title, ingredients, instructions). Include component-aware data augmentation to train on complete and partial recipes. Train and evaluate on Recipe1M with 1k and 10k splits.
AI.result: SOTA performance; 1k image-to-recipe R@1 improved from 81.8% to 87.5%; 10k R@1 improved from 56.5% to 65.5%.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates effectiveness of a single MLLM-based embedding for cross-modal retrieval between food images and recipes; improves robustness to incomplete inputs and reduces architectural complexity compared to dual-encoder approaches.

title: Dispatch-Aware Ragged Attention for Pruned Vision Transformers
summary: Token pruning methods for Vision Transformers (ViTs) promise quadratic reductions in attention FLOPs by dropping uninformative patches. Yet when pruned sequences are executed with state-of-the-art variable-length attention APIs -- including FlashAttention-2's varlen and PyTorch's NestedTensor SDPA-the wall-clock attention latency doesn't scale accordingly. We trace this to a dispatch-overhead bottleneck: at the short, post-pruning sequence lengths typical of ViTs (<=197 tokens), actual matrix arithmetic completes in single-digit microseconds while the host-side dispatch path consumes 60-90 us. We present a lightweight, bidirectional Triton attention kernel whose dispatch floor is 40 us roughly 1.5x lower than FlashAttention-2 varlen-allowing pruning savings to become more visible in wall-clock time. Integrated into a complete pack-attend-unpack pipeline, our system achieves up to 2.24x end-to-end throughput over padded PyTorch SDPA consistently across four pruning algorithms (Threshold-L2, DynamicViT, EViT, ATS), scales across DeiT-T/S/B, and maintains bit-exact classification predictions with <0.007 max absolute logit difference.

AI.tldr: A lightweight bidirectional Triton attention kernel reduces dispatch overhead in token-pruned Vision Transformers, enabling pruning gains to translate into wall-clock speedups (up to 2.24x end-to-end) across multiple pruning methods and DeiT variants, with bit-exact predictions.
AI.motivation: Token pruning reduces attention compute in ViTs, but wall-clock latency is dominated by host-side dispatch for variable-length attention; this bottleneck obscures pruning benefits.
AI.method: Introduce a bidirectional Triton attention kernel with a dispatch floor of ~40 microseconds, integrated into a pack–attend–unpack pipeline and compatible with variable-length attention. Evaluated across four pruning algorithms (Threshold-L2, DynamicViT, EViT, ATS) on DeiT-T/S/B.
AI.result: End-to-end throughput up to 2.24x higher than padded PyTorch SDPA; consistent improvements across four pruning methods and DeiT variants; preserves bit-exact classification with max absolute logit difference < 0.007.
AI.conclusion: Dispatch overhead is a principal bottleneck in pruning-enabled ViT inference; a low-dispatch Triton kernel enables pruning savings to manifest in wall-clock time and generalizes across common pruning methods and DeiT models.

title: Limits of Lamarckian Evolution Under Pressure of Morphological Novelty
summary: Lamarckian inheritance has been shown to be a powerful accelerator in systems where the joint evolution of robot morphologies and controllers is enhanced with individual learning. Its defining advantage lies in the offspring inheriting controllers learned by their parents. The efficacy of this option, however, relies on morphological similarity between parent and offspring. In this study, we examine how Lamarckian inheritance performs when the search process is driven toward high morphological variance, potentially straining the requirement for parent-offspring similarity. Using a system of modular robots that can evolve and learn to solve a locomotion task, we compare Darwinian and Lamarckian evolution to determine how they respond to shifting from pure task-based selection to a multi-objective pressure that also rewards morphological novelty. Our results confirm that Lamarckian evolution outperforms Darwinian evolution when optimizing task-performance alone. However, introducing selection pressure for morphological diversity causes a substantial performance drop, which is much greater in the Lamarckian system. Further analyses show that promoting diversity reduces parent-offspring similarity, which in turn reduces the benefits of inheriting controllers learned by parents. These results reveal the limits of Lamarckian evolution by exposing a fundamental trade-off between inheritance-based exploitation and diversity-driven exploration.

AI.tldr: Lamarckian inheritance speeds task performance under morphology-consistency but falters when morphological diversity is rewarded, revealing a trade-off between inheritance-based exploitation and diversity-driven exploration.
AI.motivation: To evaluate how Lamarckian inheritance performs when the evolutionary search is driven toward high morphological variance and novelty, challenging parent-offspring similarity.
AI.method: Empirical comparison of Darwinian and Lamarckian evolution in modular robots solving a locomotion task, with a multi-objective pressure that rewards morphological novelty.
AI.result: Lamarckian evolution outperforms Darwinian optimization for task performance alone. Introducing diversity pressure drastically reduces performance, more so in the Lamarckian system. Diversity reduces parent-offspring similarity, diminishing benefits from inherited learned controllers.
AI.conclusion: Lamarckian inheritance has limits due to a fundamental trade-off: exploitation via inheritance vs. exploration via diversity; high morphological variance erodes the advantage of inheriting learned controllers.

title: Structured Abductive-Deductive-Inductive Reasoning for LLMs via Algebraic Invariants
comment: 10 pages + 3 pages references. Accepted as a poster at the ICLR 2026 Workshop for LLM Reasoning
summary: Large language models exhibit systematic limitations in structured logical reasoning: they conflate hypothesis generation with verification, cannot distinguish conjecture from validated knowledge, and allow weak reasoning steps to propagate unchecked through inference chains. We present a symbolic reasoning scaffold that operationalizes Peirce's tripartite inference -- abduction, deduction, and induction -- as an explicit protocol for LLM-assisted reasoning. The framework enforces logical consistency through five algebraic invariants (the Gamma Quintet), the strongest of which -- the Weakest Link bound -- ensures that no conclusion in a reasoning chain can exceed the reliability of its least-supported premise. This principle, independently grounded as weakest link resolution in possibilistic logic and empirically validated for chain-of-thought reasoning, prevents logical inconsistencies from accumulating across multi-step inference. We verify all invariants through a property-based testing suite of 100 properties and 16 fuzz tests over 10^5+ generated cases, providing a verified reference implementation of the invariants suitable as a foundation for future reasoning benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Proposes a Peirce-inspired symbolic reasoning scaffold for LLMs, enforcing five algebraic invariants (the Gamma Quintet) with a Weakest Link bound to prevent propagation of weak reasoning; validated via property-based testing and fuzzing; provides a verified reference implementation for future benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Address systematic limitations in structured logical reasoning by LLMs (confounding hypothesis generation with verification, differentiating conjecture from validated knowledge, and unchecked inference chains) and establish an explicit protocol for reliable LLM-assisted reasoning.
AI.method: Introduce a symbolic reasoning scaffold grounded in Peirce's abduction, deduction, and induction. Formalize five invariants (Gamma Quintet) including the Weakest Link bound, connected to possibilistic logic. Validate empirically through a property-based testing suite (100 properties) and 16 fuzz tests over 1e5+ generated cases, yielding a verified reference implementation.
AI.result: Invariants verified through the testing suite; a verified reference implementation is provided, suitable as a foundation for future reasoning benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The Weakest Link bound ensures that no conclusion exceeds the reliability of its least-supported premise, preventing accumulation of inconsistencies across multi-step inference; the framework offers a principled, test-backed foundation for reliable reasoning benchmarks and LLM-assisted reasoning.

title: Causal Bootstrapped Alignment for Unsupervised Video-Based Visible-Infrared Person Re-Identification
summary: VVI-ReID is a critical technique for all-day surveillance, where temporal information provides additional cues beyond static images. However, existing approaches rely heavily on fully supervised learning with expensive cross-modality annotations, limiting scalability. To address this issue, we investigate Unsupervised Learning for VVI-ReID (USL-VVI-ReID), which learns identity-discriminative representations directly from unlabeled video tracklets. Directly extending image-based USL-VI-ReID methods to this setting with generic pretrained encoders leads to suboptimal performance. Such encoders suffer from weak identity discrimination and strong modality bias, resulting in severe intra-modality identity confusion and pronounced clustering granularity imbalance between visible and infrared modalities. These issues jointly degrade pseudo-label reliability and hinder effective cross-modality alignment. To address these challenges, we propose a Causal Bootstrapped Alignment (CBA) framework that explicitly exploits inherent video priors. First, we introduce Causal Intervention Warm-up (CIW), which performs sequence-level causal interventions by leveraging temporal identity consistency and cross-modality identity consistency to suppress modality- and motion-induced spurious correlations while preserving identity-relevant semantics, yielding cleaner representations for unsupervised clustering. Second, we propose Prototype-Guided Uncertainty Refinement (PGUR), which employs a coarse-to-fine alignment strategy to resolve cross-modality granularity mismatch, reorganizing under-clustered infrared representations under the guidance of reliable visible prototypes with uncertainty-aware supervision. Extensive experiments on the HITSZ-VCM and BUPTCampus benchmarks demonstrate that CBA significantly outperforms existing USL-VI-ReID methods when extended to the USL-VVI-ReID setting.

AI.tldr: Presents Causal Bootstrapped Alignment (CBA) for unsupervised VVI-ReID, introducing CIW and PGUR to counteract modality bias and granularity mismatch in unlabeled video tracklets; achieves state-of-the-art results on HITSZ-VCM and BUPTCampus.
AI.motivation: Unsupervised video-to-video ReID (VVI-ReID) lacks scalable labels and suffers from weak identity discrimination and strong modality bias when naively adapting image-based USL to video data; leveraging video priors could improve cross-modality alignment and pseudo-label quality.
AI.method: (CIW) sequence-level causal interventions using temporal identity consistency and cross-modality consistency to remove spurious modality- and motion-induced correlations; (PGUR) coarse-to-fine alignment with uncertainty-aware supervision, reorganizing under-clustered infrared prototypes guided by reliable visible prototypes.
AI.result: Significant performance gains over existing USL-VI-ReID methods when extended to USL-VVI-ReID on HITSZ-VCM and BUPTCampus, demonstrating improved intra-modality discrimination and cross-modality alignment.
AI.conclusion: CBA effectively mitigates modality bias and granularity mismatch in unsupervised VVI-ReID by exploiting video priors, yielding cleaner representations and more reliable clustering for cross-modality ReID.

title: Environment-Adaptive Solid-State LiDAR-Inertial Odometry
summary: Solid-state LiDAR-inertial SLAM has attracted significant attention due to its advantages in speed and robustness. However, achieving accurate mapping in extreme environments remains challenging due to severe geometric degeneracy and unreliable observations, which often lead to ill-conditioned optimization and map inconsistencies. To address these challenges, we propose an environment-adaptive solid-state LiDAR-inertial odometry that integrates local normal-vector constraints with degeneracy-aware map maintenance to enhance localization accuracy. Specifically, we introduce local normal-vector constraints to improve the stability of state estimation, effectively suppressing localization drift in degenerate scenarios. Furthermore, we design a degeneration-guided map update strategy to improve map precision. Benefiting from the refined map representation, localization accuracy is further enhanced in subsequent estimation. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves superior mapping accuracy and robustness in extreme and perceptually degraded environments, with an average RMSE reduction of up to 12.8% compared to the baseline method.

AI.tldr: Environment-adaptive solid-state LiDAR-inertial odometry improves localization and mapping in degenerate/extreme environments by adding local normal-vector constraints and a degeneration-guided map update strategy, achieving up to 12.8% RMSE reduction over a baseline.
AI.motivation: Extreme environments cause geometric degeneracy and unreliable observations, leading to ill-conditioned optimization and map inconsistencies. A robust, adaptive framework is needed to maintain accurate localization and maps.
AI.method: Incorporates local normal-vector constraints to stabilize state estimation and suppress drift in degenerate scenarios; introduces a degeneration-guided map update strategy to improve map precision; integrates with solid-state LiDAR-inertial odometry to enhance subsequent estimation.
AI.result: Experiments show superior mapping accuracy and robustness in extreme and perceptually degraded environments, with an average RMSE reduction of up to 12.8% compared to the baseline method.
AI.conclusion: A refined map representation coupled with degeneracy-aware maintenance enhances localization accuracy in subsequent estimation and robust performance in challenging environments.

title: KWBench: Measuring Unprompted Problem Recognition in Knowledge Work
summary:
We introduce the first version of KWBench (Knowledge Work Bench), a benchmark for unprompted problem recognition in large language models: can an LLM identify a professional scenario before attempting to solve it. Existing frontier benchmarks have saturated, and most knowledge-work evaluations to date reduce to extraction or task completion against a specification. KWBench targets the step before that: recognizing the governing structure of the situation from raw inputs alone.
  The benchmark contains 223 tasks sourced from practitioners across acquisitions, contract negotiations, clinical pharmacy, organizational politics, fraud analysis, and incentive design. Each task encodes a formal game-theoretic pattern (principal-agent conflict, signaling, mechanism design failure, strategic omission, coalitional dynamics, strategic interdependence) and carries structured ground truth recording the expert reading of the situation and the anticipated failure modes. Models receive raw data and a task prompt with no indication of problem type. Scoring is a three-tier rubric gated by a mandatory conjunctive check. Mandatory criteria encode the predicted wrong paths.
  We evaluate 16 models. The best model passes on 27.9% of tasks. The top two models agree on only 31.7% of their passes. Among the top 8, 44 tasks are solved by exactly one model; routing across the top 8 covers 50.7% of the benchmark, nearly double the best single model. Conditional on passing, quality scores converge (approx 83% across models); unconditional scores do not. Same models articulate the relevant game-theoretic concept correctly when asked, then fail to apply it unprompted. We release KWBench to shift how frontier models are evaluated on knowledge work, scoring them on whether they recognize the right problem from the situation alone, not only on how well they execute once the problem has been framed for them.

AI.tldr: KWBench is a benchmark for unprompted problem recognition in LLMs, testing whether models can identify the governing game-theoretic structure from raw data before solving. It includes 223 tasks from multiple knowledge-work domains, each encoded with a formal game-theoretic pattern and structured ground truth. 16 models were evaluated; the best passes 27.9% of tasks, with limited cross-model agreement (top two agree on 31.7% of passes). Among the top-8 models, 44 tasks are solved by exactly one model, and routing across the top-8 covers 50.7% of tasks, indicating highly dispersed success. Conditional on passing, quality converges (~83%); unconditional scores remain variable. Models can name the relevant concept when prompted but fail to apply it unprompted. KWBench aims to shift frontier evaluation toward recognizing the right problem from the situation, not merely solving once framed.
AI.motivation: Current frontier benchmarks focus on performance after problem framing (extraction, task completion). There is a need to evaluate whether LLMs can recognize the governing problem structure from raw inputs in knowledge-work contexts, which is a prerequisite for reliable, generalized performance in real-world settings.
AI.method: A dataset of 223 tasks sourced from practitioners across acquisitions, contract negotiations, clinical pharmacy, organizational politics, fraud analysis, and incentive design. Each task encodes a formal game-theoretic pattern (e.g., principal-agent conflict, signaling, mechanism-design failure, strategic omission, coalitional dynamics, strategic interdependence) and includes structured ground truth about expert interpretation and anticipated failure modes. Tasks are presented with raw data and a prompt, with no hints about problem type. Evaluation uses a three-tier rubric gated by a mandatory conjunctive check that enforces predicted wrong-paths.
AI.result: Evaluated 16 models. Best model passes 27.9% of tasks. Top two models agree on only 31.7% of their passes. Among the top 8, 44 tasks are solved by exactly one model; routing across the top 8 covers 50.7% of the benchmark. Conditional on passing, quality scores converge to about 83% across models; unconditional scores do not. Models can articulate the relevant game-theoretic concept when asked, but fail to apply it unprompted.
AI.conclusion: KWBench reframes frontier-model evaluation toward problem recognition from raw situations, not just execution after problem framing, and provides a benchmark to measure whether models identify the right problem from a given situation.

title: SPLIT: Self-supervised Partitioning for Learned Inversion in Nonlinear Tomography
summary: Machine learning has achieved impressive performance in tomographic reconstruction, but supervised training requires paired measurements and ground-truth images that are often unavailable. This has motivated self-supervised approaches, which have primarily addressed denoising and, more recently, linear inverse problems. We address nonlinear inverse problems and introduce SPLIT (Self-supervised Partitioning for Learned Inversion in Nonlinear Tomography), a self-supervised machine-learning framework for reconstructing images from nonlinear, incomplete, and noisy projection data without any samples of ground-truth images. SPLIT enforces cross-partition consistency and measurement-domain fidelity while exploiting complementary information across multiple partitions. Our main theoretical result shows that, under mild conditions, the proposed self-supervised objective is equivalent to its supervised counterpart in expectation. We regularize training with an automatic stopping rule that halts optimization when a no-reference image-quality surrogate saturates. As a concrete application, we derive SPLIT variants for multispectral computed tomography. Experiments on sparse-view acquisitions demonstrate high reconstruction quality and robustness to noise, surpassing classical iterative reconstruction and recent self-supervised baselines.

AI.tldr: Self-supervised SPLIT reconstructs nonlinear tomographic images without ground truth, achieving expected-supervised performance with cross-partition consistency and automatic stopping; effective for multispectral CT with sparse views.
AI.motivation: Lack of paired measurements/ground-truth for nonlinear inverse problems; extend self-supervision beyond denoising and linear reconstruction.
AI.method: Partition measurements into multiple views, enforce cross-partition consistency and measurement-domain fidelity, leverage complementary information across partitions; derive SPLIT variants for multispectral CT; automatic stopping rule using no-reference image-quality surrogate.
AI.result: Under mild conditions, the self-supervised objective is equivalent in expectation to the supervised loss; empirically outperforms classical iterative methods and recent self-supervised baselines on sparse-view data and exhibits robustness to noise.
AI.conclusion: SPLIT provides a principled self-supervised framework for nonlinear tomography with automatic stopping, applicable to nonlinear inverse problems and multispectral imaging; demonstrates strong performance under sparse measurements and noisy conditions.

title: PRL-Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark Evaluating LLMs' Capabilities in Frontier Physics Research
summary: The paradigm of agentic science requires AI systems to conduct robust reasoning and engage in long-horizon, autonomous exploration. However, current scientific benchmarks remain confined to domain knowledge comprehension and complex reasoning, failing to evaluate the exploratory nature and procedural complexity of real-world research. In this work, we present research-oriented evaluations in theoretical and computational physics, a natural testbed with comprehensive domain knowledge, complex reasoning, and verifiable end-to-end workflows without reliance on experiments. Here we introduce PRL-Bench (Physics Research by LLMs), a benchmark designed to systematically map the capability boundaries of LLMs in executing end-to-end physics research. Constructed from 100 curated papers from the latest issues of Physical Review Letters since August 2025 and validated by domain experts, PRL-Bench covers five major theory- and computation-intensive subfields of modern physics: astrophysics, condensed matter physics, high-energy physics, quantum information, and statistical physics. Each task in the benchmark is designed to replicate the core properties of authentic scientific research, including exploration-oriented formulation, long-horizon workflows, and objective verifiability, thereby reconstructing the essential reasoning processes and research workflows of real physics research. Evaluation across frontier models shows that performance remains limited, with the best overall score below 50, revealing a pronounced gap between current LLM capabilities and the demands of real scientific research. PRL-Bench serves a reliable testbed for accessing next generation AI scientists advancing AI systems toward autonomous scientific discovery.

AI.tldr: PRL-Bench is a physics research benchmark for LLMs, designed to test end-to-end theoretical/computational physics research workflows across five subfields; initial results show best model scores below 50, indicating substantial gap to autonomous scientific discovery.
AI.motivation: The field lacks benchmarks that evaluate exploratory, long-horizon, and verifiably workflow-driven reasoning in scientific research; need to quantify LLMs' capability to conduct authentic physics research beyond domain knowledge.
AI.method: Constructed from 100 curated Physical Review Letters papers (since Aug 2025), expert-validated; spans astrophysics, condensed matter, high-energy, quantum information, statistical physics; tasks simulate core research properties: exploration, long-horizon workflows, objective verifiability; evaluation uses frontier LLMs; no reliance on laboratory experiments.
AI.result: Models show limited performance; best overall score < 50; significant gap between current LLM capabilities and requirements of real physics research; PRL-Bench can serve as a testbed for next-generation AI scientists.
AI.conclusion: PRL-Bench provides a reliable framework for assessing AI readiness for autonomous scientific discovery in physics; highlights current limitations and guides future improvements in reasoning, planning, tool use, and evaluation strategies.

title: DTEA: A Dual-Topology Elastic Actuator Enabling Real-Time Switching Between Series and Parallel Compliance
summary: Series and parallel elastic actuators offer complementary but mutually exclusive advantages, yet no existing actuator enables real-time transition between these topologies during operation. This paper presents a novel actuator design called the Dual-Topology Elastic Actuator (DTEA), which enables dynamic switching between SEA and PEA topologies during operation. A proof-of-concept prototype of the DTEA is developed to demonstrate the feasibility of the topology-switching mechanism. Experiments are conducted to evaluate the robustness and timing of the switching mechanism under operational conditions. The actuator successfully performed 324 topology-switching cycles under load without damage, demonstrating the robustness of the mechanism. The measured switching time between SEA and PEA modes is under 33.33 ms. Additional experiments are conducted to characterize the static stiffness and disturbance rejection performance in both SEA and PEA modes. Static stiffness tests show that the PEA mode is 1.53x stiffer than the SEA mode, with KSEA = 5.57 +/- 0.02 Nm/rad and KPEA = 8.54 +/- 0.02 Nm/rad. Disturbance rejection experiments show that the mean peak deflection in SEA mode is 2.26x larger than in PEA mode (5.2 deg vs. 2.3 deg), while the mean settling time is 3.45x longer (1380 ms vs. 400 ms). The observed behaviors are consistent with the known characteristics of conventional SEA and PEA actuators, validating the functionality of both modes in the DTEA actuator.

AI.tldr: A dual-topology elastic actuator (DTEA) can switch in real-time between SEA and PEA. A prototype demonstrates 324 topology-switching cycles under load with switching time < 33.33 ms; stiffness/ disturbance rejection differ between modes (SEA: lower stiffness, larger deflections, slower settling).
AI.motivation: To combine the advantages of series elastic actuators (SEA) and parallel elastic actuators (PEA) by enabling real-time topology switching, achieving robustness and task-adaptability.
AI.method: Design and fabricate a DTEA prototype with a topology-switching mechanism; perform 324 switching cycles under load; measure switching time; conduct static stiffness tests and disturbance rejection tests in both SEA and PEA modes.
AI.result: The actuator maintained integrity for 324 cycles under load; switching time < 33.33 ms; K_SEA = 5.57 ± 0.02 Nm/rad; K_PEA = 8.54 ± 0.02 Nm/rad; mean peak deflection SEA = 5.2° vs PEA = 2.3°; mean settling time SEA = 1380 ms vs PEA = 400 ms.
AI.conclusion: DTEA successfully demonstrates real-time topology switching between SEA and PEA with performance that aligns with conventional SEA/PEA characteristics, validating robustness and feasibility of the switching mechanism.

title: Stein Variational Black-Box Combinatorial Optimization
summary: Combinatorial black-box optimization in high-dimensional settings demands a careful trade-off between exploiting promising regions of the search space and preserving sufficient exploration to identify multiple optima. Although Estimation-of-Distribution Algorithms (EDAs) provide a powerful model-based framework, they often concentrate on a single region of interest, which may result in premature convergence when facing complex or multimodal objective landscapes. In this work, we incorporate the Stein operator to introduce a repulsive mechanism among particles in the parameter space, thereby encouraging the population to disperse and jointly explore several modes of the fitness landscape. Empirical evaluations across diverse benchmark problems show that the proposed method achieves performance competitive with, and in several cases superior to, leading state-of-the-art approaches, particularly on large-scale instances. These findings highlight the potential of Stein variational gradient descent as a promising direction for addressing large, computationally expensive, discrete black-box optimization problems.

AI.tldr: Introduces Stein operator-based repulsion among particles to diversify population in EDAs, enabling multi-modal exploration in high-dimensional combinatorial black-box optimization; achieves competitive or superior performance on large-scale problems.
AI.motivation: In high-dimensional, multimodal objective landscapes, standard EDAs risk premature convergence by concentrating on a single region. A repulsive mechanism can promote exploration of multiple optima and improve robustness on difficult discrete tasks.
AI.method: Incorporates Stein variational gradient descent with a repulsion term derived from the Stein operator to enforce dispersion among particles in the EDA's parameter space, encouraging simultaneous exploration of multiple modes in the fitness landscape; applied to discrete, large-scale black-box optimization.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations across diverse benchmarks show the proposed method is competitive with state-of-the-art approaches, and in several cases superior, particularly on large-scale instances.
AI.conclusion: Stein variational gradient descent offers a promising framework for addressing large, computationally expensive discrete black-box optimization by enabling effective multi-modal exploration and mitigating premature convergence.

title: Towards Realistic Open-Vocabulary Remote Sensing Segmentation: Benchmark and Baseline
summary: Open-vocabulary remote sensing image segmentation (OVRSIS) remains underexplored due to fragmented datasets, limited training diversity, and the lack of evaluation benchmarks that reflect realistic geospatial application demands. Our previous \textit{OVRSISBenchV1} established an initial cross-dataset evaluation protocol, but its limited scope is insufficient for assessing realistic open-world generalization. To address this issue, we propose \textit{OVRSISBenchV2}, a large-scale and application-oriented benchmark for OVRSIS. We first construct \textbf{OVRSIS95K}, a balanced dataset of about 95K image--mask pairs covering 35 common semantic categories across diverse remote sensing scenes. Built upon OVRSIS95K and 10 downstream datasets, OVRSISBenchV2 contains 170K images and 128 categories, substantially expanding scene diversity, semantic coverage, and evaluation difficulty. Beyond standard open-vocabulary segmentation, it further includes downstream protocols for building extraction, road extraction, and flood detection, thereby better reflecting realistic geospatial application demands and complex deployment scenarios. We also propose \textbf{Pi-Seg}, a baseline for OVRSIS. Pi-Seg improves transferability through a \textbf{positive-incentive noise} mechanism, where learnable and semantically guided perturbations broaden the visual-text feature space during training. Extensive experiments on OVRSISBenchV1, OVRSISBenchV2, and downstream tasks show that Pi-Seg delivers strong and consistent results, particularly on the more challenging OVRSISBenchV2 benchmark. Our results highlight both the importance of realistic benchmark design and the effectiveness of perturbation-based transfer for OVRSIS. The code and datasets are available at \href{https://github.com/LiBingyu01/RSKT-Seg/tree/Pi-Seg}{LiBingyu01/RSKT-Seg/tree/Pi-Seg}.

AI.tldr: Large-scale, application-oriented OVRSIS benchmark (OVRSISBenchV2) built on OVRSIS95K (95k image-mask pairs, 35 categories) and 10 downstream datasets, totaling 170k images and 128 categories; introduces Pi-Seg with positive-incentive noise to improve cross-domain transfer in open-vocabulary RS segmentation; shows strong performance, especially on the new benchmark, and emphasizes realistic benchmark design and perturbation-based transfer.
AI.motivation: OVRSISBenchV1 had limited scope and insufficient evaluation of open-world generalization in remote sensing segmentation; there is a need for large-scale, diverse, and application-driven benchmarks and protocols.
AI.method: Construct OVRSIS95K (~95k image-mask pairs, 35 categories) covering diverse RS scenes. Build OVRSISBenchV2 by integrating OVRSIS95K with 10 downstream datasets to reach ~170k images and 128 categories; extend evaluation with downstream tasks for building extraction, road extraction, flood detection. Propose Pi-Seg baseline using positive-incentive noise: learnable, semantically guided perturbations expand the visual-text feature space during training to improve transferability.
AI.result: Pi-Seg achieves strong and consistent results, particularly on the more challenging OVRSISBenchV2 benchmark, across OVRSISBenchV1, OVRSISBenchV2, and downstream tasks.
AI.conclusion: Realistic benchmark design and perturbation-based transfer are crucial for open-vocabulary RS segmentation; Pi-Seg effectively enhances transferability and the benchmark ensemble supports robust evaluation. Code/datasets released.

title: Beyond Single-Model Optimization: Preserving Plasticity in Continual Reinforcement Learning
summary: Continual reinforcement learning must balance retention with adaptation, yet many methods still rely on \emph{single-model preservation}, committing to one evolving policy as the main reusable solution across tasks. Even when a previously successful policy is retained, it may no longer provide a reliable starting point for rapid adaptation after interference, reflecting a form of \emph{loss of plasticity} that single-policy preservation cannot address. Inspired by quality-diversity methods, we introduce \textsc{TeLAPA} (Transfer-Enabled Latent-Aligned Policy Archives), a continual RL framework that organizes behaviorally diverse policy neighborhoods into per-task archives and maintains a shared latent space so that archived policies remain comparable and reusable under non-stationary drift. This perspective shifts continual RL from retaining isolated solutions to maintaining \emph{skill-aligned neighborhoods} with competent and behaviorally related policies that support future relearning. In our MiniGrid CL setting, \textsc{TeLAPA} learns more tasks successfully, recovers competence faster on revisited tasks after interference, and retains higher performance across a sequence of tasks. Our analyses show that source-optimal policies are often not transfer-optimal, even within a local competent neighborhood, and that effective reuse depends on retaining and selecting among multiple nearby alternatives rather than collapsing them to one representative. Together, these results reframe continual RL around reusable and competent policy neighborhoods, providing a route beyond single-model preservation toward more plastic lifelong agents.

AI.tldr: TeLAPA introduces a transfer-enabled latent-aligned policy archive for continual RL, shifting from single-model preservation to skill-aligned, behaviorally diverse policy neighborhoods stored per-task in a shared latent space. This enables better reuse, plasticity, and relearning across non-stationary tasks, demonstrated in MiniGrid CL with improved task acquisition, faster recovery after interference, and higher retention.
AI.motivation: Standard continual RL often relies on preserving a single evolving policy, which can lead to loss of plasticity when rapid adaptation or relearning is needed after interference. There is a need for maintaining diverse, reusable solutions that remain comparable under non-stationary drift.
AI.method: TeLAPA builds per-task archives of diverse policies (behavioral neighborhoods) and maintains a shared latent space to keep archived policies comparable and reusable despite drift. This quality-diversity-inspired approach emphasizes retaining multiple near-optimal alternatives rather than collapsing to a single representative.
AI.result: In MiniGrid continual learning, TeLAPA enables learning more tasks, faster recovery after interference on revisited tasks, and higher performance across a sequence of tasks. Analyses show that source-optimal policies are often not transfer-optimal within a local neighborhood and that effective reuse depends on preserving and selecting among multiple nearby alternatives rather than a single policy.
AI.conclusion: Continual RL should focus on reusable, competent policy neighborhoods and maintain multiple viable alternatives to support continual adaptation, offering a path beyond single-model preservation toward more plastic lifelong agents.

title: Robust Fleet Sizing for Multi-UAV Inspection Missions under Synchronized Replacement Demand
summary:
Multi-UAV inspection missions require spare drones to replace active drones during recharging cycles. Existing fleet-sizing approaches often assume steady-state operating conditions that do not apply to finite-horizon missions, or they treat replacement requests as statistically independent events. The latter provides per-request blocking guarantees that fail to translate to mission-level reliability when demands cluster. This paper identifies a structural failure mode where efficient routing assigns similar workloads to each UAV, leading to synchronized battery depletion and replacement bursts that exhaust the spare pool even when average capacity is sufficient.
  We derive a closed-form sufficient fleet-sizing rule, k = m(ceil(R) + 1), where m is the number of active UAVs and R is the recovery-to-active time ratio. This additive buffer of m spares absorbs worst-case synchronized demand at recovery-cycle boundaries and ensures mission-level reliability even when all UAVs deplete simultaneously. Monte Carlo validation across five scenarios (m in [2, 10], R in [0.87, 3.39], 1000 trials each) shows that Erlang-B sizing with a per-request blocking target epsilon = 0.01 drops to 69.9% mission success at R = 3.39, with 95% of spare exhaustion events concentrated in the top-decile 5-minute demand windows. In contrast, the proposed rule maintains 99.8% success (Wilson 95% lower bound 99.3%) across all tested conditions, including wind variability up to CV = 0.30, while requiring only four additional drones in the most demanding scenario.

AI.tldr: Identifies a structural failure mode in multi-UAV fleet sizing where contention and synchronized battery depletion cause spare exhaustion, and provides a simple closed-form fleet-sizing rule k = m(ceil(R) + 1) that guarantees mission-level reliability by adding m spares; validated against Erlang-B with simulations across multiple scenarios showing superior robustness, including wind variability.
AI.motivation: Address the gap where finite-horizon missions and synchronized demand bursts undermine per-request blocking guarantees, leading to mission-level unreliability despite adequate average spare capacity.
AI.method: Derive a closed-form sufficient fleet-sizing rule by accounting for worst-case synchronized depletion at recovery-cycle boundaries; compare against Erlang-B sizing; perform Monte Carlo validation over varying active UAV counts (m), recovery-to-active time ratio (R), and wind variability; analyze spare exhaustion timing.
AI.result: The additive spare rule k = m(ceil(R) + 1) maintains ~99.8% mission success across tested conditions, including wind CV up to 0.30, while Erlang-B with per-request blocking falls to 69.9% at R=3.39; the worst-case burden concentrates in a top-decile 5-minute window; the rule requires only four additional drones in the most demanding case.
AI.conclusion: A simple, scalable spare provisioning rule mitigates structural failure modes from synchronized workloads, ensuring mission-level reliability in finite-horizon UAV inspections; empirically validated and outperforms traditional per-request blocking approaches.

title: Discover and Prove: An Open-source Agentic Framework for Hard Mode Automated Theorem Proving in Lean 4
summary: Most ATP benchmarks embed the final answer within the formal statement -- a convention we call "Easy Mode" -- a design that simplifies the task relative to what human competitors face and may lead to optimistic estimates of model capability. We call the stricter, more realistic setting "Hard Mode": the system must independently discover the answer before constructing a formal proof. To enable Hard Mode research, we make two contributions. First, we release MiniF2F-Hard and FIMO-Hard, expert-reannotated Hard Mode variants of two widely-used ATP benchmarks. Second, we introduce Discover And Prove (DAP), an agentic framework that uses LLM natural-language reasoning with explicit self-reflection to discover answers, then rewrites Hard Mode statements into Easy Mode ones for existing ATP provers. DAP sets the state of the art: on CombiBench it raises solved problems from 7 (previous SOTA, Pass@16) to 10; on PutnamBench it is the first system to formally prove 36 theorems in Hard Mode -- while simultaneously revealing that state-of-the-art LLMs exceed 80% answer accuracy on the same problems where formal provers manage under 10%, exposing a substantial gap that Hard Mode benchmarks are uniquely suited to measure.

AI.tldr: Hard Mode benchmarks for automated theorem proving (ATP) reveal a more faithful measure of capability than Easy Mode; the paper releases Hard Mode variants (MiniF2F-Hard, FIMO-Hard) and introduces Discover And Prove (DAP), an LLM-based agentic framework that discovers answers and rewrites Hard Mode problems into Easy Mode for provers. DAP improves state-of-the-art on CombiBench and PutnamBench and highlights a substantial gap between LLM-based reasoning and formal provers under Hard Mode.
AI.motivation: Standard ATP benchmarks embed the final answer in the problem statement (Easy Mode), yielding optimistic performance estimates. To assess true autonomous problem solving and proof construction, Hard Mode requires the system to discover the solution before proving it. The authors provide Hard Mode benchmarks and an agentic framework to advance this setting.
AI.method: 1) Release expert-annotated Hard Mode variants MiniF2F-Hard and FIMO-Hard. 2) Propose Discover And Prove (DAP): use LLMs for natural-language reasoning with explicit self-reflection to discover answers, then rewrite Hard Mode statements into Easy Mode equivalents for existing ATP provers.
AI.result: On CombiBench, solved problems increased from 7 (Pass@16) to 10 with DAP. On PutnamBench, DAP is the first system to formally prove 36 theorems in Hard Mode. Moreover, state-of-the-art LLMs exceed 80% answer accuracy on the same problems where formal provers perform under 10%, highlighting a large gap and showing Hard Mode benchmarks uniquely measure capability.
AI.conclusion: Hard Mode benchmarks are a more realistic and stringent evaluation of ATP systems. The DAP framework demonstrates how agentic LLM-based reasoning can both solve and prove theorems in Hard Mode and exposes a substantial capability gap between LLMs and formal provers on such tasks, underscoring the need for Hard Mode benchmarks.

title: From Zero to Detail: A Progressive Spectral Decoupling Paradigm for UHD Image Restoration with New Benchmark
summary: Ultra-high-definition (UHD) image restoration poses unique challenges due to the high spatial resolution, diverse content, and fine-grained structures present in UHD images. To address these issues, we introduce a progressive spectral decomposition for the restoration process, decomposing it into three stages: zero-frequency \textbf{enhancement}, low-frequency \textbf{restoration}, and high-frequency \textbf{refinement}. Based on this formulation, we propose a novel framework, \textbf{ERR}, which integrates three cooperative sub-networks: the zero-frequency enhancer (ZFE), the low-frequency restorer (LFR), and the high-frequency refiner (HFR). The ZFE incorporates global priors to learn holistic mappings, the LFR reconstructs the main content by focusing on coarse-scale information, and the HFR adopts our proposed frequency-windowed Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (FW-KAN) to recover fine textures and intricate details for high-fidelity restoration. To further advance research in UHD image restoration, we also construct a large-scale, high-quality benchmark dataset, \textbf{LSUHDIR}, comprising 82{,}126 UHD images with diverse scenes and rich content. Our proposed methods demonstrate superior performance across a range of UHD image restoration tasks, and extensive ablation studies confirm the contribution and necessity of each module. Project page: https://github.com/NJU-PCALab/ERR.
