Meanwhile, a Structure-Preserving Dual Distillation (SPDD) is proposed to decompose localization knowledge into coarse scene layout and fine pose precision, and distills them separately to alleviate both types of forgetting during continual adaptation. As a new manipulation scene arrives, SARS provides geometrically representative replay samples from all prior scenes, and SPDD applies structured distillation on these samples to retain previously learned knowledge. After training on the new scene, SARS incorporates selected samples from the new scene into the replay buffer for future rehearsal, allowing the model to continuously accumulate multi-scene calibration capability. Experiments on multiple public datasets show significant anti scene forgetting performance, maintaining accuracy on past scenes while preserving adaptation to new scenes, confirming the effectiveness of the framework.

AI.tldr: A continual hand-eye calibration framework that mitigates catastrophic forgetting in open-world scene adaptation via Spatial-Aware Replay Strategy (SARS) and Structure-Preserving Dual Distillation (SPDD). SARS builds a geometrically uniform replay buffer to cover scene pose space and selects informative viewpoints; SPDD distills localization knowledge into coarse scene layout and fine pose precision separately. As new scenes arrive, SARS replays samples from prior scenes; after training, new-scene samples are added to the buffer for future rehearsal. Experiments show strong anti-forgetting while maintaining adaptation to new scenes.
AI.motivation: Deep learning-based hand-eye calibration models exhibit catastrophic forgetting when adapting to unseen open-world scenes; simple rehearsal-based continual learning fails to prevent forgetting across scene changes. There is a need for geometry-aware replay and structure-preserving knowledge transfer to maintain multi-scene calibration capability.
AI.method: Propose Spatial-Aware Replay Strategy (SARS) to construct a geometrically uniform and informative replay buffer that ensures coverage of each scene's pose space by replacing redundant frames with maximally informative viewpoints. Propose Structure-Preserving Dual Distillation (SPDD) to split localization knowledge into coarse scene layout and fine pose precision and distill them separately to reduce forgetting. Upon arrival of a new scene, SARS provides replay samples from all prior scenes, SPDD distills them, and after training, incorporates selected samples from the new scene into the buffer for future rehearsal.
AI.result: Experiments on multiple public datasets demonstrate significant reduction in forgetting across previously learned scenes while preserving the ability to adapt to new scenes.
AI.conclusion: The framework effectively mitigates forgetting in continual hand-eye calibration for open-world manipulation, enabling robust multi-scene adaptation through geometry-aware replay and structured knowledge distillation.

title: MARCH: Multi-Agent Radiology Clinical Hierarchy for CT Report Generation
summary: Automated 3D radiology report generation often suffers from clinical hallucinations and a lack of the iterative verification found in human practice. While recent Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have advanced the field, they typically operate as monolithic "black-box" systems without the collaborative oversight characteristic of clinical workflows. To address these challenges, we propose MARCH (Multi-Agent Radiology Clinical Hierarchy), a multi-agent framework that emulates the professional hierarchy of radiology departments and assigns specialized roles to distinct agents. MARCH utilizes a Resident Agent for initial drafting with multi-scale CT feature extraction, multiple Fellow Agents for retrieval-augmented revision, and an Attending Agent that orchestrates an iterative, stance-based consensus discourse to resolve diagnostic discrepancies. On the RadGenome-ChestCT dataset, MARCH significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in both clinical fidelity and linguistic accuracy. Our work demonstrates that modeling human-like organizational structures enhances the reliability of AI in high-stakes medical domains.

AI.tldr: A hierarchical multi-agent framework (MARCH) for automated 3D radiology report generation that mimics radiology department roles to improve clinical fidelity and linguistic accuracy, outperforming baselines on RadGenome-ChestCT.
AI.motivation: Clinical radiology report generation suffers from hallucinations and lacks iterative human verification. Monolithic VLMs fail to capture collaborative oversight present in clinical workflows, risking reliability in high-stakes settings.
AI.method: MARCH uses a Resident Agent for initial drafting with multi-scale CT feature extraction, multiple Fellow Agents for retrieval-augmented revision, and an Attending Agent to orchestrate iterative, stance-based consensus discourse to resolve diagnostic discrepancies.
AI.result: On the RadGenome-ChestCT dataset, MARCH significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in both clinical fidelity and linguistic accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Modeling human-like organizational structures can enhance the reliability of AI in high-stakes medical domains.

title: Natural gradient descent with momentum
summary: We consider the problem of approximating a function by an element of a nonlinear manifold which admits a differentiable parametrization, typical examples being neural networks with differentiable activation functions or tensor networks. Natural gradient descent (NGD) for the optimization of a loss function can be seen as a preconditioned gradient descent where updates in the parameter space are driven by a functional perspective. In a spirit similar to Newton's method, a NGD step uses, instead of the Hessian, the Gram matrix of the generating system of the tangent space to the approximation manifold at the current iterate, with respect to a suitable metric. This corresponds to a locally optimal update in function space, following a projected gradient onto the tangent space to the manifold. Still, both gradient and natural gradient descent methods get stuck in local minima. Furthermore, when the model class is a nonlinear manifold or the loss function is not ideally conditioned (e.g., the KL-divergence for density estimation, or a norm of the residual of a partial differential equation in physics informed learning), even the natural gradient might yield non-optimal directions at each step. This work introduces a natural version of classical inertial dynamic methods like Heavy-Ball or Nesterov and show how it can improve the learning process when working with nonlinear model classes.

AI.tldr: Proposes a natural inertia dynamics for natural gradient descent on nonlinear manifolds to improve optimization in neural/tensor networks and physics-informed problems.
AI.motivation: Overcome local minima and ill-conditioning in NGD for nonlinear model classes and non-ideal losses (e.g., KL divergence, PDE residuals).
AI.method: Introduce a natural version of inertial dynamics (Heavy-Ball, Nesterov) within the natural gradient framework, preconditioning updates by the Gram matrix of the tangent space and incorporating momentum in function or parameter space.
AI.result: Conceptual framework; demonstrates that natural inertia can improve learning with nonlinear model classes.
AI.conclusion: Natural inertia NGD provides a promising optimization approach for nonlinear manifolds, potentially yielding faster convergence and better minima in complex learning tasks.

title: SENSE: Stereo OpEN Vocabulary SEmantic Segmentation
summary: Open-vocabulary semantic segmentation enables models to segment objects or image regions beyond fixed class sets, offering flexibility in dynamic environments. However, existing methods often rely on single-view images and struggle with spatial precision, especially under occlusions and near object boundaries. We propose SENSE, the first work on Stereo OpEN Vocabulary SEmantic Segmentation, which leverages stereo vision and vision-language models to enhance open-vocabulary semantic segmentation. By incorporating stereo image pairs, we introduce geometric cues that improve spatial reasoning and segmentation accuracy. Trained on the PhraseStereo dataset, our approach achieves strong performance in phrase-grounded tasks and demonstrates generalization in zero-shot settings. On PhraseStereo, we show a +2.9% improvement in Average Precision over the baseline method and +0.76% over the best competing method. SENSE also provides a relative improvement of +3.5% mIoU on Cityscapes and +18% on KITTI compared to the baseline work. By jointly reasoning over semantics and geometry, SENSE supports accurate scene understanding from natural language, essential for autonomous robots and Intelligent Transportation Systems.

AI.tldr: SENSE: a stereo open-vocabulary segmentation method that uses stereo pairs and vision-language models to improve spatial precision and open-vocabulary grounding, achieving state-of-the-art gains on PhraseStereo and notable improvements on Cityscapes and KITTI.
AI.motivation: Open-vocabulary semantic segmentation enables flexible, language-driven understanding but currently relies on single-view cues, which limit spatial precision, especially under occlusions and near boundaries. Stereo geometry can provide robust spatial cues to improve segmentation accuracy.
AI.method: SENSE integrates stereo image pairs with vision-language models to jointly reason about semantics and geometry. Trained on PhraseStereo, it leverages geometric cues (e.g., disparity, epipolar geometry) to refine open-vocabulary predictions and enhance phrase-grounded segmentation. The approach also demonstrates zero-shot generalization.
AI.result: On PhraseStereo, SENSE achieves +2.9% AP over the baseline and +0.76% over the best competing method. It also yields +3.5% mIoU on Cityscapes and +18% on KITTI relative to the baseline. The method shows strong performance on phrase-grounded tasks and generalizes to zero-shot settings.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating stereo geometry with open-vocabulary, language-driven segmentation improves spatial reasoning and scene understanding, offering more accurate, boundary-aware segmentation. This is beneficial for autonomous robots and Intelligent Transportation Systems, where robust open-vocabulary perception is essential.

title: Characterising LLM-Generated Competency Questions: a Cross-Domain Empirical Study using Open and Closed Models
comment: arXiv admin note: text overlap with arXiv:2507.02989
summary: Competency Questions (CQs) are a cornerstone of requirement elicitation in ontology engineering. CQs represent requirements as a set of natural language questions that an ontology should satisfy; they are traditionally modelled by ontology engineers together with domain experts as part of a human-centred, manual elicitation process. The use of Generative AI automates CQ creation at scale, therefore democratising the process of generation, widening stakeholder engagement, and ultimately broadening access to ontology engineering. However, given the large and heterogeneous landscape of LLMs, varying in dimensions such as parameter scale, task and domain specialisation, and accessibility, it is crucial to characterise and understand the intrinsic, observable properties of the CQs they produce (e.g., readability, structural complexity) through a systematic, cross-domain analysis. This paper introduces a set of quantitative measures for the systematic comparison of CQs across multiple dimensions. Using CQs generated from well defined use cases and scenarios, we identify their salient properties, including readability, relevance with respect to the input text and structural complexity of the generated questions. We conduct our experiments over a set of use cases and requirements using a range of LLMs, including both open (KimiK2-1T, LLama3.1-8B, LLama3.2-3B) and closed models (Gemini 2.5 Pro, GPT 4.1). Our analysis demonstrates that LLM performance reflects distinct generation profiles shaped by the use case.

AI.tldr: Defines quantitative metrics to compare competency questions (CQs) produced by various LLMs across defined use cases, showing that generation profiles depend on both the use case and the model type.
AI.motivation: To systematically characterize observable CQ properties (readability, relevance, and structural complexity) across a diverse set of LLMs and use cases for quality assurance in ontology requirement elicitation.
AI.method: Introduce quantitative measures for CQ evaluation, generate CQs from predefined use cases with a mix of open (KimiK2-1T, LLama3.1-8B, LLama3.2-3B) and closed (Gemini 2.5 Pro, GPT 4.1) models, and assess readability, input relevance, and structural complexity across multiple domains.
AI.result: Demonstrates distinct generation profiles for LLMs shaped by the use case; performance varies by model and task; readability, relevance, and structure trade-offs emerge across models.
AI.conclusion: LLMs enable scalable CQ generation but require cross-domain benchmarking; results guide model and prompting choices to tailor CQ generation to specific use cases.

title: NeuroLip: An Event-driven Spatiotemporal Learning Framework for Cross-Scene Lip-Motion-based Visual Speaker Recognition
summary: Visual speaker recognition based on lip motion offers a silent, hands-free, and behavior-driven biometric solution that remains effective even when acoustic cues are unavailable. Compared to traditional methods that rely heavily on appearance-dependent representations, lip motion encodes subject-specific behavioral dynamics driven by consistent articulation patterns and muscle coordination, offering inherent stability across environmental changes. However, capturing these robust, fine-grained dynamics is challenging for conventional frame-based cameras due to motion blur and low dynamic range. To exploit the intrinsic stability of lip motion and address these sensing limitations, we propose NeuroLip, an event-based framework that captures fine-grained lip dynamics under a strict yet practical cross-scene protocol: training is performed under a single controlled condition, while recognition must generalize to unseen viewing and lighting conditions. NeuroLip features a 1) Temporal-aware Voxel Encoding module with adaptive event weighting, 2) Structure-aware Spatial Enhancer that amplifies discriminative behavioral patterns by suppressing noise while preserving vertically structured motion information, and 3) Polarity Consistency Regularization mechanism to retain motion-direction cues encoded in event polarities. To facilitate systematic evaluation, we introduce DVSpeaker, a comprehensive event-based lip-motion dataset comprising 50 subjects recorded under four distinct viewpoint and illumination scenarios. Extensive experiments demonstrate that NeuroLip achieves near-perfect matched-scene accuracy and robust cross-scene generalization, attaining over 71% accuracy on unseen viewpoints and nearly 76% under low-light conditions, outperforming representative existing methods by at least 8.54%. The dataset and code are publicly available at https://github.com/JiuZeongit/NeuroLip.

AI.tldr: NeuroLip is an event-based lip-motion recognition framework that robustly generalizes across scenes and lighting, outperforming frame-based methods; introduces DVSpeaker dataset.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of frame-based lip-motion recognition (motion blur, limited dynamic range) and exploit the stable, subtle dynamics of lip motion under cross-scene conditions; provide cross-scene evaluation protocol.
AI.method: Temporal-aware Voxel Encoding with adaptive event weighting; Structure-aware Spatial Enhancer to reduce noise and preserve vertical motion structure; Polarity Consistency Regularization to retain motion-direction cues; training under a single controlled condition with recognition across unseen viewpoints and lighting; evaluation on DVSpeaker dataset (50 subjects, four scenarios).
AI.result: Near-perfect matched-scene accuracy; 71%+ accuracy on unseen viewpoints; ~76% accuracy under low-light; improvements over baselines by at least 8.54%; dataset and code released at GitHub.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates strong cross-scene generalization for event-based lip-motion recognition; provides a new dataset (DVSpeaker) and publicly available code to facilitate systematic evaluation.

title: Learning Affine-Equivariant Proximal Operators
summary: Proximal operators are fundamental across many applications in signal processing and machine learning, including solving ill-posed inverse problems. Recent work has introduced Learned Proximal Networks (LPNs), providing parametric functions that compute exact proximals for data-driven and potentially non-convex regularizers. However, in many settings it is important to include additional structure to these regularizers--and their corresponding proximals--such as shift and scale equivariance. In this work, we show how to obtain learned functions parametrized by neural networks that provably compute exact proximal operators while being equivariant to shifts and scaling, which we dub Affine-Equivariant Learned Proximal Networks (AE-LPNs). We demonstrate our results on synthetic, constructive examples, and then on real data via denoising in out-of-distribution settings. Our equivariant learned proximals enhance robustness to noise distributions and affine shifts far beyond training distributions, improving the practical utility of learned proximal operators

AI.tldr: Introduces Affine-Equivariant Learned Proximal Networks (AE-LPNs) that provably compute exact proximal operators while being equivariant to shifts and scaling; demonstrated on synthetic and real denoising tasks, showing improved robustness to distribution shifts.
AI.motivation: Regularizers in proximal methods often require structure; adding shift and scale equivariance to learned proximals can improve robustness to distributional shifts and enhance practical utility.
AI.method: Neural networks are parameterized to yield exact proximal operators with provable affine (shift and scaling) equivariance constraints, resulting in AE-LPNs; theoretical guarantees plus empirical validation on synthetic constructs and out-of-distribution denoising tasks.
AI.result: AE-LPNs successfully produce exact proximal operators that are affine-equivariant; experiments show enhanced robustness to noise distributions and affine shifts beyond training conditions, improving denoising performance under distribution shifts.
AI.conclusion: Affine-equivariant learned proximals extend the Learned Proximal Network framework and broaden its applicability, offering more robust, structure-preserving proximal operators for non-convex or data-driven regularizers.

title: FineCog-Nav: Integrating Fine-grained Cognitive Modules for Zero-shot Multimodal UAV Navigation
summary: UAV vision-language navigation (VLN) requires an agent to navigate complex 3D environments from an egocentric perspective while following ambiguous multi-step instructions over long horizons. Existing zero-shot methods remain limited, as they often rely on large base models, generic prompts, and loosely coordinated modules. In this work, we propose FineCog-Nav, a top-down framework inspired by human cognition that organizes navigation into fine-grained modules for language processing, perception, attention, memory, imagination, reasoning, and decision-making. Each module is driven by a moderate-sized foundation model with role-specific prompts and structured input-output protocols, enabling effective collaboration and improved interpretability. To support fine-grained evaluation, we construct AerialVLN-Fine, a curated benchmark of 300 trajectories derived from AerialVLN, with sentence-level instruction-trajectory alignment and refined instructions containing explicit visual endpoints and landmark references. Experiments show that FineCog-Nav consistently outperforms zero-shot baselines in instruction adherence, long-horizon planning, and generalization to unseen environments. These results suggest the effectiveness of fine-grained cognitive modularization for zero-shot aerial navigation. Project page: https://smartdianlab.github.io/projects-FineCogNav.

AI.tldr: FineCog-Nav introduces a cognitive modular architecture for UAV VLN, using fine-grained, role-specific modules driven by moderate foundation models, plus a new AerialVLN-Fine benchmark with aligned instructions. It reports improved instruction adherence, long-horizon planning, and generalization over zero-shot baselines.
AI.motivation: Current zero-shot UAV VLN methods rely on large base models, generic prompts, and loosely coupled modules. There is a need for interpretable, fine-grained modular designs that can handle long-horizon instructions in 3D environments and improve generalization.
AI.method: Propose FineCog-Nav, a top-down cognitive framework with modules for language processing, perception, attention, memory, imagination, reasoning, and decision-making. Each module is powered by a moderate-sized foundation model with role-specific prompts and strict input-output protocols to ensure coordinated collaboration. Create AerialVLN-Fine, a benchmark of 300 trajectories derived from AerialVLN with sentence-level instruction-trajectory alignment and refined instructions featuring explicit endpoints and landmarks.
AI.result: Experimental results show FineCog-Nav consistently outperforms zero-shot baselines in instruction adherence, long-horizon planning, and generalization to unseen environments.
AI.conclusion: FineCog-Nav demonstrates that fine-grained cognitive modularization with role-guided foundation models can enhance zero-shot aerial navigation, offering improved interpretability and performance; the AerialVLN-Fine benchmark supports fine-grained evaluation of such systems.

title: Learning to Reason with Insight for Informal Theorem Proving
summary: Although most of the automated theorem-proving approaches depend on formal proof systems, informal theorem proving can align better with large language models' (LLMs) strength in natural language processing. In this work, we identify a primary bottleneck in informal theorem proving as a lack of insight, namely the difficulty of recognizing the core techniques required to solve complex problems. To address this, we propose a novel framework designed to cultivate this essential reasoning skill and enable LLMs to perform insightful reasoning. We propose $\mathtt{DeepInsightTheorem}$, a hierarchical dataset that structures informal proofs by explicitly extracting core techniques and proof sketches alongside the final proof. To fully exploit this dataset, we design a Progressive Multi-Stage SFT strategy that mimics the human learning process, guiding the model from basic proof writing to insightful thinking. Our experiments on challenging mathematical benchmarks demonstrate that this insight-aware generation strategy significantly outperforms baselines. These results demonstrate that teaching models to identify and apply core techniques can substantially improve their mathematical reasoning.

AI.tldr: Proposes DeepInsightTheorem and a Progressive Multi-Stage SFT to teach LLMs to perform insight-based informal theorem proving by extracting core techniques and proof sketches, showing improved reasoning on challenging benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Informal theorem proving lacks 'insight'—the ability to recognize core techniques. LLMs struggle to solve complex problems without structured guidance. A dataset and staged training aim to cultivate this essential reasoning skill.
AI.method: Introduce DeepInsightTheorem, a hierarchical dataset that captures core techniques and proof sketches alongside final proofs; implement Progressive Multi-Stage SFT that guides learning from basic proofs to insightful technique-based reasoning.
AI.result: Empirical results on difficult mathematical benchmarks indicate that the insight-aware generation strategy outperforms baselines, suggesting improved mathematical reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Teaching models to identify and apply core techniques can substantially enhance informal mathematical reasoning; the framework shows promise for improving LLM-based informal theorem proving.

title: Diffusion Autoencoder for Unsupervised Artifact Restoration in Handheld Fundus Images
comment: 5 pages, 2 figures, 1 Table - ISBI IEEE 2025 CONFERENCE
summary: The advent of handheld fundus imaging devices has made ophthalmologic diagnosis and disease screening more accessible, efficient, and cost-effective. However, images captured from these setups often suffer from artifacts such as flash reflections, exposure variations, and motion-induced blur, which degrade image quality and hinder downstream analysis. While generative models have been effective in image restoration, most depend on paired supervision or predefined artifact structures, making them less adaptable to unstructured degradations commonly observed in handheld fundus images. To address this, we propose an unsupervised diffusion autoencoder that integrates a context encoder with the denoising process to learn semantically meaningful representations for artifact restoration. The model is trained only on high-quality table-top fundus images and infers to restore artifact-affected handheld acquisitions. We validate the restorations through quantitative and qualitative evaluations, and have shown that diagnostic accuracy increases to 81.17% on an unseen dataset and multiple artifact conditions

AI.tldr: Unsupervised diffusion autoencoder with a context encoder restores artifacted handheld fundus images using only high-quality tabletop data, achieving improved diagnostic accuracy (81.17%) on unseen handheld datasets.
AI.motivation: Handheld fundus photography suffers from unstructured artifacts (flare, exposure variation, motion blur) that hamper analysis. Paired-supervised or artifact-specific generative models are limited in generalization; an unsupervised approach aims to handle diverse degradations.
AI.method: An unsupervised diffusion autoencoder that integrates a context encoder into the denoising process. Trained on high-quality tabletop fundus images; infers to restore artifact-affected handheld acquisitions without paired supervision, leveraging semantic representations for restoration.
AI.result: Quantitative and qualitative evaluations indicate restored images improve diagnostic accuracy on unseen handheld data, achieving 81.17% across multiple artifact conditions.
AI.conclusion: The approach demonstrates effective, unsupervised restoration of handheld fundus images with unstructured degradations, enabling improved diagnostic performance without reliance on paired training data.

title: Predicting Where Steering Vectors Succeed
comment: 19 pages, incl. 10 appendix pages, 4 figures, 20 tables
summary: Steering vectors work for some concepts and layers but fail for others, and practitioners have no way to predict which setting applies before running an intervention. We introduce the Linear Accessibility Profile (LAP), a per-layer diagnostic that repurposes the logit lens as a predictor of steering vector effectiveness. The key measure, $A_{\mathrm{lin}}$, applies the model's unembedding matrix to intermediate hidden states, requiring no training. Across 24 controlled binary concept families on five models (Pythia-2.8B to Llama-8B), peak $A_{\mathrm{lin}}$ predicts steering effectiveness at $ρ= +0.86$ to $+0.91$ and layer selection at $ρ= +0.63$ to $+0.92$. A three-regime framework explains when difference-of-means steering works, when nonlinear methods are needed, and when no method can work. An entity-steering demo confirms the prediction end-to-end: steering at the LAP-recommended layer redirects completions on Gemma-2-2B and OLMo-2-1B-Instruct, while the middle layer (the standard heuristic) has no effect on either model.

AI.tldr: Introduces Linear Accessibility Profile (LAP), a per-layer diagnostic that predicts steering vector effectiveness using A_lin, a non-training metric based on the unembedding matrix applied to hidden states. Across 24 concept families and five models, A_lin correlates strongly with steering success, enabling layer selection and revealing when steering via difference-of-means, nonlinear methods, or not possible. Demonstrated end-to-end on Gemma and OLMo models, LAP layer recommendations outperform mid-layer heuristics.
AI.motivation: Steering vectors work variably across concepts and layers, and practitioners cannot predict which settings will be effective before running interventions. A lightweight, training-free diagnostic is needed to predict steering viability per layer.
AI.method: Define Linear Accessibility Profile (LAP) as a per-layer diagnostic. Compute A_lin by applying the model's unembedding matrix to intermediate hidden states (no training required). Evaluate across five models (Pythia-2.8B to Llama-8B) and 24 binary concept families. Assess correlation between A_lin and steering effectiveness (ρ) and layer selection. Propose a three-regime framework for when difference-of-means steering, nonlinear methods, or no method work. Demonstrate end-to-end with entities steering on Gemma-2-2B and OLMo-2-1B-Instruct using LAP-recommended layer; compare to the middle layer as a baseline.
AI.result: Peak A_lin predicts steering effectiveness with correlations ρ ≈ +0.86 to +0.91; layer selection correlation ρ ≈ +0.63 to +0.92. A three-regime framework explains when simple steering methods suffice, when nonlinear methods are needed, and when none work. An entity-steering demo shows end-to-end effectiveness at LAP-recommended layers for Gemma-2-2B and OLMo-2-1B-Instruct; the middle layer (standard heuristic) shows no effect.
AI.conclusion: LAP provides a robust, training-free per-layer diagnostic that guides where steering is viable, enabling reliable layer selection and highlighting regimes where different intervention methods are appropriate. The results are demonstrated across multiple models and concept families, with demonstrated end-to-end steering when followed.

title: Using Large Language Models and Knowledge Graphs to Improve the Interpretability of Machine Learning Models in Manufacturing
summary: Explaining Machine Learning (ML) results in a transparent and user-friendly manner remains a challenging task of Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI). In this paper, we present a method to enhance the interpretability of ML models by using a Knowledge Graph (KG). We store domain-specific data along with ML results and their corresponding explanations, establishing a structured connection between domain knowledge and ML insights. To make these insights accessible to users, we designed a selective retrieval method in which relevant triplets are extracted from the KG and processed by a Large Language Model (LLM) to generate user-friendly explanations of ML results. We evaluated our method in a manufacturing environment using the XAI Question Bank. Beyond standard questions, we introduce more complex, tailored questions that highlight the strengths of our approach. We evaluated 33 questions, analyzing responses using quantitative metrics such as accuracy and consistency, as well as qualitative ones such as clarity and usefulness. Our contribution is both theoretical and practical: from a theoretical perspective, we present a novel approach for effectively enabling LLMs to dynamically access a KG in order to improve the explainability of ML results. From a practical perspective, we provide empirical evidence showing that such explanations can be successfully applied in real-world manufacturing environments, supporting better decision-making in manufacturing processes.

AI.tldr: A KG-enhanced XAI method uses selective retrieval of KG triplets, processed by an LLM to generate user-friendly explanations for ML results in manufacturing; evaluated with 33 questions.
AI.motivation: Address the interpretability gap by coupling domain knowledge with ML explanations through a structured KG, enabling dynamic, context-aware explanations.
AI.method: Construct a knowledge graph storing domain data, ML results and explanations; perform selective retrieval to extract relevant triplets; use an LLM to generate user-friendly explanations from retrieved triplets; evaluate with the XAI Question Bank including complex, tailored questions.
AI.result: Quantitative metrics (accuracy, consistency) and qualitative metrics (clarity, usefulness) show the approach yields coherent explanations; demonstrates feasibility in real-world manufacturing environments.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a novel framework where LLMs dynamically access a KG to improve ML explainability, with empirical evidence of applicability in manufacturing and potential to support better decision-making.

title: Reward Weighted Classifier-Free Guidance as Policy Improvement in Autoregressive Models
summary: Consider an auto-regressive model that produces outputs x (e.g., answers to questions, molecules) each of which can be summarized by an attribute vector y (e.g., helpfulness vs. harmlessness, or bio-availability vs. lipophilicity). An arbitrary reward function r(y) encodes tradeoffs between these properties. Typically, tilting the model's sampling distribution to increase this reward is done at training time via reinforcement learning. However, if the reward function changes, re-alignment requires re-training. In this paper, we show that a reward weighted classifier-free guidance (RCFG) can act as a policy improvement operator in this setting, approximating tilting the sampling distribution by the Q function. We apply RCFG to molecular generation, demonstrating that it can optimize novel reward functions at test time. Finally, we show that using RCFG as a teacher and distilling into the base policy to serve as a warm start significantly speeds up convergence for standard RL.

AI.tldr: RCFG is a reward-weighted classifier-free guidance method that acts as a policy improvement operator for auto-regressive generation, enabling test-time optimization of arbitrary reward functions. It approximates Q-tilting, is demonstrated on molecular generation, and can speed up RL through teacher-student distillation.
AI.motivation: To enable flexible alignment of generative models when reward objectives change, avoiding full retraining, and to improve sample efficiency in RL for domains like molecule design.
AI.method: Introduce reward-weighted classifier-free guidance (RCFG) as a policy improvement operator that approximates tilting the sampling distribution via the Q-function. Apply RCFG to molecular generation to optimize new reward functions at test time. Use RCFG as a teacher to distill into the base policy, providing a warm-start that speeds convergence for standard RL.
AI.result: RCFG can approximate reward-tilting of the sampling distribution, enabling optimization of novel reward functions at test time in molecular generation. Distilling RCFG into the base policy accelerates RL convergence.
AI.conclusion: RCFG offers a flexible, plug-in alignment method for auto-regressive models, enabling test-time reward optimization and faster RL via distillation, with demonstrated utility in molecule design.

title: Sketch and Text Synergy: Fusing Structural Contours and Descriptive Attributes for Fine-Grained Image Retrieval
comment: Image Retrieval, Hand-drawn Sketch, Multi-stage Cross-modal Feature Alignment
summary: Fine-grained image retrieval via hand-drawn sketches or textual descriptions remains a critical challenge due to inherent modality gaps. While hand-drawn sketches capture complex structural contours, they lack color and texture, which text effectively provides despite omitting spatial contours. Motivated by the complementary nature of these modalities, we propose the Sketch and Text Based Image Retrieval (STBIR) framework. By synergizing the rich color and texture cues from text with the structural outlines provided by sketches, STBIR achieves superior fine-grained retrieval performance. First, a curriculum learning driven robustness enhancement module is proposed to enhance the model's robustness when handling queries of varying quality. Second, we introduce a category-knowledge-based feature space optimization module, thereby significantly boosting the model's representational power. Finally, we design a multi-stage cross-modal feature alignment mechanism to effectively mitigate the challenges of cross modal feature alignment. Furthermore, we curate the fine-grained STBIR benchmark dataset to rigorously validate the efficacy of our proposed framework and to provide data support as a reference for subsequent related research. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed STBIR framework significantly outperforms state of the art methods.

AI.tldr: STBIR integrates sketches and text for fine-grained image retrieval, introducing three modules—robustness via curriculum learning, category-knowledge-based feature space optimization, and multi-stage cross-modal alignment—plus a new benchmark dataset; shows state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Bridge the modality gap between sketch-based structural cues and text-based color/texture cues to enable robust, discriminative fine-grained retrieval; address query quality variability and strengthen cross-modal alignment.
AI.method: Three core modules: (1) curriculum learning driven robustness enhancement to handle varying query quality; (2) category-knowledge-based feature space optimization to boost representational power; (3) multi-stage cross-modal feature alignment to mitigate cross-modal disparities; plus curating a fine-grained STBIR benchmark dataset for evaluation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show that STBIR significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods on the proposed benchmark.
AI.conclusion: STBIR demonstrates the efficacy of leveraging complementary sketch and text modalities for fine-grained retrieval and provides a benchmark resource to guide future research.

title: PAWN: Piece Value Analysis with Neural Networks
summary: Predicting the relative value of any given chess piece in a position remains an open challenge, as a piece's contribution depends on its spatial relationships with every other piece on the board. We demonstrate that incorporating the state of the full chess board via latent position representations derived using a CNN-based autoencoder significantly improves accuracy for MLP-based piece value prediction architectures. Using a dataset of over 12 million piece-value pairs gathered from Grandmaster-level games, with ground-truth labels generated by Stockfish 17, our enhanced piece value predictor significantly outperforms context-independent MLP-based systems, reducing validation mean absolute error by 16% and predicting relative piece value within approximately 0.65 pawns. More generally, our findings suggest that encoding the full problem state as context provides useful inductive bias for predicting the contribution of any individual component.

AI.tldr: Full-board context via a CNN autoencoder improves piece-value prediction; MAE reduced by 16%; error ~0.65 pawns; dataset of 12M labels from GM games with Stockfish 17.
AI.motivation: Address the context-dependence of a piece's value and the limitations of context-free MLPs by encoding board state as a latent representation to guide per-piece valuation.
AI.method: Train a CNN-based autoencoder to learn latent representations of entire chess positions; use these latents as input to an MLP that predicts individual piece values; train on ~12M piece-value samples labeled by Stockfish 17; compare to context-independent MLP baselines; evaluate on validation set.
AI.result: Significant improvement over context-free models: ~16% reduction in mean absolute error; relative-value accuracy ~0.65 pawns; supports effectiveness of full-state encoding.
AI.conclusion: Encoding the full problem state as context provides a beneficial inductive bias for predicting component contributions; the approach likely generalizes to other domains where a component's value depends on interactions with all others.

title: Seeing the Intangible: Survey of Image Classification into High-Level and Abstract Categories
summary: The field of Computer Vision (CV) is increasingly shifting towards ``high-level'' visual sensemaking tasks, yet the exact nature of these tasks remains unclear and tacit. This survey paper addresses this ambiguity by systematically reviewing research on high-level visual understanding, focusing particularly on Abstract Concepts (ACs) in automatic image classification. Our survey contributes in three main ways: Firstly, it clarifies the tacit understanding of high-level semantics in CV through a multidisciplinary analysis, and categorization into distinct clusters, including commonsense, emotional, aesthetic, and inductive interpretative semantics. Secondly, it identifies and categorizes computer vision tasks associated with high-level visual sensemaking, offering insights into the diverse research areas within this domain. Lastly, it examines how abstract concepts such as values and ideologies are handled in CV, revealing challenges and opportunities in AC-based image classification. Notably, our survey of AC image classification tasks highlights persistent challenges, such as the limited efficacy of massive datasets and the importance of integrating supplementary information and mid-level features. We emphasize the growing relevance of hybrid AI systems in addressing the multifaceted nature of AC image classification tasks. Overall, this survey enhances our understanding of high-level visual reasoning in CV and lays the groundwork for future research endeavors.

AI.tldr: Survey on high-level visual understanding in CV, focusing on Abstract Concepts (ACs) in image classification. Categorizes high-level semantics into commonsense, emotional, aesthetic, and inductive interpretative semantics; maps tasks, analyzes handling of values/ideologies, and discusses challenges and opportunities, including data limitations and mid-level feature integration. Argues for hybrid AI systems and lays groundwork for future research.
AI.motivation: Clarify the tacit, high-level semantics in computer vision and systematically organize AC-related research to enable principled progress in AC-based image classification.
AI.method: Systematic review and multidisciplinary analysis: categorize high-level semantics into clusters; identify CV tasks tied to high-level sensemaking; examine treatment of abstract concepts (e.g., values/ideologies); synthesize challenges, opportunities, and the role of hybrid AI.
AI.result: Provides a clarified taxonomy of high-level semantics, identifies corresponding CV tasks, highlights persistent challenges (e.g., data efficacy, reliance on supplementary information and mid-level features), and advocates for hybrid AI systems; demonstrates the relevance of ACs in image classification and outlines directions for future work.
AI.conclusion: Enhances understanding of high-level visual reasoning in CV and establishes a foundation for future AC-focused research by outlining taxonomy, tasks, challenges, and the potential of hybrid approaches.

title: RefereeBench: Are Video MLLMs Ready to be Multi-Sport Referees
summary: While Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) excel at generic video understanding, their ability to support specialized, rule-grounded decision-making remains insufficiently explored. In this paper, we introduce RefereeBench, the first large-scale benchmark for evaluating MLLMs as automatic sports referees. Spanning 11 sports with 925 curated videos and 6,475 QA pairs, RefereeBench evaluates five core officiating abilities: foul existence, foul and penalty classification, foul and penalty reasoning, entity perception, and temporal grounding. The benchmark is fully human-annotated to ensure high-quality annotations grounded in authentic officiating logic and multimodal evidence. Extensive evaluations of state-of-the-art MLLMs show that even the strongest models, such as Doubao-Seed-1.8 and Gemini-3-Pro, achieve only around 60% accuracy, while the strongest open-source model, Qwen3-VL, reaches only 47%. These results indicate that current models remain far from being reliable sports referees. Further analysis shows that while models can often identify incidents and involved entities, they struggle with rule application and temporal grounding, and frequently over-call fouls on normal clips. Our benchmark highlights the need for future MLLMs that better integrate domain knowledge and multimodal understanding, advancing trustworthy AI-assisted officiating and broader multimodal decision-making.

AI.tldr: A large-scale benchmark, RefereeBench, evaluates multimodal LLMs as automatic sports referees across 11 sports with 925 videos and 6,475 QA pairs, highlighting gaps in foul rule application and temporal grounding.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of domain-grounded, trustworthy decision-making in multimodal LLMs for sports officiating and to provide a resource for evaluating and guiding model improvement.
AI.method: Fully human-annotated benchmark spanning five officiating abilities (foul existence, foul/penalty classification, foul/penalty reasoning, entity perception, temporal grounding); 925 curated videos; 6,475 QA pairs across 11 sports; evaluation of state-of-the-art MLLMs including Doubao-Seed-1.8, Gemini-3-Pro, and Qwen3-VL.
AI.result: Strong models reach ~60% accuracy; the strongest open-source model reaches ~47%; results show models identify incidents and entities but struggle with rule application and temporal grounding and often over-call fouls on normal clips.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark reveals the need for better integration of domain knowledge with multimodal understanding to enable trustworthy AI-assisted officiating and broader multimodal decision-making.

title: Adapting in the Dark: Efficient and Stable Test-Time Adaptation for Black-Box Models
summary: Test-Time Adaptation (TTA) for black-box models accessible only via APIs remains a largely unexplored challenge. Existing approaches such as post-hoc output refinement offer limited adaptive capacity, while Zeroth-Order Optimization (ZOO) enables input-space adaptation but faces high query costs and optimization challenges in the unsupervised TTA setting. We introduce BETA (Black-box Efficient Test-time Adaptation), a framework that addresses these limitations by employing a lightweight, local white-box steering model to create a tractable gradient pathway. Through a prediction harmonization technique combined with consistency regularization and prompt learning-oriented filtering, BETA enables stable adaptation with no additional API calls and negligible latency beyond standard inference. On ImageNet-C, BETA achieves a +7.1% accuracy gain on ViT-B/16 and +3.4% on CLIP, surpassing strong white-box and gray-box methods including TENT and TPT. On a commercial API, BETA achieves comparable performance to ZOO at 250x lower cost while maintaining real-time inference speed, establishing it as a practical and efficient solution for real-world black-box TTA.

AI.tldr: Proposes BETA, a black-box efficient test-time adaptation framework using a local white-box steering model to enable gradient-based adaptation for black-box APIs without extra API calls, achieving significant accuracy gains on ImageNet-C and competitive cost against ZOO on a commercial API.
AI.motivation: Black-box TTA approaches either offer limited adaptation (post-hoc refinement) or rely on costly zeroth-order optimization. There is a need for a practical, low-latency, gradient-based method that operates without additional API calls.
AI.method: Introduce a lightweight local white-box steering model to create a tractable gradient pathway for adaptation. Combine prediction harmonization with consistency regularization and prompt-learning-oriented filtering to achieve stable, unsupervised adaptation suitable for black-box models.
AI.result: On ImageNet-C, +7.1% accuracy for ViT-B/16 and +3.4% for CLIP, outperforming strong white-box and gray-box baselines (e.g., TENT, TPT). On a commercial API, performance is comparable to ZOO at ~250x lower cost with real-time inference.
AI.conclusion: BETA provides a practical and efficient solution for real-world black-box test-time adaptation, enabling stable, low-latency adaptation without extra API calls and with strong empirical gains.

title: Concept-wise Attention for Fine-grained Concept Bottleneck Models
comment: 10 pages, 7 figures, Accepted by CVPR 2026 Fingdings
summary: Recently impressive performance has been achieved in Concept Bottleneck Models (CBM) by utilizing the image-text alignment learned by a large pre-trained vision-language model (i.e. CLIP). However, there exist two key limitations in concept modeling. Existing methods often suffer from pre-training biases, manifested as granularity misalignment or reliance on structural priors. Moreover, fine-tuning with Binary Cross-Entropy (BCE) loss treats each concept independently, which ignores mutual exclusivity among concepts, leading to suboptimal alignment. To address these limitations, we propose Concept-wise Attention for Fine-grained Concept Bottleneck Models (CoAt-CBM), a novel framework that achieves adaptive fine-grained image-concept alignment and high interpretability. Specifically, CoAt-CBM employs learnable concept-wise visual queries to adaptively obtain fine-grained concept-wise visual embeddings, which are then used to produce a concept score vector. Then, a novel concept contrastive optimization guides the model to handle the relative importance of the concept scores, enabling concept predictions to faithfully reflect the image content and improved alignment. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CoAt-CBM consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods. The codes will be available upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: CoAt-CBM introduces concept-wise attention and a concept contrastive loss to enable adaptive, fine-grained image-to-concept alignment in Concept Bottleneck Models, improving interpretability and state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: Addresses pre-training biases in CBMs, such as granularity misalignment and reliance on structural priors, and overcomes BCE's independent treatment of concepts by modeling inter-concept relations and mutual exclusivity.
AI.method: Uses learnable concept-wise visual queries to extract fine-grained, concept-specific embeddings that feed into a concept score vector; introduces a novel concept contrastive optimization to weigh and relate concept scores according to their relative importance, enhancing alignment with image content.
AI.result: Empirical results show CoAt-CBM consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods on relevant benchmarks; code will be released upon acceptance.
AI.conclusion: The framework achieves adaptive, fine-grained image-concept alignment with improved interpretability, mitigating pre-training biases and the limitations of independent BCE-based supervision.

title: VoodooNet: Achieving Analytic Ground States via High-Dimensional Random Projections
summary: We present VoodooNet, a non-iterative neural architecture that replaces the stochastic gradient descent (SGD) paradigm with a closed-form analytic solution via Galactic Expansion. By projecting input manifolds into a high-dimensional, high-entropy "Galactic" space ($d \gg 784$), we demonstrate that complex features can be untangled without the thermodynamic cost of backpropagation. Utilizing the Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse to solve for the output layer in a single step, VoodooNet achieves a classification accuracy of \textbf{98.10\% on MNIST} and \textbf{86.63\% on Fashion-MNIST}. Notably, our results on Fashion-MNIST surpass a 10-epoch SGD baseline (84.41\%) while reducing the training time by orders of magnitude. We observe a near-logarithmic scaling law between dimensionality and accuracy, suggesting that performance is a function of "Galactic" volume rather than iterative refinement. This "Magic Hat" approach offers a new frontier for real-time Edge AI, where the traditional training phase is bypassed in favor of instantaneous manifold discovery.

AI.tldr: Non-iterative neural network (VoodooNet) uses a closed-form analytic solution via Galactic Expansion: high-dimensional projection and Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse to determine output weights in one step, achieving high MNIST accuracy and fast training, with a scaling law suggesting performance grows with Galactic volume.
AI.motivation: Provide a gradient-free alternative to backpropagation to reduce training time and computational cost, enabling real-time edge AI by instantaneous manifold discovery.
AI.method: Project inputs into a very high-dimensional space (d >> 784) using Galactic Expansion, then compute the output layer weights with the Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse in closed form, avoiding iterative gradient descent.
AI.result: Reported accuracies: MNIST 98.10%, Fashion-MNIST 86.63%. Fashion-MNIST exceeds a 10-epoch SGD baseline (84.41%). Training time reduced by orders of magnitude. Observed near-logarithmic scaling between dimensionality and accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The approach, termed 'Magic Hat', could redefine training by bypassing iterative optimization, enabling real-time edge AI; performance appears tied to the volume of the projected space rather than incremental refinement.

title: Flexible Empowerment at Reasoning with Extended Best-of-N Sampling
summary: This paper proposes a novel method that incorporates empowerment when reasoning actions in reinforcement learning (RL), thereby achieving the flexibility of exploration-exploitation dilemma (EED). In previous methods, empowerment for promoting exploration has been provided as a bonus term to the task-specific reward function as an intrinsically-motivated RL. However, this approach introduces a delay until the policy that accounts for empowerment is learned, making it difficult to adjust the emphasis on exploration as needed. On the other hand, a trick devised for fine-tuning recent foundation models at reasoning, so-called best-of-N (BoN) sampling, allows for the implicit acquisition of modified policies without explicitly learning them. It is expected that applying this trick to exploration-promoting terms, such as empowerment, will enable more flexible adjustment of EED. Therefore, this paper investigates BoN sampling for empowerment. Furthermore, to adjust the degree of policy modification in a generalizable manner while maintaining computational cost, this paper proposes a novel BoN sampling method extended by Tsalis statistics. Through toy problems, the proposed method's cability to balance EED is verified. In addition, it is demonstrated that the proposed method improves RL performance to solve complex locomotion tasks.

AI.tldr: A BoN sampling approach augmented with Tsallis statistics to flexibly leverage empowerment for exploration-exploitation in RL, enabling implicit policy modification without explicit empowerment policy learning, validated on toy and locomotion tasks.
AI.motivation: Address the latency and rigidity of explicit empowerment-based intrinsic rewards by adopting Best-of-N sampling to implicitly steer policies, and extend it with Tsallis statistics to control degree of policy modification in a computation-friendly way.
AI.method: Integrate empowerment as an intrinsic objective in BoN sampling; extend BoN with Tsallis statistics to modulate policy changes; evaluate on toy problems and locomotion tasks to assess EED balance and performance.
AI.result: Demonstrates that the proposed approach can balance EED in simple tasks and improves RL performance on complex locomotion tasks.
AI.conclusion: BoN sampling with empowerment and Tsallis-statistics extension provides a flexible, scalable mechanism for exploration-exploitation trade-off in RL without explicit empowerment policy learning, with potential broader applicability.

title: SegMix:Shuffle-based Feedback Learning for Semantic Segmentation of Pathology Images
summary: Segmentation is a critical task in computational pathology, as it identifies areas affected by disease or abnormal growth and is essential for diagnosis and treatment. However, acquiring high-quality pixel-level supervised segmentation data requires significant workload demands from experienced pathologists, limiting the application of deep learning. To overcome this challenge, relaxing the label conditions to image-level classification labels allows for more data to be used and more scenarios to be enabled. One approach is to leverage Class Activation Map (CAM) to generate pseudo pixel-level annotations for semantic segmentation with only image-level labels. However, this method fails to thoroughly explore the essential characteristics of pathology images, thus identifying only small areas that are insufficient for pseudo masking. In this paper, we propose a novel shuffle-based feedback learning method inspired by curriculum learning to generate higher-quality pseudo-semantic segmentation masks. Specifically, we perform patch level shuffle of pathology images, with the model adaptively adjusting the shuffle strategy based on feedback from previous learning. Experimental results demonstrate that our proposed approach outperforms state-of-the-arts on three different datasets.

AI.tldr: A shuffle-based feedback curriculum learning method improves pseudo pixel-level segmentation masks from image-level labels using patch-level shuffling and adaptive strategy, outperforming CAM-based baselines on three pathology datasets.
AI.motivation: Pixel-level annotations are costly; CAM-based pseudo-labels with image-level supervision are insufficiently accurate for precise semantic segmentation in pathology. A curriculum-like, feedback-driven approach could exploit pathology-specific characteristics to yield higher-quality masks.
AI.method: Patch-level image shuffling with an adaptive shuffle strategy guided by feedback from the learning process, inspired by curriculum learning, to refine pseudo-segmentation masks from image-level labels.
AI.result: Experimental results show the proposed method outperforms state-of-the-art approaches on three datasets.
AI.conclusion: The shuffle-based feedback learning framework effectively leverages weak supervision to produce higher-quality pseudo-segmentation masks in pathology, with potential applicability to other domains and label-scarce settings.

title: Majority Voting for Code Generation
summary: We investigate Functional Majority Voting (FMV), a method based on functional consensus for code generation with Large Language Models, which identifies a representative solution from multiple generations using their runtime execution signatures on test inputs. We find that FMV is an effective test-time inference strategy, substantially boosting performance on LiveCodeBench without a large compute overhead. Furthermore, we extend the utility of functional consensus and apply it as an aggregation strategy for label-free Test-Time Reinforcement Learning. We demonstrate that this increases pass@1 on holdout tasks, but find no evidence of self-improvement beyond the base model's performance ceiling.

AI.tldr: FMV selects a representative solution from multiple LLM generations via runtime execution signatures; improves test-time code generation and enables label-free TT-RL aggregation, with gains on LiveCodeBench but no evidence of self-improvement beyond the base model's ceiling.
AI.motivation: Mitigate variability and quality in LLM-generated code by exploiting runtime behavior to identify the best solution without retraining, and extend the idea of functional consensus to test-time reinforcement learning.
AI.method: Generate multiple candidate solutions, compute their runtime execution signatures on test inputs, and select a representative solution (or aggregate) based on those signatures; apply the same aggregation approach to label-free Test-Time Reinforcement Learning.
AI.result: FMV yields significant gains on LiveCodeBench with minimal compute overhead; in TT-RL, aggregation improves pass@1 on holdout tasks, but there is no evidence of self-improvement beyond the base model’s performance ceiling.
AI.conclusion: Functional consensus via runtime behavior is an effective, low-overhead inference strategy for code generation and TT-RL; benefits appear bounded by the base model's capabilities.

title: Fed3D: Federated 3D Object Detection
summary: 3D object detection models trained in one server plays an important role in autonomous driving, robotics manipulation, and augmented reality scenarios. However, most existing methods face severe privacy concern when deployed on a multi-robot perception network to explore large-scale 3D scene. Meanwhile, it is highly challenging to employ conventional federated learning methods on 3D object detection scenes, due to the 3D data heterogeneity and limited communication bandwidth. In this paper, we take the first attempt to propose a novel Federated 3D object detection framework (i.e., Fed3D), to enable distributed learning for 3D object detection with privacy preservation. Specifically, considering the irregular input 3D object in local robot and various category distribution between robots could cause local heterogeneity and global heterogeneity, respectively. We then propose a local-global class-aware loss for the 3D data heterogeneity issue, which could balance gradient back-propagation rate of different 3D categories from local and global aspects. To reduce communication cost on each round, we develop a federated 3D prompt module, which could only learn and communicate the prompts with few learnable parameters. To the end, several extensive experiments on federated 3D object detection show that our Fed3D model significantly outperforms state-of-the-art algorithms with lower communication cost when providing the limited local training data.

AI.tldr: Fed3D proposes a federated 3D object detection framework with privacy-preserving distributed learning. It introduces a local-global class-aware loss to balance gradient flows across heterogeneous 3D categories and a lightweight federated 3D prompt module to reduce communication by transmitting only a few learnable prompts. Empirical results claim significant outperformance of state-of-the-art methods with lower communication costs under limited local data.
AI.motivation: Privacy concerns and communication constraints in multi-robot perception networks for 3D object detection, coupled with severe 3D data heterogeneity that challenges conventional federated learning.
AI.method: Propose a local-global class-aware loss to balance gradient updates from local and global distributions across categories. Develop a federated 3D prompt module that learns and communicates only a small set of prompts to reduce bandwidth. Integrate these components into a Fed3D framework for distributed, privacy-preserving 3D object detection.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show Fed3D significantly outperforms state-of-the-art algorithms while incurring lower communication costs when local training data is limited.
AI.conclusion: Fed3D enables privacy-preserving, distributed learning for 3D object detection in federated settings. It addresses input irregularities and category distribution heterogeneity via a local-global class-aware loss and reduces communication overhead with a lightweight prompt module, representing a promising first attempt for real-world multi-robot deployments.

title: PINNACLE: An Open-Source Computational Framework for Classical and Quantum PINNs
summary: We present PINNACLE, an open-source computational framework for physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) that integrates modern training strategies, multi-GPU acceleration, and hybrid quantum-classical architectures within a unified modular workflow. The framework enables systematic evaluation of PINN performance across benchmark problems including 1D hyperbolic conservation laws, incompressible flows, and electromagnetic wave propagation. It supports a range of architectural and training enhancements, including Fourier feature embeddings, random weight factorization, strict boundary condition enforcement, adaptive loss balancing, curriculum training, and second-order optimization strategies, with extensibility to additional methods. We provide a comprehensive benchmark study quantifying the impact of these methods on convergence, accuracy, and computational cost, and analyze distributed data parallel scaling in terms of runtime and memory efficiency. In addition, we extend the framework to hybrid quantum-classical PINNs and derive a formal estimate for circuit-evaluation complexity under parameter-shift differentiation. Results highlight the sensitivity of PINNs to architectural and training choices, confirm their high computational cost relative to classical solvers, and identify regimes where hybrid quantum models offer improved parameter efficiency. PINNACLE provides a foundation for benchmarking physics-informed learning methods and guiding future developments through quantitative assessment of their trade-offs.

AI.tldr: PINNACLE is an open-source PINN framework with multi-GPU support and hybrid quantum-classical capabilities, offering a modular workflow and extensive benchmarking across PDE problems; results show sensitivity to architectural/training choices, high compute cost versus classical solvers, with some quantum regimes offering improved parameter efficiency.
AI.motivation: Provide a unified, extensible platform to systematically evaluate PINN training strategies, architectures, and quantum-classical hybrids, enabling rigorous benchmarking of convergence, accuracy, and computational cost across representative PDE problems.
AI.method: Develop PINNACLE with modular architecture supporting Fourier features, weight factorization, boundary enforcement, adaptive loss balancing, curriculum training, and second-order optimization; perform comprehensive benchmarks on 1D hyperbolic conservation laws, incompressible flows, electromagnetic wave propagation; analyze distributed data-parallel scaling; extend to hybrid quantum-classical PINNs and derive circuit-evaluation complexity under parameter-shift differentiation.
AI.result: Quantified how architectural/training choices affect convergence, accuracy, and cost; established scaling patterns in distributed training; demonstrated that PINNs can be computationally expensive relative to classical solvers, and identified regimes where hybrid quantum models yield better parameter efficiency.
AI.conclusion: PINNACLE provides a foundation for benchmarking physics-informed learning methods and guiding future development through quantitative assessment of trade-offs among accuracy, cost, and scalability, including the potential benefits of quantum-classical hybrids.

title: Beyond a Single Frame: Multi-Frame Spatially Grounded Reasoning Across Volumetric MRI
summary: Spatial reasoning and visual grounding are core capabilities for vision-language models (VLMs), yet most medical VLMs produce predictions without transparent reasoning or spatial evidence. Existing benchmarks also evaluate VLMs on isolated 2D images, overlooking the volumetric nature of clinical imaging, where findings can span multiple frames or appear on only a few slices. We introduce Spatially Grounded MRI Visual Question Answering (SGMRI-VQA), a 41,307-pair benchmark for multi-frame, spatially grounded reasoning on volumetric MRI. Built from expert radiologist annotations in the fastMRI+ dataset across brain and knee studies, each QA pair includes a clinician-aligned chain-of-thought trace with frame-indexed bounding box coordinates. Tasks are organized hierarchically across detection, localization, counting/classification, and captioning, requiring models to jointly reason about what is present, where it is, and across which frames it extends. We benchmark 10 VLMs and show that supervised fine-tuning of Qwen3-VL-8B with bounding box supervision consistently improves grounding performance over strong zero-shot baselines, indicating that targeted spatial supervision is an effective path toward grounded clinical reasoning.

AI.tldr: SGMRI-VQA introduces a large, multi-frame, spatially grounded MRI QA benchmark with expert per-frame bounding boxes and clinician-aligned chain-of-thought traces; supervised bounding-box supervision improves grounding on Qwen3-VL-8B over zero-shot baselines.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of transparent, spatially grounded reasoning in medical VLMs and the challenge of volumetric imaging that spans multiple frames.
AI.method: Construct SG MRI VQA: 41,307 QA pairs from fastMRI+ across brain and knee; includes frame-indexed bounding boxes and clinician-aligned chain-of-thought traces; tasks span detection, localization, counting/classification, and captioning; benchmark 10 VLMs; show that supervised fine-tuning of Qwen3-VL-8B with bounding box supervision improves grounding.
AI.result: Supervised bounding-box supervision yields better grounding than zero-shot baselines; targeted spatial supervision effectively enhances grounded clinical reasoning in VLMs.
AI.conclusion: Targeted spatial supervision and multi-frame grounding enable more transparent and reliable clinical reasoning in VLMs; dataset enables comprehensive evaluation and guides development of grounding-capable medical VLMs.

title: Stargazer: A Scalable Model-Fitting Benchmark Environment for AI Agents under Astrophysical Constraints
summary: The rise of autonomous AI agents suggests that dynamic benchmark environments with built-in feedback on scientifically grounded tasks are needed to evaluate the capabilities of these agents in research work. We introduce Stargazer, a scalable environment for evaluating AI agents on dynamic, iterative physics-grounded model-fitting tasks using inference on radial-velocity (RV) time series data. Stargazer comprises 120 tasks across three difficulty tiers, including 20 real archival cases, covering diverse scenarios ranging from high-SNR single-planet systems to complex multi-planetary configurations requiring involved low-SNR analysis. Our evaluation of eight frontier agents reveals a gap between numerical optimization and adherence to physical constraints: although agents often achieve a good statistical fit, they frequently fail to recover correct physical system parameters, a limitation that persists even when agents are equipped with vanilla skills. Furthermore, increasing test-time compute yields only marginal gains, with excessive token usage often reflecting recursive failure loops rather than meaningful exploration. Stargazer presents an opportunity to train, evaluate, scaffold, and scale strategies on a model-fitting problem of practical research relevance today. Our methodology to design a simulation-driven environment for AI agents presumably generalizes to many other model-fitting problems across scientific domains. Source code and the project website are available at https://github.com/Gudmorning2025/Stargazer and https://gudmorning2025.github.io/Stargazer, respectively.

AI.tldr: Stargazer is a scalable physics-grounded benchmark for AI agents evaluating model-fitting on radial-velocity time series, with 120 tasks across three difficulty tiers (including 20 archival cases). Frontier agents often achieve good statistical fits but fail to recover true physical parameters; more compute yields limited benefits due to recursive failure loops. Code and site released.
AI.motivation: There is a need for dynamic, feedback-driven benchmarks that assess whether AI agents can reason about physical constraints, not just optimize statistical fits. The gap between numerical optimization and physically plausible solutions motivates a dedicated, scalable environment.
AI.method: A simulation-driven environment (Stargazer) that presents iterative, physics-based model-fitting tasks using radial-velocity time series. It includes 120 tasks across three difficulty levels (20 real archival cases) and evaluates eight frontier agents to compare statistical fitting versus recovery of true system parameters; analyzes the effect of increased test-time compute and token usage.
AI.result: Agents achieve good statistical fits but frequently recover incorrect physical parameters. Vanilla skills remain insufficient to enforce physical plausibility. Increased compute provides marginal gains; high token usage often reflects recursive failure loops rather than meaningful exploration.
AI.conclusion: Stargazer offers a scalable, generalizable benchmark for model-fitting in scientific domains and can scaffold and scale strategies for realistic research problems. The methodology likely generalizes to other scientific model-fitting tasks; source code is publicly available.

title: Aligning What Vision-Language Models See and Perceive with Adaptive Information Flow
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have demonstrated strong capability in a wide range of tasks such as visual recognition, document parsing, and visual grounding. Nevertheless, recent work shows that while VLMs often manage to capture the correct image region corresponding to the question, they do not necessarily produce the correct answers. In this work, we demonstrate that this misalignment could be attributed to suboptimal information flow within VLMs, where text tokens distribute too much attention to irrelevant visual tokens, leading to incorrect answers. Based on the observation, we show that modulating the information flow during inference can improve the perception capability of VLMs. The idea is that text tokens should only be associated with important visual tokens during decoding, eliminating the interference of irrelevant regions. To achieve this, we propose a token dynamics-based method to determine the importance of visual tokens, where visual tokens that exhibit distinct activation patterns during different decoding stages are viewed as important. We apply our approach to representative open-source VLMs and evaluate on various datasets, including visual question answering, visual grounding and counting, optical character recognition, and object hallucination. The results show that our approach significantly improves the performance of baselines. Project page: https://cxliu0.github.io/AIF/.

AI.tldr: A token dynamics-based method modulates information flow in vision-language models by marking visual tokens as important if their activations differ across decoding stages, restricting text–visual interactions to these tokens to improve alignment and accuracy across VLM tasks.
AI.motivation: VLMs can localize the relevant image region yet still give incorrect answers due to suboptimal information flow where text tokens overly attend to irrelevant visual tokens, causing misalignment between perception and reasoning.
AI.method: Introduce a token dynamics-based criterion to identify important visual tokens: visual tokens exhibiting distinct activation patterns across decoding stages are considered important; during inference, text tokens are associated only with these important tokens to suppress interference from irrelevant regions.
AI.result: Applied to representative open-source VLMs and evaluated on diverse tasks including visual question answering, visual grounding and counting, optical character recognition, and mitigating object hallucination; results show significant performance gains over baselines.
AI.conclusion: Modulating information flow at inference—by pruning attention to non-important visual tokens based on dynamic activation patterns—improves perception and answer correctness in VLMs and generalizes across tasks and models.

title: NK-GAD: Neighbor Knowledge-Enhanced Unsupervised Graph Anomaly Detection
summary: Graph anomaly detection aims to identify irregular patterns in graph-structured data. Most unsupervised GNN-based methods rely on the homophily assumption that connected nodes share similar attributes. However, real-world graphs often exhibit attribute-level heterophily, where connected nodes have dissimilar attributes. Our analysis of attribute-level heterophily graphs reveals two phenomena indicating that current approaches are not practical for unsupervised graph anomaly detection: 1) attribute similarities between connected nodes show nearly identical distributions across different connected node pair types, and 2) anomalies cause consistent variation trends between the graph with and without anomalous edges in the low- and high-frequency components of the spectral energy distributions, while the mid-part exhibits more erratic variations. Based on these observations, we propose NK-GAD, a neighbor knowledge-enhanced unsupervised graph anomaly detection framework. NK-GAD integrates a joint encoder capturing both similar and dissimilar neighbor features, a neighbor reconstruction module modeling normal distributions, a center aggregation module refining node features, and dual decoders for reconstructing attributes and structures. Experiments on seven datasets show NK-GAD achieves an average 3.29\% AUC improvement.

AI.tldr: NK-GAD is a neighbor knowledge-enhanced unsupervised graph anomaly detection framework designed for attribute-level heterophily; it uses a joint encoder to integrate similar and dissimilar neighbor features, a neighbor reconstruction module, a center aggregation module, and dual decoders for attributes and structures, achieving ~3.29% average AUC improvement across seven datasets.
AI.motivation: Unsupervised GNN-based anomaly detection under attribute-level heterophily is poorly served by homophily-based methods; two empirical phenomena show current approaches fail: similar attribute distributions across connected node pairs and consistent spectral-trend differences between graphs with/without anomalies.
AI.method: Proposes NK-GAD: joint encoder capturing both similar and dissimilar neighbor features; neighbor reconstruction module modeling normal distributions; center aggregation refining node features; dual decoders reconstructing attributes and graph structures.
AI.result: Experimental evaluation on seven datasets reports an average AUC improvement of 3.29 percentage points over baselines.
AI.conclusion: NK-GAD offers a practical unsupervised GAD framework for heterophilous attribute-level graphs, leveraging neighbor knowledge and dual reconstruction to detect anomalies; further work could explore scalability, robustness, and sensitivity to graph properties.

title: Watching Movies Like a Human: Egocentric Emotion Understanding for Embodied Companions
summary: Embodied robotic agents often perceive movies through an egocentric screen-view interface rather than native cinematic footage, introducing domain shifts such as viewpoint distortion, scale variation, illumination changes, and environmental interference. However, existing research on movie emotion understanding is almost exclusively conducted on cinematic footage, limiting cross-domain generalization to real-world viewing scenarios. To bridge this gap, we introduce EgoScreen-Emotion (ESE), the first benchmark dataset for egocentric screen-view movie emotion understanding. ESE contains 224 movie trailers captured under controlled egocentric screen-view conditions, producing 28,667 temporally aligned key-frames annotated by multiple raters with a confidence-aware multi-label protocol to address emotional ambiguity.
