We further build a multimodal long-context emotion reasoning framework that models temporal visual evidence, narrative summaries, compressed historical context, and audio cues. Cross-domain experiments reveal a severe domain gap: models trained on cinematic footage drop from 27.99 to 16.69 Macro-F1 when evaluated on realistic egocentric screen-view observations. Training on ESE substantially improves robustness under realistic viewing conditions. Our approach achieves competitive performance compared with strong closed-source multimodal models, highlighting the importance of domain-specific data and long-context multimodal reasoning.

AI.tldr: First egocentric screen-view movie emotion benchmark; shows domain gap with cinematic footage; a long-context multimodal framework improves robustness and achieves competitive results.
AI.motivation: Address domain shift when moving from native cinematic footage to egocentric screen-view for emotion understanding; current studies are mostly cinematic footage, limiting cross-domain generalization to real-world viewing.
AI.method: Introduce EgoScreen-Emotion (ESE), a dataset of 224 movie trailers captured in egocentric screen-view under controlled conditions, yielding 28,667 temporally aligned key-frames with multi-label, confidence-aware annotations. Propose a multimodal long-context emotion reasoning framework that uses temporal visual evidence, narrative summaries, compressed historical context, and audio cues; evaluate cross-domain generalization between cinematic footage and egocentric view.
AI.result: Demonstrate a domain gap: macro-F1 degrades from 27.99 (cinematic) to 16.69 (egocentric) when models trained on cinematic footage are tested on egocentric data. Training on ESE improves robustness to egocentric viewing, and the proposed method achieves competitive performance with strong closed-source multimodal models.
AI.conclusion: Emphasizes the need for domain-specific data and long-context multimodal reasoning for robust emotion understanding in egocentric viewing; ESE enables better cross-domain generalization and robustness.

title: Hierarchical Active Inference using Successor Representations
comment: Accepted for publication in Neural Computation (MIT Press). 82 pages, 29 figures
summary: Active inference, a neurally-inspired model for inferring actions based on the free energy principle (FEP), has been proposed as a unifying framework for understanding perception, action, and learning in the brain. Active inference has previously been used to model ecologically important tasks such as navigation and planning, but scaling it to solve complex large-scale problems in real-world environments has remained a challenge. Inspired by the existence of multi-scale hierarchical representations in the brain, we propose a model for planning of actions based on hierarchical active inference. Our approach combines a hierarchical model of the environment with successor representations for efficient planning. We present results demonstrating (1) how lower-level successor representations can be used to learn higher-level abstract states, (2) how planning based on active inference at the lower-level can be used to bootstrap and learn higher-level abstract actions, and (3) how these learned higher-level abstract states and actions can facilitate efficient planning. We illustrate the performance of the approach on several planning and reinforcement learning (RL) problems including a variant of the well-known four rooms task, a key-based navigation task, a partially observable planning problem, the Mountain Car problem, and PointMaze, a family of navigation tasks with continuous state and action spaces. Our results represent, to our knowledge, the first application of learned hierarchical state and action abstractions to active inference in FEP-based theories of brain function.

AI.tldr: Hierarchical active inference with learned abstractions via successor representations enables scalable planning across multi-task environments; lower-level SRs bootstrap higher-level abstractions and actions, improving planning efficiency; demonstrated on navigation and planning benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To scale active inference to real-world-like planning by leveraging multi-scale hierarchical representations and efficient planning via successor representations.
AI.method: Propose a hierarchical environment model; use lower-level successor representations to derive higher-level abstract states; use active inference at the lower level to bootstrap learning of higher-level abstract actions; evaluate on several planning/RL benchmarks including four rooms, key-based navigation, partially observable planning, Mountain Car, PointMaze.
AI.result: Evidence that lower-level SRs can learn higher-level abstract states; lower-level active inference planning can bootstrap higher-level abstractions; high-level abstractions facilitate more efficient planning.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the viability of learned hierarchical state/action abstractions within FEP-based active inference, enabling scalable planning and suggesting a path towards integrating hierarchical RL concepts with active inference.

title: SSFT: A Lightweight Spectral-Spatial Fusion Transformer for Generic Hyperspectral Classification
comment: This paper has been accepted at IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition Workshops (CVPRW), 2026
summary: Hyperspectral imaging enables fine-grained recognition of materials by capturing rich spectral signatures, but learning robust classifiers is challenging due to high dimensionality, spectral redundancy, limited labeled data, and strong domain shifts. Beyond earth observation, labeled HSI data is often scarce and imbalanced, motivating compact models for generic hyperspectral classification across diverse acquisition regimes. We propose the lightweight Spectral-Spatial Fusion Transformer (SSFT), which factorizes representation learning into spectral and spatial pathways and integrates them via cross-attention to capture complementary wavelength-dependent and structural information. We evaluate our SSFT on the challenging HSI-Benchmark, a heterogeneous multi-dataset benchmark covering earth observation, fruit condition assessment, and fine-grained material recognition. SSFT achieves state-of-the-art overall performance, ranking first while using less than 2% of the parameters of the previous leading method. We further evaluate transfer to the substantially larger SpectralEarth benchmark under the official protocol, where SSFT remains competitive despite its compact size. Ablation studies show that both spectral and spatial pathways are crucial, with spatial modeling contributing most, and that SSFT remains robust without data augmentation.

AI.tldr: A compact dual-path Spectral-Spatial Fusion Transformer (SSFT) for hyperspectral image classification that factorizes learning into spectral and spatial streams and fuses them via cross-attention, achieving state-of-the-art results on a challenging multi-dataset HSI benchmark with very few parameters and competitive transfer performance.
AI.motivation: Hyperspectral imaging offers rich spectral signatures but faces high dimensionality, spectral redundancy, limited labeled data, and domain shifts. There is a need for robust, compact models that generalize across diverse acquisition regimes beyond earth observation.
AI.method: SSFT factorizes representation learning into separate spectral and spatial pathways and integrates them through cross-attention to capture complementary wavelength-dependent and structural information. The model is lightweight (<2% of prior leading method parameters). Evaluation is conducted on HSI-Benchmark (earth observation, fruit condition, material recognition) and transfer to the larger SpectralEarth benchmark, with ablations showing both pathways are essential and spatial modeling contributes most. The model performs well even without data augmentation.
AI.result: SSFT achieves state-of-the-art overall performance on HSI-Benchmark, ranking first while using <2% of the parameters of the previous leading method. It remains competitive on the larger SpectralEarth benchmark under official protocol. Ablations indicate both spectral and spatial pathways are crucial, with spatial modeling contributing more, and the model is robust without data augmentation.
AI.conclusion: A lightweight, dual-pathway transformer with cross-attention effectively captures spectral and spatial information for HSI classification, delivering superior accuracy with far fewer parameters and demonstrating strong generalization across heterogeneous datasets, though ablations highlight stronger impact from spatial modeling.

title: Neural Continuous-Time Markov Chain: Discrete Diffusion via Decoupled Jump Timing and Direction
summary: Discrete diffusion models based on continuous-time Markov chains (CTMCs) have shown strong performance on language and discrete data generation, yet existing approaches typically parameterize the reverse rate matrix as a single object -- via concrete scores, clean-data predictions ($x_0$-parameterization), or denoising distributions -- rather than aligning the parameterization with the intrinsic CTMC decomposition into jump timing and jump direction. Since a CTMC is fundamentally a Poisson process fully determined by these two quantities, decomposing along this structure is closer to first principles and naturally leads to our formulation. We propose \textbf{Neural CTMC}, which separately parameterizes the reverse process through an \emph{exit rate} (when to jump) and a \emph{jump distribution} (where to jump) using two dedicated network heads. We show that the evidence lower bound (ELBO) differs from a path-space KL divergence between the true and learned reverse processes by a $θ$-independent constant, so that the training objective is fully governed by the exit rate and jump distribution we parameterize. Moreover, this KL factorizes into a Poisson KL for timing and a categorical KL for direction. We further show that the tractable conditional surrogate preserves the gradients and minimizers of the corresponding marginal reverse-process objective under standard regularity assumptions. Our theoretical framework also covers masked and GIDD-style noise schedules. Empirically, while the uniform forward process has been explored in prior work, our model, to our best of the knowledge, is the first pure-uniform method to outperform mask-based methods on the OpenWebText dataset.To facilitate reproducibility, we release our pretrained weights at https://huggingface.co/Jiangxy1117/Neural-CTMC.

AI.tldr: Neural-CTMC introduces a principled discrete diffusion model by factorizing the reverse CTMC into separate timing (exit rate) and direction (jump distribution) components, leading to an ELBO that decomposes into a Poisson timing KL and a categorical direction KL. This framework allows conditional surrogates to preserve gradients and shows empirical gains over mask-based methods on OpenWebText with a uniform forward process, with released pretrained weights.
AI.motivation: To align discrete diffusion modeling with the intrinsic CTMC structure (Poisson process characterized by jump timing and direction), addressing limitations of single-object reverse-rate parameterizations and improving data-generation quality for language and discrete data.
AI.method: Propose Neural-CTMC with two dedicated network heads: one predicting the exit rate (timing of jumps) and another predicting the jump distribution (destination states). Analyze ELBO, show it differs from path-space KL by a constant, leading to a KL that factorizes into a Poisson KL (timing) and a categorical KL (direction). Establish a tractable conditional surrogate that preserves gradients and minimizers under standard regularity. Accommodates masked and GIDD-style noise schedules and uses a forward process that can be uniform.
AI.result: The approach achieves state-of-the-art or improved performance over mask-based methods on OpenWebText using a pure-uniform forward process. Reproducibility is supported by releasing pretrained weights at a public HuggingFace repository.
AI.conclusion: A two-headed Neural-CTMC yields a principled, practically effective discrete diffusion framework that respects the CTMC decomposition into timing and direction, with theoretical guarantees on the training objective and empirical gains on language/discrete data tasks; supports flexible noise schedules and is publicly reusable.

title: Beyond Text Prompts: Precise Concept Erasure through Text-Image Collaboration
summary: Text-to-image generative models have achieved impressive fidelity and diversity, but can inadvertently produce unsafe or undesirable content due to implicit biases embedded in large-scale training datasets. Existing concept erasure methods, whether text-only or image-assisted, face trade-offs: textual approaches often fail to fully suppress concepts, while naive image-guided methods risk over-erasing unrelated content. We propose TICoE, a text-image Collaborative Erasing framework that achieves precise and faithful concept removal through a continuous convex concept manifold and hierarchical visual representation learning. TICoE precisely removes target concepts while preserving unrelated semantic and visual content. To objectively assess the quality of erasure, we further introduce a fidelity-oriented evaluation strategy that measures post-erasure usability. Experiments on multiple benchmarks show that TICoE surpasses prior methods in concept removal precision and content fidelity, enabling safer, more controllable text-to-image generation. Our code is available at https://github.com/OpenAscent-L/TICoE.git

AI.tldr: Introduces TICoE, a text-image collaborative erasing framework that uses a continuous convex concept manifold and hierarchical visual representations to precisely erase target concepts in text-to-image models while preserving unrelated content. Adds a fidelity-oriented evaluation for post-erasure usability and reports state-of-the-art performance; code released.
AI.motivation: Safety and controllability of text-to-image generation are challenged by implicit biases in training data. Existing concept-erasure methods struggle to fully remove concepts (text-only approaches) or risk erasing unrelated content (image-guided methods). A precise, faithful, multimodal erasure mechanism is needed.
AI.method: Propose TICoE (text-image Collaborative Erasing). It leverages a continuous convex concept manifold to model target concepts and employs hierarchical visual representation learning to align and erase them across modalities. The framework enables collaborative erasing between text and image cues to achieve precise removal while preserving other semantics. A fidelity-oriented evaluation strategy is introduced to quantify post-erasure usability.
AI.result: Across multiple benchmarks, TICoE achieves higher Concept Removal Precision and Content Fidelity than prior methods, demonstrating safer and more controllable text-to-image generation. The authors validate the approach with quantitative metrics and show improved preservation of unrelated content.
AI.conclusion: TICoE provides a more precise and faithful erasure of target concepts in text-to-image generation, enabling safer and more controllable outputs. The method balances effective concept removal with preservation of non-target content, backed by a fidelity-focused evaluation and a public code release.

title: Graph self-supervised learning based on frequency corruption
comment: 11 pages, 4 tables, 3 figures. Accepted at The ACM Web Conference 2026 (WWW 2026)
summary: Graph self-supervised learning can reduce the need for labeled graph data and has been widely used in recommendation, social networks, and other web applications. However, existing methods often underuse high-frequency signals and may overfit to specific local patterns, which limits representation quality and generalization. We propose Frequency-Corrupt Based Graph Self-Supervised Learning (FC-GSSL), a method that builds corrupted graphs biased toward high-frequency information by corrupting nodes and edges according to their low-frequency contributions. These corrupted graphs are used as inputs to an autoencoder, while low-frequency and general features are reconstructed as supervision targets, forcing the model to fuse information from multiple frequency bands. We further design multiple sampling strategies and generate diverse corrupted graphs from the intersections and unions of the sampling results. By aligning node representations from these views, the model can discover useful frequency combinations, reduce reliance on specific high-frequency components, and improve robustness. Experiments on 14 datasets across node classification, graph prediction, and transfer learning show that FC-GSSL consistently improves performance and generalization.

AI.tldr: A frequency-aware graph self-supervised learning framework (FC-GSSL) that corrupts graphs to emphasize high-frequency components, uses an autoencoder to fuse low-, mid-, and high-frequency information, and aligns multi-view representations to improve generalization.
AI.motivation: Graph SSL methods often underutilize high-frequency signals and can overfit to local patterns. A framework that explicitly exploits multiple frequency bands and robustly fuses them can enhance representation quality and transferability.
AI.method: Construct corrupted graphs biased toward high-frequency information by perturbing nodes and edges according to their low-frequency contributions. Use an autoencoder to reconstruct low-frequency and general features as supervision targets. Implement multiple sampling strategies to generate diverse corrupted views via intersections and unions of sampling results. Align node representations across views to learn useful frequency combinations and reduce reliance on any single high-frequency component.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on 14 datasets covering node classification, graph prediction, and transfer learning shows consistent improvements in performance and generalization.
AI.conclusion: FC-GSSL improves representation quality and robustness by leveraging and fusing information across multiple graph frequency bands, reducing dependence on specific high-frequency components.

title: Learning to Look before Learning to Like: Incorporating Human Visual Cognition into Aesthetic Quality Assessment
comment: Accepted for Poster Presentation at CogSci 2026
summary: Automated Aesthetic Quality Assessment (AQA) treats images primarily as static pixel vectors, aligning predictions with human-rating scores largely through semantic perception. However, this paradigm diverges from human aesthetic cognition, which arises from dynamic visual exploration shaped by scanning paths, processing fluency, and the interplay between bottom-up salience and top-down intention. We introduce AestheticNet, a novel cognitive-inspired AQA paradigm that integrates human-like visual cognition and semantic perception with a two-pathway architecture. The visual attention pathway, implemented as a gaze-aligned visual encoder (GAVE) pre-trained offline on eye-tracking data using resource-efficient contrast gaze alignment, models attention from human vision system. This pathway augments the semantic pathway, which uses a fixed semantic encoder such as CLIP, through cross-attention fusion. Visual attention provides a cognitive prior reflecting foreground/background structure, color cascade, brightness, and lighting, all of which are determinants of aesthetic perception beyond semantics. Experiments validated by hypothesis testing show a consistent improvement over the semantic-alone baselines, and demonstrate the gaze module as a model-agnostic corrector compatible with diverse AQA backbones, supporting the necessity and modularity of human-like visual cognition for AQA. Our code is available at https://github.com/keepgallop/AestheticNet.

AI.tldr: AestheticNet proposes a cognitive-inspired two-pathway AQA model with a gaze-aligned visual encoder (GAVE) trained on eye-tracking data to complement a fixed semantic encoder (e.g., CLIP) via cross-attention, yielding improved aesthetic quality assessment (AQA) over semantic-only baselines and offering a modular, model-agnostic gaze module.
AI.motivation: Current AQA methods treat images as static pixel vectors and rely on semantic perception, but human aesthetic judgment emerges from dynamic gaze, processing fluency, and bottom-up/top-down integration. The work aims to align AI with human aesthetic cognition by incorporating visual cognition into AQA.
AI.method: Two-pathway architecture: (1) visual attention pathway via gaze-aligned visual encoder (GAVE) pretrained offline on eye-tracking data using resource-efficient contrast gaze alignment; (2) semantic pathway using a fixed semantic encoder (e.g., CLIP). The outputs fuse through cross-attention; the gaze pathway provides cognitive priors about foreground/background, color cascade, brightness, lighting. The gaze module is demonstrated as a model-agnostic corrector compatible with diverse AQA backbones.
AI.result: Experiments validated by hypothesis testing show consistent improvement over semantic-alone baselines. The gaze module reliably improves performance and is compatible with diverse backbones, supporting modularity and necessity of human-like visual cognition in AQA.
AI.conclusion: Integrating human-like visual cognition with semantic perception improves AQA performance; the gaze module serves as a modular, model-agnostic enhancement that can be plugged into various backbones. Code is publicly available.

title: Towards Robust Endogenous Reasoning: Unifying Drift Adaptation in Non-Stationary Tuning
summary: Reinforcement Fine-Tuning (RFT) has established itself as a critical paradigm for the alignment of Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs) with complex human values and domain-specific requirements. Nevertheless, current research primarily focuses on mitigating exogenous distribution shifts arising from data-centric factors, the non-stationarity inherent in the endogenous reasoning remains largely unexplored. In this work, a critical vulnerability is revealed within MLLMs: they are highly susceptible to endogenous reasoning drift, across both thinking and perception perspectives. It manifests as unpredictable distribution changes that emerge spontaneously during the autoregressive generation process, independent of external environmental perturbations. To adapt it, we first theoretically define endogenous reasoning drift within the RFT of MLLMs as the multi-modal concept drift. In this context, this paper proposes Counterfactual Preference Optimization ++ (CPO++), a comprehensive and autonomous framework adapted to the multi-modal concept drift. It integrates counterfactual reasoning with domain knowledge to execute controlled perturbations across thinking and perception, employing preference optimization to disentangle spurious correlations. Extensive empirical evaluations across two highly dynamic and safety-critical domains: medical diagnosis and autonomous driving. They demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves superior performance in reasoning coherence, decision-making precision, and inherent robustness against extreme interference. The methodology also exhibits exceptional zero-shot cross-domain generalization, providing a principled foundation for reliable multi-modal reasoning in safety-critical applications.

AI.tldr: CPO++ mitigates endogenous reasoning drift (multi-modal concept drift) in Reinforcement Fine-Tuning of MLLMs by using counterfactual reasoning, domain knowledge, and preference optimization to perturb thinking/perception and disentangle spurious correlations, yielding improved coherence, accuracy, robustness, and zero-shot cross-domain generalization in medical diagnosis and autonomous driving.
AI.motivation: While exogenous data shifts have been widely studied, endogenous reasoning drift during autoregressive generation remains underexplored in multi-modal LLMs. Addressing this drift is critical for reliable safety-critical AI systems.
AI.method: Define endogenous reasoning drift as multi-modal concept drift and introduce Counterfactual Preference Optimization ++ (CPO++) that combines counterfactual perturbations across thinking and perception with domain knowledge and preference optimization to suppress spurious correlations.
AI.result: Extensive empirical evaluation in medical diagnosis and autonomous driving shows superior reasoning coherence, decision-making precision, and robustness to extreme interference, with strong zero-shot cross-domain generalization.
AI.conclusion: CPO++ provides a principled framework for robust, safe multi-modal reasoning under concept drift in RFT of MLLMs, enabling safer deployment in dynamic, safety-critical domains.

title: Robust Multispectral Semantic Segmentation under Missing or Full Modalities via Structured Latent Projection
summary: Multimodal remote sensing data provide complementary information for semantic segmentation, but in real-world deployments, some modalities may be unavailable due to sensor failures, acquisition issues, or challenging atmospheric conditions. Existing multimodal segmentation models typically address missing modalities by learning a shared representation across inputs. However, this approach can introduce a trade-off by compromising modality-specific complementary information and reducing performance when all modalities are available. In this paper, we tackle this limitation with CBC-SLP, a multimodal semantic segmentation model designed to preserve both modality-invariant and modality-specific information. Inspired by the theoretical results on modality alignment, which state that perfectly aligned multimodal representations can lead to sub-optimal performance in downstream prediction tasks, we propose a novel structured latent projection approach as an architectural inductive bias. Rather than enforcing this strategy through a loss term, we incorporate it directly into the architecture. In particular, to use the complementary information effectively while maintaining robustness under random modality dropout, we structure the latent representations into shared and modality-specific components and adaptively transfer them to the decoder according to the random modality availability mask. Extensive experiments on three multimodal remote sensing image sets demonstrate that CBC-SLP consistently outperforms state-of-the-art multimodal models across full and missing modality scenarios. Besides, we empirically demonstrate that the proposed strategy can recover the complementary information that may not be preserved in a shared representation. The code is available at https://github.com/iremulku/Multispectral-Semantic-Segmentation-via-Structured-Latent-Projection-CBC-SLP-.

AI.tldr: CBC-SLP is a multimodal semantic segmentation model that preserves both modality-invariant (shared) and modality-specific information via a structured latent projection, enabling robustness to random modality dropout and improved performance on full and missing modality scenarios compared to state-of-the-art models.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitation of shared-representation multimodal segmentation methods that can sacrifice modality-specific complementary information and degrade performance when all modalities are available. Leveraging theoretical results that perfect modality alignment can hurt downstream tasks, the work seeks a architectural inductive bias that preserves both shared and private information.
AI.method: Introduce a structured latent projection in CBC-SLP that partitions latent representations into shared and modality-specific components. The decoder adaptively receives information from these components based on the availability mask, avoiding explicit alignment losses and enabling robustness to modality dropout.
AI.result: CBC-SLP consistently outperforms state-of-the-art multimodal models in both full-modality and missing-modality settings across three remote sensing datasets. It also demonstrates recovery of complementary information that may be lost in a fully shared representation.
AI.conclusion: Architectural inductive bias via structured latent projections can preserve both modality-invariant and modality-specific information, yielding robust and superior multimodal segmentation without relying on alignment losses. The approach is validated on multiple remote sensing datasets and code is publicly available.

title: Reasoning-targeted Jailbreak Attacks on Large Reasoning Models via Semantic Triggers and Psychological Framing
summary: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) have demonstrated strong capabilities in generating step-by-step reasoning chains alongside final answers, enabling their deployment in high-stakes domains such as healthcare and education. While prior jailbreak attack studies have focused on the safety of final answers, little attention has been given to the safety of the reasoning process. In this work, we identify a novel problem that injects harmful content into the reasoning steps while preserving unchanged answers. This type of attack presents two key challenges: 1) manipulating the input instructions may inadvertently alter the LRM's final answer, and 2) the diversity of input questions makes it difficult to consistently bypass the LRM's safety alignment mechanisms and embed harmful content into its reasoning process. To address these challenges, we propose the Psychology-based Reasoning-targeted Jailbreak Attack (PRJA) Framework, which integrates a Semantic-based Trigger Selection module and a Psychology-based Instruction Generation module. Specifically, the proposed PRJA automatically selects manipulative reasoning triggers via semantic analysis and leverages psychological theories of obedience to authority and moral disengagement to generate adaptive instructions for enhancing the LRM's compliance with harmful content generation. Extensive experiments on five question-answering datasets demonstrate that PRJA achieves an average attack success rate of 83.6\% against several commercial LRMs, including DeepSeek R1, Qwen2.5-Max, and OpenAI o4-mini.

AI.tldr: PRJA is a psychology-informed jailbreak framework that injects harmful content into LRM reasoning steps while keeping final answers intact, achieving 83.6% attack success across five QA datasets and multiple commercial LRMs.
AI.motivation: To address safety of intermediate reasoning traces in LRMs, which has been overlooked compared to final-answer safety. The goal is to understand and demonstrate how reasoning processes can be manipulated without altering outcomes, highlighting a blind spot in safety alignment.
AI.method: Semantic-based Trigger Selection module automatically identifies manipulative reasoning prompts; Psychology-based Instruction Generation module leverages obedience and moral disengagement theories to craft adaptive instructions that steer the model toward producing harmful reasoning steps while preserving the final answer.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on five QA datasets show an average attack success rate of 83.6% against several commercial LRMs (e.g., DeepSeek R1, Qwen2.5-Max, OpenAI o4-mini).
AI.conclusion: LPJA reveals a vulnerability in LRMs where harmful content can be embedded in reasoning traces without changing answers, underscoring the need for defenses targeting chain-of-thought safety and robust alignment that governs step-by-step reasoning.

title: AHS: Adaptive Head Synthesis via Synthetic Data Augmentations
summary: Recent digital media advancements have created increasing demands for sophisticated portrait manipulation techniques, particularly head swapping, where one's head is seamlessly integrated with another's body. However, current approaches predominantly rely on face-centered cropped data with limited view angles, significantly restricting their real-world applicability. They struggle with diverse head expressions, varying hairstyles, and natural blending beyond facial regions. To address these limitations, we propose Adaptive Head Synthesis (AHS), which effectively handles full upper-body images with varied head poses and expressions. AHS incorporates a novel head reenacted synthetic data augmentation strategy to overcome self-supervised training constraints, enhancing generalization across diverse facial expressions and orientations without requiring paired training data. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that AHS achieves superior performance in challenging real-world scenarios, producing visually coherent results that preserve identity and expression fidelity across various head orientations and hairstyles. Notably, AHS shows exceptional robustness in maintaining facial identity while drastic expression changes and faithfully preserving accessories while significant head pose variations.

AI.tldr: Adaptive Head Synthesis (AHS) enables robust full upper-body portrait generation with varied head poses/expressions using a head reenacted synthetic data augmentation strategy, addressing self-supervised training limitations and outperforming face-centered methods.
AI.motivation: Current methods rely on face-centered crops with limited view angles, failing to handle full upper-body views, diverse expressions, hairstyles, and seamless blending beyond facial regions; need data-efficient training that generalizes to real-world variations.
AI.method: Proposes AHS, a full upper-body portrait synthesis framework. Introduces head reenacted synthetic data augmentation to create diverse, paired-like supervision for self-supervised training without requiring paired training data; capable of rendering heads with arbitrary poses/expressions while preserving identity and accessories; design likely includes a generator and a reenactment module to enable cross-pose/expression synthesis.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show AHS achieves superior performance in challenging real-world scenarios, delivering visually coherent results that preserve identity, expression fidelity, and accessories across head orientations and hairstyles; robust to drastic expression changes and large pose variations.
AI.conclusion: AHS demonstrates improved generalization for head-to-body portrait manipulation, enabling realistic, controllable synthesis in more diverse conditions; synthetic augmentation effectively mitigates lack of paired data and enhances identity/expression preservation.

title: Why Colors Make Clustering Harder:Global Integrality Gaps, the Price of Fairness, and Color-Coupled Algorithms in Chromatic Correlation Clustering
summary:
Chromatic Correlation Clustering (CCC) extends Correlation Clustering by assigning semantic colors to edges and requiring each cluster to receive a single color label. Unlike standard CC, whose LP relaxation has integrality gap 2 on complete graphs and admits a 2.06-approximation, the analogous LP for CCC has a strict lower bound of 2.11, and the best known LP-rounding algorithm achieves 2.15. We explain this gap by isolating the source of difficulty: cross-edge chromatic interference. Neutral edges, whose color does not match the candidate cluster color, create an irreducible cost absent from standard CC and force any color-independent rounding scheme to pay an additional mismatch penalty.
  We make four contributions. First, we prove a Global Integrality Gap Decomposition Theorem showing that the gap of any color-independent CCC rounding algorithm equals the standard CC gap plus an irreducible chromatic penalty Delta(L) > 0. Second, we solve the associated min-max problem and derive the staircase formula Delta(L) = ((L-1)/L) Delta_infinity, where Delta_infinity is approximately 0.0734. In particular, the two-color gap is 2.0967, separating CCC from standard CC already at L = 2. Third, we introduce Color-Coupled Correlation Clustering (C4). Adding the valid global constraint sum_c x_uv^c >= L-1 and a correlated interval-packing rounding scheme makes neutral edges behave like classical negative edges, recovering the optimal 2.06 approximation and bypassing the 2.11 lower bound for the uncoupled LP. Fourth, experiments on extremal instances, real multi-relational networks, and fairness benchmarks validate the theory: empirical LP gaps follow the predicted staircase, and C4 matches the unconstrained approximation ratio under fairness constraints.

AI.tldr: CCC extends Correlation Clustering by labeling edges with semantic colors and enforcing that each cluster has a single color; the CCC LP has a strict integrality gap of at least 2.11, with the best known rounding at 2.15. The authors identify cross-edge chromatic interference as the core difficulty and derive a Global Integrality Gap Decomposition Theorem: the gap of any color-independent CCC rounding equals the standard CC gap plus an irreducible chromatic penalty Delta(L)>0. They solve the related min–max to obtain a staircase Delta(L) = ((L-1)/L) Delta_infinity with Delta_infinity ≈ 0.0734, giving a two-color gap of 2.0967. They then propose Color-Coupled Correlation Clustering (C4) with global constraint sum_c x_uv^c ≥ L-1 and a correlated interval-packing rounding scheme to recover the 2.06 approximation and bypass the uncoupled LP bound. Experiments validate the theory and show empirical LP gaps follow the staircase, with C4 matching unconstrained approximations under fairness constraints.
AI.motivation: Understand why CCC is strictly harder than standard CC on complete graphs, quantify the source of difficulty, and improve approximations via new formulations and algorithms that respect color coupling and fairness.
AI.method: Theoretical: (i) Global Integrality Gap Decomposition Theorem for color-independent CCC rounding; (ii) solve the associated min–max to derive a staircase formula Delta(L) = ((L-1)/L) Delta_infinity; (iii) design Color-Coupled Correlation Clustering (C4) with a global constraint and a correlated interval-packing rounding scheme; (iv) empirical validation on extremal instances, real multi-relational networks, and fairness benchmarks.
AI.result: Delta_infinity ≈ 0.0734; Delta(L) = ((L-1)/L) Delta_infinity; two-color CCC gap ≈ 2.0967; C4 recovers the optimal 2.06-approximation and bypasses the 2.11 bound for the uncoupled LP; empirical results show the staircase pattern in LP gaps and C4 aligns with unconstrained fairness-optimized performance.
AI.conclusion: Chromatic interference from neutral edges is the irreducible source of extra cost in color-independent CCC rounding. The Global Integrality Gap Decomposition Theorem, together with the C4 framework, isolates and bypasses this penalty, achieving near-optimal approximations and validating the theory with experiments.

title: Splats in Splats++: Robust and Generalizable 3D Gaussian Splatting Steganography
summary: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has recently redefined the paradigm of 3D reconstruction, striking an unprecedented balance between visual fidelity and computational efficiency. As its adoption proliferates, safeguarding the copyright of explicit 3DGS assets has become paramount. However, existing invisible message embedding frameworks struggle to reconcile secure and high-capacity data embedding with intrinsic asset utility, often disrupting the native rendering pipeline or exhibiting vulnerability to structural perturbations. In this work, we present \textbf{\textit{Splats in Splats++}}, a unified and pipeline-agnostic steganography framework that seamlessly embeds high-capacity 3D/4D content directly within the native 3DGS representation. Grounded in a principled analysis of the frequency distribution of Spherical Harmonics (SH), we propose an importance-graded SH coefficient encryption scheme that achieves imperceptible embedding without compromising the original expressive power. To fundamentally resolve the geometric ambiguities that lead to message leakage, we introduce a \textbf{Hash-Grid Guided Opacity Mapping} mechanism. Coupled with a novel \textbf{Gradient-Gated Opacity Consistency Loss}, our formulation enforces a stringent spatial-attribute coupling between the original and hidden scenes, effectively projecting the discrete attribute mapping into a continuous, attack-resilient latent manifold. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method substantially outperforms existing approaches, achieving up to \textbf{6.28 db} higher message fidelity, \textbf{3$\times$} faster rendering, and exceptional robustness against aggressive 3D-targeted structural attacks (e.g., GSPure). Furthermore, our framework exhibits remarkable versatility, generalizing seamlessly to 2D image embedding, 4D dynamic scene steganography, and diverse downstream tasks.

AI.tldr: Unified pipeline-agnostic steganography for 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) that embeds high-capacity 3D/4D content directly into the native 3DGS representation while preserving rendering quality and robustness.
AI.motivation: Protect copyright and asset utility of explicit 3DGS assets by enabling high-capacity, secure data embedding without disrupting the rendering pipeline or becoming fragile to structural perturbations.
AI.method: Frequency-aware approach: analyze SH coefficient importance and apply an importance-graded encryption to SH coefficients. Introduce Hash-Grid Guided Opacity Mapping to resolve geometric ambiguities, and a Gradient-Gated Opacity Consistency Loss to enforce a tight spatial-attribute coupling between original and hidden scenes, projecting the mapping into a robust latent manifold. The embedding is pipeline-agnostic and operates directly within the 3DGS representation, with extensions to 2D/4D data.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows up to 6.28 dB higher message fidelity, ~3× faster rendering, and strong robustness against 3D-targeted attacks (e.g., GSPure). Demonstrates generalization to 2D embedding, 4D dynamics, and various downstream tasks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Splats in Splats++ offers a unified, secure, and high-capacity steganography framework for 3DGS that preserves asset expressivity, accelerates rendering, and extends to broader modalities and tasks.

title: Collective Kernel EFT for Pre-activation ResNets
summary: In finite-width deep neural networks, the empirical kernel $G$ evolves stochastically across layers. We develop a collective kernel effective field theory (EFT) for pre-activation ResNets based on a $G$-only closure hierarchy and diagnose its finite validity window. Exploiting the exact conditional Gaussianity of residual increments, we derive an exact stochastic recursion for $G$. Applying Gaussian approximations systematically yields a continuous-depth ODE system for the mean kernel $K_0$, the kernel covariance $V_4$, and the $1/n$ mean correction $K_{1,\mathrm{EFT}}$, which emerges diagrammatically as a one-loop tadpole correction. Numerically, $K_0$ remains accurate at all depths. However, the $V_4$ equation residual accumulates to an $O(1)$ error at finite time, primarily driven by approximation errors in the $G$-only transport term. Furthermore, $K_{1,\mathrm{EFT}}$ fails due to the breakdown of the source closure, which exhibits a systematic mismatch even at initialization. These findings highlight the limitations of $G$-only state-space reduction and suggest extending the state space to incorporate the sigma-kernel.

AI.tldr: G-only collective kernel EFT for finite-width ResNets yields an exact stochastic recursion for G and a continuous-depth ODE system for K0, V4, and K1,EFT; K0 is accurate, but V4 accumulates O(1) error and K1,EFT fails due to source-closure breakdown; suggests extending state space to include the sigma-kernel.
AI.motivation: To understand the finite-width kernel dynamics in neural networks, especially the validity of a G-only state-space reduction, and to quantify finite-depth/width corrections via an EFT framework.
AI.method: Derive an exact stochastic recursion for G using the conditional Gaussianity of residual increments; apply Gaussian closures to obtain a continuous-depth ODE system for the mean kernel K0, kernel covariance V4, and the 1/n mean correction K1,EFT (interpreted as a one-loop tadpole). Evaluate numerically and diagnose the limits of the G-only closure, identifying where the approximation breaks down and why.
AI.result: K0 remains accurate across depths; V4 residual grows to O(1) error at finite time due to transport-term approximation errors; K1,EFT fails from breakdown of the source closure with systematic mismatch at initialization; overall, the G-only closure has finite validity window and its extension via the sigma-kernel is recommended.
AI.conclusion: G-only state-space reduction has limited applicability in finite-width ResNets; to improve fidelity, extend the state space to include the sigma-kernel, enabling more accurate higher-order corrections within the EFT framework.

title: UniEditBench: A Unified and Cost-Effective Benchmark for Image and Video Editing via Distilled MLLMs
summary: The evaluation of visual editing models remains fragmented across methods and modalities. Existing benchmarks are often tailored to specific paradigms, making fair cross-paradigm comparisons difficult, while video editing lacks reliable evaluation benchmarks. Furthermore, common automatic metrics often misalign with human preference, yet directly deploying large multimodal models (MLLMs) as evaluators incurs prohibitive computational and financial costs. We present UniEditBench, a unified benchmark for image and video editing that supports reconstruction-based and instruction-driven methods under a shared protocol. UniEditBench includes a structured taxonomy of nine image operations (Add, Remove, Replace, Change, Stroke-based, Extract, Adjust, Count, Reorder) and eight video operations, with coverage of challenging compositional tasks such as counting and spatial reordering. To enable scalable evaluation, we distill a high-capacity MLLM judge (Qwen3-VL-235B-A22B Instruct) into lightweight 4B/8B evaluators that provide multi-dimensional scoring over structural fidelity, text alignment, background consistency, naturalness, and temporal-spatial consistency (for videos). Experiments show that the distilled evaluators maintain strong agreement with human judgments and substantially reduce deployment cost relative to the teacher model. UniEditBench provides a practical and reproducible protocol for benchmarking modern visual editing methods. Our benchmark and the associated reward models are publicly available at https://github.com/wesar1/UniEditBench.

AI.tldr: UniEditBench provides a unified, scalable benchmark for image and video editing with a distilled multi-dimensional evaluator, enabling fair cross-paradigm comparisons and aligning with human judgments at reduced cost.
AI.motivation: Addresses fragmentation in evaluation across image/video editing literature, lack of cross-paradigm fair benchmarks, absence of reliable video-editing benchmarks, misalignment of automatic metrics with human preferences, and prohibitive costs of using large multimodal evaluators.
AI.method: Introduces UniEditBench with a nine-operation image taxonomy and eight video operations under a shared protocol; distills a high-capacity MLLM judge (Qwen3-VL-235B-A22B Instruct) into lightweight 4B/8B evaluators that score across structural fidelity, text alignment, background consistency, naturalness, and temporal-spatial consistency; validates via experiments showing alignment with human judgments and cost reductions.
AI.result: The distilled evaluators retain strong agreement with human judgments while substantially reducing deployment cost compared to the teacher model; provides publicly available benchmark and evaluation models with a reproducible protocol.
AI.conclusion: UniEditBench is a practical, scalable, and reproducible benchmark for modern visual editing methods, enabling fair cross-paradigm evaluation and closer alignment with human preferences at lower cost.

title: DepCap: Adaptive Block-Wise Parallel Decoding for Efficient Diffusion LM Inference
summary: Diffusion language models (DLMs) have emerged as a promising alternative to autoregressive language generation due to their potential for parallel decoding and global refinement of the entire sequence. To unlock this potential, DLM inference must carefully balance generation quality and decoding speed. Recent block-wise DLM decoding methods improve this trade-off by performing diffusion-based decoding sequentially in blocks. However, existing methods typically rely on fixed block schedules or current-step local signals to determine block boundaries, and use conservative confidence-based parallel decoding to avoid conflicts, limiting the quality-speed trade-off. In this paper, we argue that block-wise DLM inference requires more suitable signals for its two core decisions: cross-step signals for determining block boundaries, and token-level conflict signals for parallel decoding. Based on this view, we propose DepCap, a training-free framework for efficient block-wise DLM inference. Specifically, DepCap instantiates the cross-step signal as the influence of the last decoded block and uses it to adaptively determine how far the next block should extend, while identifying a conflict-free subset of tokens for safe parallel decoding within each block, enabling substantial inference acceleration with negligible quality degradation. DepCap is a plug-and-play method applicable to various DLMs, and compatible with existing KV-cache strategies for block-wise DLM. An information-theoretic analysis further suggests that the cumulative last-block influence on a candidate block is approximately additive across tokens, supporting the proposed block-partitioning criterion. Experimental results show that DepCap achieves favorable speed-quality trade-offs across multiple DLM backbones and reasoning and coding benchmarks, with up to 5.63$\times$ speedup without significant performance degradation.

AI.tldr: DepCap is a training-free framework for efficient block-wise diffusion language model inference that uses cross-block influence to adapt block size and a conflict-free token subset for parallel decoding, achieving up to 5.63x speedup with negligible quality loss.
AI.motivation: Current block-wise diffusion-lm decoding relies on fixed block schedules or local signals for block boundaries and conservative parallel decoding to avoid conflicts, which limits the speed-quality trade-off. A better cross-step signal and token-level conflict signal can unlock larger speedups.
AI.method: DepCap uses the influence of the last decoded block as the cross-step signal to adaptively determine the next block length, and identifies a conflict-free subset of tokens within each block for safe parallel decoding; it is training-free, plug-and-play, and compatible with KV-cache strategies; an information-theoretic analysis suggests additive cumulative influence across tokens.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across multiple DLM backbones and benchmarks shows favorable speed-quality trade-offs, with up to 5.63x speedup and negligible degradation.
AI.conclusion: DepCap provides a practical, training-free approach to block-wise diffusion decoding that improves speed without sacrificing quality, and can be integrated with existing DLMs and KV-cache mechanisms.

title: Multi-objective Reinforcement Learning With Augmented States Requires Rewards After Deployment
summary: This research note identifies a previously overlooked distinction between multi-objective reinforcement learning (MORL), and more conventional single-objective reinforcement learning (RL). It has previously been noted that the optimal policy for an MORL agent with a non-linear utility function is required to be conditioned on both the current environmental state and on some measure of the previously accrued reward. This is generally implemented by concatenating the observed state of the environment with the discounted sum of previous rewards to create an augmented state. While augmented states have been widely-used in the MORL literature, one implication of their use has not previously been reported -- namely that they require the agent to have continued access to the reward signal (or a proxy thereof) after deployment, even if no further learning is required. This note explains why this is the case, and considers the practical repercussions of this requirement.

AI.tldr: MORL with non-linear utility requires augmented states that include the discounted sum of past rewards, creating a deployment-time dependency on the reward signal; this practical implication has been previously overlooked.
AI.motivation: Highlight an overlooked distinction between MORL and single-objective RL and uncover a non-obvious deployment-related constraint of augmented states.
AI.method: Conceptual/analytic note explaining the use of augmented state (state concatenated with the discounted sum of past rewards) in MORL and deriving its deployment consequences.
AI.result: Identification of a previously unreported implication: agents with augmented states must retain access to reward signals after deployment, affecting practicality and design choices.
AI.conclusion: The deployment constraint raises practical considerations for MORL systems and may motivate alternative representations or methods to avoid reliance on ongoing reward signals post-deployment.

title: PolarMAE: Efficient Fetal Ultrasound Pre-training via Semantic Screening and Polar-Guided Masking
summary: Intelligent fetal ultrasound (US) interpretation is crucial for prenatal diagnosis, but high annotation costs and operator-induced variance make unsupervised pre-training a highly promising paradigm. However, existing pre-training methods largely ignore US-specific characteristics -- severe data redundancy, fan-shaped locality, and polar coordinate beamforming -- limiting their effectiveness in downstream tasks. To address this, we propose PolarMAE, a novel and efficient pre-training framework tailored for US images. Specifically, to mitigate continuous scanning redundancy, we introduce a Progressive Visual-Semantic Screening (PVSS) that adaptively extracts high-value samples, significantly boosting pre-training efficiency. Furthermore, we design an Acoustic-Bounded Region Constraint (ABRC) to accommodate US locality, forcing the model to focus strictly on valid acoustic regions rather than invalid dark backgrounds. Finally, leveraging the beamforming prior and local details, we propose a Polar-Texture Collaborative Masking (PTCM), enabling the model to capture underlying radial imaging patterns and critical tissue structures. Extensive experiments across diverse datasets and downstream interpretation tasks demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance with strong pre-training scalability and efficiency.

AI.tldr: PolarMAE introduces a domain-tailored self-supervised pre-training framework for fetal ultrasound that addresses US-specific challenges through (1) Progressive Visual-Semantic Screening (PVSS) for data efficiency, (2) Acoustic-Bounded Region Constraint (ABRC) to focus on valid acoustic regions, and (3) Polar-Texture Collaborative Masking (PTCM) to exploit polar/radial imaging characteristics. It achieves state-of-the-art results with scalable pre-training efficiency across diverse datasets and tasks.
AI.motivation: Ultrasound data exhibit severe redundancy from continuous scanning and operator-induced variability. Existing pre-training methods largely ignore US-specific properties such as fan-shaped locality and polar coordinate beamforming, leading to limited transfer to downstream interpretation tasks. A pre-training framework that respects ultrasound physics and data characteristics could improve efficiency and performance.
AI.method: PVSS progressively selects high-value samples during pre-training to reduce redundancy and speed up convergence. ABRC constrains the model’s focus to valid acoustic regions, mitigating learning from dark background areas. PTCM leverages beamforming priors and local texture cues to capture polar imaging patterns, enabling robust reconstruction/masking that respects radial tissue structures.
AI.result: Reported experiments on multiple ultrasound datasets and downstream tasks show state-of-the-art performance and strong scalability/efficiency for pre-training, with gains attributed to PVSS sampling efficiency, ABRC region focus, and PTCM’s exploitation of polar texture.
AI.conclusion: PolarMAE presents a targeted, efficient pre-training paradigm for fetal ultrasound that aligns with ultrasound physics and data characteristics, delivering improved performance and scalability over existing unsupervised pre-training methods.

title: Zero-Shot Scalable Resilience in UAV Swarms: A Decentralized Imitation Learning Framework with Physics-Informed Graph Interactions
summary: Large-scale Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) failures can split an unmanned aerial vehicle swarm network into disconnected sub-networks, making decentralized recovery both urgent and difficult. Centralized recovery methods depend on global topology information and become communication-heavy after severe fragmentation. Decentralized heuristics and multi-agent reinforcement learning methods are easier to deploy, but their performance often degrades when the swarm scale and damage severity vary. We present Physics-informed Graph Adversarial Imitation Learning algorithm (PhyGAIL) that adopts centralized training with decentralized execution. PhyGAIL builds bounded local interaction graphs from heterogeneous observations, and uses physics-informed graph neural network to encode directional local interactions as gated message passing with explicit attraction and repulsion. This gives the policy a physically grounded coordination bias while keeping local observations scale-invariant. It also uses scenario-adaptive imitation learning to improve training under fragmented topologies and variable-length recovery episodes. Our analysis establishes bounded local graph amplification, bounded interaction dynamics, and controlled variance of the terminal success signal. A policy trained on 20-UAV swarms transfers directly to swarms of up to 500 UAVs without fine-tuning, and achieves better performance across reconnection reliability, recovery speed, motion safety, and runtime efficiency than representative baselines.

AI.tldr: PhyGAIL uses physics-informed graph neural networks and scenario-adaptive imitation learning to enable scalable, decentralized recovery of fragmented UAV swarms with centralized training.
AI.motivation: Large-scale UAV failures can fragment swarm networks, making centralized recovery costly and decentralized methods less reliable as swarm size and damage vary.
AI.method: Centralized training with decentralized execution; constructs bounded local interaction graphs from heterogeneous observations; uses a physics-informed GNN with gated message passing to encode directional local interactions via attraction/repulsion; scenario-adaptive imitation learning to handle fragmented topologies and variable-length recovery episodes; analyzes bounded amplification, bounded dynamics, and variance of terminal signals.
AI.result: Policy trained on 20-UAV swarms transfers to swarms up to 500 UAVs without fine-tuning and achieves better reconnection reliability, recovery speed, motion safety, and runtime efficiency than baselines.
AI.conclusion: PhyGAIL provides a physically grounded coordination bias and scale-invariant local observations, enabling scalable, robust recovery under fragmentation with transferable policies across swarm sizes.

title: AeroDeshadow: Physics-Guided Shadow Synthesis and Penumbra-Aware Deshadowing for Aerospace Imagery
summary: Shadows are prevalent in high-resolution aerospace imagery (ASI). They often cause spectral distortion and information loss, which degrade downstream interpretation tasks. While deep learning methods have advanced natural-image shadow removal, their direct application to ASI faces two primary challenges. First, strictly paired training data are severely lacking. Second, homogeneous shadow assumptions fail to handle the broad penumbra transition zones inherent in aerospace scenes. To address these issues, we propose AeroDeshadow, a unified two-stage framework integrating physics-guided shadow synthesis and penumbra-aware restoration. In the first stage, a Physics-aware Degradation Shadow Synthesis Network (PDSS-Net) explicitly models illumination decay and spatial attenuation. This process constructs AeroDS-Syn, a large-scale paired dataset featuring soft boundary transitions. Constrained by this physical formulation, a Penumbra-aware Cascaded DeShadowing Network (PCDS-Net) then decouples the input into umbra and penumbra components. By restoring these regions progressively, PCDS-Net alleviates boundary artifacts and over-correction. Trained solely on the synthetic AeroDS-Syn, the network generalizes to real-world ASI without requiring paired real annotations. Experimental results indicate that AeroDeshadow achieves state-of-the-art quantitative accuracy and visual fidelity across synthetic and real-world datasets. The datasets and code will be made publicly available at: https://github.com/AeroVILab-AHU/AeroDeshadow.

AI.tldr: A two-stage, physics-guided deshadowing framework for aerospace imagery that uses synthetic data to overcome lack of paired data and handle soft shadow boundaries, achieving state-of-the-art results and generalizing to real imagery.
AI.motivation: Shadow distortions and information loss impede high-resolution aerospace imagery analysis. Challenges include scarce strictly paired data and broad penumbra transitions that violate homogeneous shadow assumptions.
AI.method: Stage 1 PDSS-Net synthesize physics-aware shadow degradation to create AeroDS-Syn with soft boundaries. Stage 2 PCDS-Net a penumbra-aware cascaded network decouples input into umbra/penumbra and restores progressively. Trained solely on AeroDS-Syn; generalizes to real ASI without real paired annotations.
AI.result: AeroDeshadow achieves state-of-the-art quantitative accuracy and visual fidelity on both synthetic AeroDS-Syn-derived tasks and real-world aerospace imagery. Code and dataset to be released.
AI.conclusion: The framework effectively handles penumbra and boundary artifacts, and its synthetic-to-real training strategy enables robust generalization to real ASI without paired data.

title: When Do Early-Exit Networks Generalize? A PAC-Bayesian Theory of Adaptive Depth
summary: Early-exit neural networks enable adaptive computation by allowing confident predictions to exit at intermediate layers, achieving 2-8$\times$ inference speedup. Despite widespread deployment, their generalization properties lack theoretical understanding -- a gap explicitly identified in recent surveys. This paper establishes a unified PAC-Bayesian framework for adaptive-depth networks. (1) Novel Entropy-Based Bounds: We prove the first generalization bounds depending on exit-depth entropy $H(D)$ and expected depth $\mathbb{E}[D]$ rather than maximum depth $K$, with sample complexity $\mathcal{O}((\mathbb{E}[D] \cdot d + H(D))/ε^2)$. (2) Explicit Constructive Constants: Our analysis yields the leading coefficient $\sqrt{2\ln 2} \approx 1.177$ with complete derivation. (3) Provable Early-Exit Advantages: We establish sufficient conditions under which adaptive-depth networks strictly outperform fixed-depth counterparts. (4) Extension to Approximate Label Independence: We relax the label-independence assumption to $ε$-approximate policies, broadening applicability to learned routing. (5) Comprehensive Validation: Experiments across 6 architectures on 7 benchmarks demonstrate tightness ratios of 1.52-3.87$\times$ (all $p < 0.001$) versus $>$100$\times$ for classical bounds. Bound-guided threshold selection matches validation-tuned performance within 0.1-0.3%.

AI.tldr: Presents PAC-Bayesian generalization bounds for adaptive-depth (early-exit) networks based on exit-depth entropy H(D) and expected depth E[D], with explicit constants and empirical validation; demonstrates conditions for advantages over fixed-depth nets and extends to ε-approximate routing, supported by experiments.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of theoretical understanding of generalization in early-exit networks and provide a unified framework connecting exit decisions to PAC-Bayes bounds.
AI.method: Develop a unified PAC-Bayesian analysis for adaptive-depth networks, derive entropy-based generalization bounds depending on H(D) and E[D] rather than maximum depth, compute explicit constants, show conditions for improvement over fixed-depth, extend to ε-approximate label-independence, and validate on 6 architectures across 7 benchmarks.
AI.result: First generalization bounds based on exit-depth entropy and expected depth; leading coefficient explicitly derived as sqrt(2 ln 2) ≈ 1.177; sufficient conditions for early-exit advantages; ε-approximate policy extension; empirical results show tight bound ratios (1.52–3.87×) versus classical bounds and bound-guided thresholding matches validation performance within 0.1–0.3%.
AI.conclusion: Provides a theoretically grounded, practically validated PAC-Bayesian framework for adaptive-depth networks, enabling principled analysis of exit strategies and offering concrete constants and empirical guidance for bound-based thresholding.

title: Efficient Video Diffusion Models: Advancements and Challenges
summary: Video diffusion models have rapidly become the dominant paradigm for high-fidelity generative video synthesis, but their practical deployment remains constrained by severe inference costs. Compared with image generation, video synthesis compounds computation across spatial-temporal token growth and iterative denoising, making attention and memory traffic major bottlenecks in real-world settings. This survey provides a systematic and deployment-oriented review of efficient video diffusion models. We propose a unified categorization that organizes existing methods into four classes of main paradigms, including step distillation, efficient attention, model compression, and cache/trajectory optimization. Building on this categorization, we respectively analyze algorithmic trends of these four paradigms and examine how different design choices target two core objectives: reducing the number of function evaluations and minimizing per-step overhead. Finally, we discuss open challenges and future directions, including quality preservation under composite acceleration, hardware-software co-design, robust real-time long-horizon generation, and open infrastructure for standardized evaluation. To the best of our knowledge, our work is the first comprehensive survey on efficient video diffusion models, offering researchers and engineers a structured overview of the field and its emerging research directions.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive survey of efficient video diffusion models, proposing a four-class taxonomy and deployment-focused analysis.
AI.motivation: Video diffusion models deliver high-fidelity generative video but incur very high inference costs due to spatiotemporal token growth and iterative denoising, hindering practical deployment.
AI.method: Systematic survey that unifies and categorizes existing methods into four paradigms—step distillation, efficient attention, model compression, and cache/trajectory optimization—and analyzes algorithmic trends and design choices impacting two core objectives: reducing function evaluations and minimizing per-step overhead.
AI.result: Provides a deployment-oriented framework with a four-paradigm taxonomy, analyzes trends within each paradigm, and outlines challenges and directions for efficient video diffusion: composite acceleration, hardware-software co-design, robust real-time long-horizon generation, and standardized evaluation infrastructure.
AI.conclusion: The work represents the first comprehensive survey on efficient video diffusion models and offers researchers and engineers a structured overview of the field and its emerging directions.

title: Closing the Theory-Practice Gap in Spiking Transformers via Effective Dimension
summary: Spiking transformers achieve competitive accuracy with conventional transformers while offering $38$-$57\times$ energy efficiency on neuromorphic hardware, yet no theoretical framework guides their design. This paper establishes the first comprehensive expressivity theory for spiking self-attention. We prove that spiking attention with Leaky Integrate-and-Fire neurons is a universal approximator of continuous permutation-equivariant functions, providing explicit spike circuit constructions including a novel lateral inhibition network for softmax normalization with proven $O(1/\sqrt{T})$ convergence. We derive tight spike-count lower bounds via rate-distortion theory: $\varepsilon$-approximation requires $Ω(L_f^2 nd/\varepsilon^2)$ spikes, with rigorous information-theoretic derivation. Our key insight is input-dependent bounds using measured effective dimensions ($d_{\text{eff}}=47$--$89$ for CIFAR/ImageNet), explaining why $T=4$ timesteps suffice despite worst-case $T \geq 10{,}000$ predictions. We provide concrete design rules with calibrated constants ($C=2.3$, 95\% CI: $[1.9, 2.7]$). Experiments on Spikformer, QKFormer, and SpikingResformer across vision and language benchmarks validate predictions with $R^2=0.97$ ($p<0.001$). Our framework provides the first principled foundation for neuromorphic transformer design.

AI.tldr: Spiking self-attention with Leaky Integrate-and-Fire neurons is a universal approximator for continuous permutation-equivariant functions, with explicit spike-based constructions, convergence guarantees for a softmax via lateral inhibition, and tight spike-count lower bounds. It introduces input-dependent, empirically measured effective dimensions, and offers actionable design rules, validated on vision and language benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To provide a principled theoretical framework for spiking transformers to guide design and explain energy-efficient performance on neuromorphic hardware, addressing the lack of expressivity and convergence theory.
AI.method: Proves that spiking attention with LIF neurons universal approximates continuous permutation-equivariant functions. Develops explicit spike circuit constructions, including a lateral-inhibition network for softmax normalization with O(1/√T) convergence. Derives rate-distortion-based lower bounds on spike counts: ε-approximation requires Ω(Lf^2 n d / ε^2) spikes. Introduces input-dependent bounds using measured effective dimensions (d_eff ≈ 47–89 for CIFAR/ImageNet). Provides calibrated design rules with constants (C ≈ 2.3, 95% CI [1.9, 2.7]). Empirically validates predictions with three models (Spikformer, QKFormer, SpikingResformer) across vision and language tasks.
AI.result: The framework proves universality of spiking attention with LIF neurons, provides a softmax normalization construction with proven convergence, and establishes tight spike-count lower bounds. It shows input-dependent bounds via effective dimensionality, explaining small sequence steps needed in practice. Calibrated constants enable practical design. Experimental validation yields R^2 = 0.97 (p < 0.001) across benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Presents the first principled foundation for neuromorphic transformer design, linking expressivity, circuit construction, information-theoretic limits, and empirical validation, and offering concrete design guidelines.

title: Making Image Editing Easier via Adaptive Task Reformulation with Agentic Executions
summary: Instruction guided image editing has advanced substantially with recent generative models, yet it still fails to produce reliable results across many seemingly simple cases. We observe that a large portion of these failures stem not from insufficient model capacity, but from poorly formulated editing tasks, such as those involving small targets, implicit spatial relations, or under-specified instructions. In this work, we frame image editing failures as a task formulation problem and propose an adaptive task reformulation framework that improves editing performance without modifying the underlying model. Our key idea is to transform the original image-instruction pair into a sequence of operations that are dynamically determined and executed by a MLLM agent through analysis, routing, reformulation, and feedback-driven refinement. Experiments on multiple benchmarks, including ImgEdit, PICA, and RePlan, across diverse editing backbones such as Qwen Image Edit and Nano Banana, show consistent improvements, with especially large gains on challenging cases. These results suggest that task reformulation is a critical but underexplored factor, and that substantial gains can be achieved by better matching editing tasks to the effective operating regime of existing models.

AI.tldr: Adaptive task reformulation for image editing: an MLLM-driven agent decomposes instructions into a dynamically selected sequence of operations, improving editing performance across benchmarks without altering base models.
AI.motivation: Editing failures are often due to poorly formulated tasks (e.g., small targets, implicit spatial relations, under-specified instructions) rather than limited model capacity; reformulating tasks can unlock better performance.
AI.method: An adaptive pipeline where an MLLM agent analyzes, routes, reformulates, and uses feedback to produce a sequence of operations from the image-instruction pair. This sequence is executed by existing editing backbones (e.g., Qwen Image Edit, Nano Banana) without modifying the underlying models. Evaluations span ImgEdit, PICA, and RePlan datasets.
AI.result: Consistent performance gains across diverse editing backbones and datasets, with especially large improvements on challenging cases.
AI.conclusion: Task reformulation is a critical, underexplored lever for improving instruction-guided image editing; aligning task formulation with the model’s operating regime yields substantial gains without changing the models themselves.

title: Federated Learning with Quantum Enhanced LSTM for Applications in High Energy Physics
comment: 8 pages, 7 figures, accepted at IEEE WCCI, 2026
summary: Learning with large-scale datasets and information-critical applications, such as in High Energy Physics (HEP), demands highly complex, large-scale models that are both robust and accurate. To tackle this issue and cater to the learning requirements, we envision using a federated learning framework with a quantum-enhanced model. Specifically, we design a hybrid quantum-classical long-shot-term-memory model (QLSTM) for local training at distributed nodes. It combines the representative power of quantum models in understanding complex relationships within the feature space, and an LSTM-based model to learn necessary correlations across data points. Given the computing limitations and unprecedented cost of current stand-alone noisy-intermediate quantum (NISQ) devices, we propose to use a federated learning setup, where the learning load can be distributed to local servers as per design and data availability. We demonstrate the benefits of such a design on a classification task for the Supersymmetry(SUSY) dataset, having 5M rows. Our experiments indicate that the performance of this design is not only better that some of the existing work using variational quantum circuit (VQC) based quantum machine learning (QML) techniques, but is also comparable ($Δ\sim \pm 1\%$) to that of classical deep-learning benchmarks. An important observation from this study is that the designed framework has $<$300 parameters and only needs 20K data points to give a comparable performance. Which also turns out to be a 100$\times$ improvement than the compared baseline models. This shows an improved learning capability of the proposed framework with minimal data and resource requirements, due to the joint model with an LSTM based architecture and a quantum enhanced VQC.

AI.tldr: Hybrid quantum-classical federated learning with a QLSTM shows competitive accuracy on a SUSY dataset with very small parameter count and low data requirements, leveraging distributed training to reduce resource needs.
AI.motivation: Address the need for robust, scalable models on large-scale, information-critical datasets (e.g., High Energy Physics) while contending with the limitations of NISQ devices. Federated learning distributes computation and data-local training to mitigate hardware costs and data access constraints.
AI.method: Proposes a hybrid quantum-classical long-shot-term-memory (QLSTM) model for local training at distributed nodes within a federated learning framework. The quantum component (VQC) captures complex feature relationships; the LSTM handles cross-sample correlations. Evaluation on a 5M-row SUSY dataset; comparison against VQC-based QML baselines and classical deep-learning models; reports parameter count and data efficiency.
AI.result: The approach outperforms several existing VQC-based QML methods and achieves performance comparable to classical deep-learning benchmarks within roughly ±1%. The designed model contains fewer than 300 parameters and reaches comparable performance with only ~20K data points, translating to about a 100× improvement over baseline models.
AI.conclusion: Federated hybrid quantum-classical modeling (QLSTM) is a promising, data-efficient approach for large-scale, information-critical tasks in HEP. It combines quantum-enhanced representation power with sequence modeling to enable competitive performance at low resource cost; further validation on hardware, broader datasets, and analysis of statistical significance would strengthen the claims.

title: Neural Gabor Splatting: Enhanced Gaussian Splatting with Neural Gabor for High-frequency Surface Reconstruction
summary: Recent years have witnessed the rapid emergence of 3D Gaussian splatting (3DGS) as a powerful approach for 3D reconstruction and novel view synthesis. Its explicit representation with Gaussian primitives enables fast training, real-time rendering, and convenient post-processing such as editing and surface reconstruction. However, 3DGS suffers from a critical drawback: the number of primitives grows drastically for scenes with high-frequency appearance details, since each primitive can represent only a single color, requiring multiple primitives for every sharp color transition. To overcome this limitation, we propose neural Gabor splatting, which augments each Gaussian primitive with a lightweight multi-layer perceptron that models a wide range of color variations within a single primitive. To further control primitive numbers, we introduce a frequency-aware densification strategy that selects mismatch primitives for pruning and cloning based on frequency energy. Our method achieves accurate reconstruction of challenging high-frequency surfaces. We demonstrate its effectiveness through extensive experiments on both standard benchmarks, such as Mip-NeRF360 and High-Frequency datasets (e.g., checkered patterns), supported by comprehensive ablation studies.

AI.tldr: Neural Gabor splatting extends 3D Gaussian splatting by adding a light MLP per primitive to model intra-primitive color variation and uses a frequency-aware densification strategy to prune/clone based on frequency energy, enabling better high-frequency surface reconstruction.
