AI.motivation: 3D Gaussian splatting suffers from high primitive counts for high-frequency details because a single primitive encodes only a single color; need an efficient representation to capture sharp color transitions without exploding primitive count.
AI.method: Introduce a lightweight MLP per Gaussian primitive (neural Gabor) to model color variations within the primitive; implement a frequency-aware densification strategy that prunes/clones primitives based on their frequency energy to control primitive numbers.
AI.result: Achieves accurate reconstruction of challenging high-frequency surfaces; validated on standard benchmarks such as Mip-NeRF360 and High-Frequency datasets (e.g., checkered patterns) with ablation studies.
AI.conclusion: The approach improves 3D Gaussian splatting by enabling intra-primitive color modeling and controlled primitive counts, yielding better high-frequency reconstruction and robustness across benchmarks.

title: Pruning Unsafe Tickets: A Resource-Efficient Framework for Safer and More Robust LLMs
summary: Machine learning models are increasingly deployed in real-world applications, but even aligned models such as Mistral and LLaVA still exhibit unsafe behaviors inherited from pre-training. Current alignment methods like SFT and RLHF primarily encourage models to generate preferred responses, but do not explicitly remove the unsafe subnetworks that trigger harmful outputs. In this work, we introduce a resource-efficient pruning framework that directly identifies and removes parameters associated with unsafe behaviors while preserving model utility. Our method employs a gradient-free attribution mechanism, requiring only modest GPU resources, and generalizes across architectures and quantized variants. Empirical evaluations on ML models show substantial reductions in unsafe generations and improved robustness against jailbreak attacks, with minimal utility loss. From the perspective of the Lottery Ticket Hypothesis, our results suggest that ML models contain "unsafe tickets" responsible for harmful behaviors, and pruning reveals "safety tickets" that maintain performance while aligning outputs. This provides a lightweight, post-hoc alignment strategy suitable for deployment in resource-constrained settings.

AI.tldr: Prunes unsafe neural subnetworks after training via a gradient-free attribution method, yielding a light-weight, post-hoc alignment technique that reduces unsafe outputs with minimal utility loss.
AI.motivation: Aligning large language models and multimodal models remains imperfect; existing methods like SFT/RLHF do not explicitly remove unsafe components. A resource-efficient pruning approach targets harmful substrings to improve safety without sacrificing utility, enabling deployment in constrained environments.
AI.method: A gradient-free attribution mechanism identifies parameters tied to unsafe behaviors. The framework prunes these parameters, generalizes across architectures and quantized variants, and requires modest GPU resources. Evaluation includes safety improvements and robustness against jailbreak attacks.
AI.result: Substantial reductions in unsafe generations and enhanced jailbreak robustness with minimal loss in model utility. Supports the Lottery Ticket Hypothesis view that there exist 'unsafe tickets' and removable 'safety tickets' that preserve performance while aligning outputs.
AI.conclusion: The paper offers a lightweight, post-hoc alignment strategy that is practical for resource-constrained deployment and generalizes across models and precisions by removing unsafe subnetworks rather than solely shaping outputs.

title: Fusing Cellular Network Data and Tollbooth Counts for Urban Traffic Flow Estimation
summary: Traffic simulations, essential for planning urban transit infrastructure interventions, require vehicle-category-specific origin-destination (OD) data. Existing data sources are imperfect: sparse tollbooth sensors provide accurate vehicle counts by category, while extensive mobility data from cellular network activity captures aggregated crowd movement, but lack modal disaggregation and have systematic biases. This study develops a machine learning framework to correct and disaggregate cellular network data using sparse tollbooth counts as ground truth. The model uses temporal and spatial features to learn the complex relationship between aggregated mobility data and vehicular data. The framework infers destinations from transit routes and implements routing logic to distribute corrected flows between OD pairs. This approach is applied to a bus depot expansion in Trondheim, Norway, generating hourly OD matrices by vehicle length category. The results show how limited but accurate sensor measurements can correct extensive but aggregated mobility data to produce grounded estimates of background vehicular traffic flows. These macro-scale estimates can be refined for micro-scale analysis at desired locations. The framework provides a generalisable approach for generating origin-destination data from cellular network data. This enables downstream tasks, like detailed traffic simulations for infrastructure planning in data-scarce contexts, supporting urban planners in making informed decisions.

AI.tldr: A machine learning framework combines sparse, accurate tollbooth counts with broad but aggregated cellular mobility data to disaggregate and generate vehicle-category OD matrices for traffic simulations; applied to Trondheim for a bus depot expansion, demonstrating grounding of macro-scale data and enabling scalable planning in data-scarce contexts.
AI.motivation: Urban traffic simulations require vehicle-category OD data, but existing sources are either sparse (toll sensors) or aggregated with bias (cellular mobility). The study addresses data fusion to produce disaggregated OD by vehicle length for planning infrastructure expansions.
AI.method: The approach learns the mapping from aggregated cellular mobility data to vehicle-specific counts using temporal and spatial features, uses ground-truth toll counts as supervision, infers destinations from transit routes, and applies routing logic to allocate flows to OD pairs. The framework is demonstrated on a Trondheim, Norway bus depot expansion, producing hourly OD matrices by vehicle length category.
AI.result: Hourly OD matrices by vehicle length category are generated, showing that sparse but accurate toll data can correct extensive mobility data to yield grounded background traffic flows; the results can be refined to micro-scale locations and are generalizable to other contexts.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a generalizable method for deriving vehicle-category OD data from cellular mobility data, enabling realistic, data-grounded traffic simulations for infrastructure planning in data-scarce environments.

title: From Competition to Coopetition: Coopetitive Training-Free Image Editing Based on Text Guidance
summary: Text-guided image editing, a pivotal task in modern multimedia content creation, has seen remarkable progress with training-free methods that eliminate the need for additional optimization. Despite recent progress, existing methods are typically constrained by a competitive paradigm in which the editing and reconstruction branches are independently driven by their respective objectives to maximize alignment with target and source prompts. The adversarial strategy causes semantic conflicts and unpredictable outcomes due to the lack of coordination between branches. To overcome these issues, we propose Coopetitive Training-Free Image Editing (CoEdit), a novel zero-shot framework that transforms attention control from competition to coopetitive negotiation, achieving editing harmony across spatial and temporal dimensions. Spatially, CoEdit introduces Dual-Entropy Attention Manipulation, which quantifies directional entropic interactions between branches to reformulate attention control as a harmony-maximization problem, eventually improving the localization of editable and preservable regions. Temporally, we present Entropic Latent Refinement mechanism to dynamically adjust latent representations over time, minimizing accumulated editing errors and ensuring consistent semantic transitions throughout the denoising trajectory. Additionally, we propose the Fidelity-Constrained Editing Score, a composite metric that jointly evaluates semantic editing and background fidelity. Extensive experiments on standard benchmarks demonstrate that CoEdit achieves superior performance in both editing quality and structural preservation, enhancing multimedia information utilization by enabling more effective interaction between visual and textual modalities. The code will be available at https://github.com/JinhaoShen/CoEdit.

AI.tldr: CoEdit introduces coopetitive training-free image editing for zero-shot text-guided editing by aligning spatial and temporal attention through Dual-Entropy Attention Manipulation and Entropic Latent Refinement, plus Fidelity-Constrained Editing Score.
AI.motivation: Addresses semantic conflicts between independently optimized editing and reconstruction branches in existing zero-shot methods, which leads to unstable outputs and misalignment between text prompts and edited images.
AI.method: Spatial: Dual-Entropy Attention Manipulation quantifies directional entropic interactions between branches to reformulate attention control as a harmony-maximization problem, improving localization of editable and preservable regions. Temporal: Entropic Latent Refinement dynamically adjusts latent representations over the denoising trajectory to minimize accumulated editing errors and ensure consistent semantic transitions. A Fidelity-Constrained Editing Score is proposed to jointly evaluate semantic editing and background fidelity.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on standard benchmarks show CoEdit achieves superior editing quality and structural preservation, with better localization of editable/preservable regions and stronger cross-modal coherence; code is released at the authors' GitHub.
AI.conclusion: CoEdit converts the editing task from a competitive to a coopetitive framework, achieving spatial-temporal harmony in zero-shot text-guided image editing and offering a new, joint metric for evaluation.

title: Similarity-Based Bike Station Expansion via Hybrid Denoising Autoencoders
summary: Urban bike-sharing systems require strategic station expansion to meet growing demand. Traditional allocation approaches rely on explicit demand modelling that may not capture the urban characteristics distinguishing successful stations. This study addresses the need to exploit patterns from existing stations to inform expansion decisions, particularly in data-constrained environments. We present a data-driven framework leveraging existing stations deemed desirable by operational metrics. A hybrid denoising autoencoder (HDAE) learns compressed latent representations from multi-source grid-level features (socio-demographic, built environment, and transport network), with a supervised classification head regularising the embedding space structure. Expansion candidates are selected via greedy allocation with spatial constraints based on latent-space similarity to existing stations. Evaluation on Trondheim's bike-sharing network demonstrates that HDAE embeddings yield more spatially coherent clusters and allocation patterns than raw features. Sensitivity analyses across similarity methods and distance metrics confirm robustness. A consensus-based procedure across multiple parametrisations distils 32 high-confidence extension zones where all parametrisations agree. The results demonstrate how representation learning captures complex patterns that raw features miss, enabling evidence-based expansion planning without explicit demand modelling. The consensus procedure strengthens recommendations by requiring agreement across parametrisations, while framework configurability allows planners to incorporate operational knowledge. The methodology generalises to any location-allocation problem where existing desirable instances inform the selection of new candidates.

AI.tldr: Data-driven expansion for urban bike-sharing using a hybrid denoising autoencoder to learn latent representations from multi-source grid features; greedy, spatially-constrained candidate selection with a consensus across parameterizations identifies 32 high-confidence extension zones, improving spatial coherence without explicit demand modelling.
AI.motivation: Overcome reliance on explicit demand modelling in expansion planning, by leveraging patterns inferred from existing desirable stations, especially in data-constrained cities, to inform where new stations should be placed.
AI.method: Train a hybrid denoising autoencoder (HDAE) on multi-source grid-level features (socio-demographic, built environment, transport network) with a supervised classification head to regularize the latent space. Use latent-space similarity to existing stations to drive greedy allocation of expansion candidates under spatial constraints. Apply a consensus-based procedure across multiple parametrisations to identify high-confidence extension zones. Evaluate on Trondheim bike-sharing network, with sensitivity analyses across similarity methods and distance metrics.
AI.result: HDAE embeddings yield more spatially coherent clusters and allocation patterns than raw features. Robust to similarity metrics and distance definitions. Identified 32 extension zones with consensus across all parametrisations. Demonstrates that representation learning captures patterns missed by raw features and supports evidence-based expansion without explicit demand modelling.
AI.conclusion: Representation learning provides a powerful alternative for location-allocation planning in bike-sharing, enabling coherent expansion decisions without explicit demand forecasts. The consensus procedure strengthens recommendations by enforcing cross-parametrisation agreement, while the framework is configurable and generalizable to other location-allocation problems that rely on desirable existing instances.

title: MMGait: Towards Multi-Modal Gait Recognition
summary: Gait recognition has emerged as a powerful biometric technique for identifying individuals at a distance without requiring user cooperation. Most existing methods focus primarily on RGB-derived modalities, which fall short in real-world scenarios requiring multi-modal collaboration and cross-modal retrieval. To overcome these challenges, we present MMGait, a comprehensive multi-modal gait benchmark integrating data from five heterogeneous sensors, including an RGB camera, a depth camera, an infrared camera, a LiDAR scanner, and a 4D Radar system. MMGait contains twelve modalities and 334,060 sequences from 725 subjects, enabling systematic exploration across geometric, photometric, and motion domains. Based on MMGait, we conduct extensive evaluations on single-modal, cross-modal, and multi-modal paradigms to analyze modality robustness and complementarity. Furthermore, we introduce a new task, Omni Multi-Modal Gait Recognition, which aims to unify the above three gait recognition paradigms within a single model. We also propose a simple yet powerful baseline, OmniGait, which learns a shared embedding space across diverse modalities and achieves promising recognition performance. The MMGait benchmark, codebase, and pretrained checkpoints are publicly available at https://github.com/BNU-IVC/MMGait.

AI.tldr: Introduces MMGait: a large-scale, multi-modal gait benchmark with 5 sensors (RGB, depth, IR, LiDAR, 4D Radar) across 12 modalities, enabling single-, cross-, and multi-modal gait recognition; proposes OmniGait for unified embedding learning across modalities.
AI.motivation: RGB-dominant gait methods face real-world limitations in cross-modal and retrieval scenarios; a comprehensive benchmark and a unified model are needed to study modality robustness, complementarity, and cross-modal transfer.
AI.method: Construct MMGait with data from five heterogeneous sensors, yielding 12 modalities, 334,060 sequences from 725 subjects. Conduct extensive evaluations on single-, cross-, and multi-modal paradigms. Introduce Omni Multi-Modal Gait Recognition as a unifying task and propose OmniGait, a baseline that learns a shared embedding space across modalities. Release codebase and pretrained checkpoints.
AI.result: Demonstrates modality robustness and complementarity across the six modalities; OmniGait achieves strong performance in cross- and multi-modal settings, validating a unified embedding approach. The dataset and baselines provide a foundation for future multi-modal gait research.
AI.conclusion: MMGait provides a comprehensive benchmark and a unified framework (OmniGait) for multi-modal gait recognition, enabling robust cross-modal retrieval and recognition; public release of data and code will accelerate research in this domain.

title: EVIL: Evolving Interpretable Algorithms for Zero-Shot Inference on Event Sequences and Time Series with LLMs
summary: We introduce EVIL (\textbf{EV}olving \textbf{I}nterpretable algorithms with \textbf{L}LMs), an approach that uses LLM-guided evolutionary search to discover simple, interpretable algorithms for dynamical systems inference. Rather than training neural networks on large datasets, EVIL evolves pure Python/NumPy programs that perform zero-shot, in-context inference across datasets. We apply EVIL to three distinct tasks: next-event prediction in temporal point processes, rate matrix estimation for Markov jump processes, and time series imputation. In each case, a single evolved algorithm generalizes across all evaluation datasets without per-dataset training (analogous to an amortized inference model). To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to show that LLM-guided program evolution can discover a single compact inference function for these dynamical-systems problems. Across the three domains, the discovered algorithms are often competitive with, and even outperform, state-of-the-art deep learning models while being orders of magnitudes faster, and remaining fully interpretable.

AI.tldr: EVIL evolves simple Python/NumPy inference programs guided by LLMs for zero-shot dynamical-system inference, yielding compact, interpretable algorithms that generalize across datasets; competitive with deep learning and orders of magnitude faster.
AI.motivation: Need interpretable, efficient inference algorithms for dynamical systems that generalize without per-dataset training.
AI.method: LLM-guided evolutionary search to discover pure Python/NumPy programs that perform zero-shot, in-context inference across datasets. Applied to three tasks: next-event prediction in temporal point processes, rate-matrix estimation for Markov jump processes, and time-series imputation. A single evolved algorithm generalizes across evaluation datasets (amortized inference).
AI.result: Discovered algorithms are often competitive with, or outperform, state-of-the-art deep learning models, while being orders of magnitude faster and fully interpretable; a single algorithm generalizes across datasets.
AI.conclusion: LLM-guided program evolution can discover compact, interpretable inference functions for dynamical-systems problems, demonstrating first-of-its-kind ability to produce a single, fast, interpretable inference function across multiple tasks.

title: IA-CLAHE: Image-Adaptive Clip Limit Estimation for CLAHE
comment: Accepted to NTIRE 2026 Workshop at CVPR 2026
summary: This paper proposes image-adaptive contrast limited adaptive histogram equalization (IA-CLAHE). Conventional CLAHE is widely used to boost the performance of various computer vision tasks and to improve visual quality for human perception in practical industrial applications. CLAHE applies contrast limited histogram equalization to each local region to enhance local contrast. However, CLAHE often leads to over-enhancement, because the contrast-limiting parameter clip limit is fixed regardless of the histogram distribution of each local region. Our IA-CLAHE addresses this limitation by adaptively estimating tile-wise clip limits from the input image. To achieve this, we train a lightweight clip limits estimator with a differentiable extension of CLAHE, enabling end-to-end optimization. Unlike prior learning-based CLAHE methods, IA-CLAHE does not require pre-searched ground-truth clip limits or task-specific datasets, because it learns to map input image histograms toward a domain-invariant uniform distribution, enabling zero-shot generalization across diverse conditions. Experimental results show that IA-CLAHE consistently improves recognition performance, while simultaneously enhancing visual quality for human perception, without requiring any task-specific training data.

AI.tldr: An image-adaptive CLAHE (IA-CLAHE) adaptively estimates per-tile clip limits to avoid over-enhancement, using a differentiable CLAHE extension for end-to-end training. It claims zero-shot generalization without task-specific data, improving recognition and visual quality.
AI.motivation: CLAHE can over-enhance images when clip limits are fixed per tile; need adaptive, image-driven clip limits to preserve local structure and improve downstream recognition and perceptual quality.
AI.method: Train a lightweight clip-limit estimator within a differentiable CLAHE framework, enabling end-to-end optimization. The estimator maps input histograms toward a domain-invariant uniform distribution, supporting zero-shot generalization across diverse conditions.
AI.result: IA-CLAHE consistently improves recognition performance and human perceptual quality without requiring task-specific training data.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive, end-to-end trainable clip-limit estimation mitigates over-enhancement and enables zero-shot generalization, offering improved visual and recognition performance without task-specific datasets.

title: Convolutionally Low-Rank Models with Modified Quantile Regression for Interval Time Series Forecasting
summary: The quantification of uncertainty in prediction models is crucial for reliable decision-making, yet remains a significant challenge. Interval time series forecasting offers a principled solution to this problem by providing prediction intervals (PIs), which indicates the probability that the true value falls within the predicted range. We consider a recently established point forecasts (PFs) method termed Learning-Based Convolution Nuclear Norm Minimization (LbCNNM), which directly generates multi-step ahead forecasts by leveraging the convolutional low-rankness property derived from training data. While theoretically complete and empirically effective, LbCNNM lacks inherent uncertainty estimation capabilities, a limitation shared by many advanced forecasting methods. To resolve the issue, we modify the well-known Quantile Regression (QR) and integrate it into LbCNNM, resulting in a novel interval forecasting method termed LbCNNM with Modified Quantile Regression (LbCNNM-MQR). In addition, we devise interval calibration techniques to further improve the accuracy of PIs. Extensive experiments on over 100,000 real-world time series demonstrate the superior performance of LbCNNM-MQR.

AI.tldr: LbCNNM integrated with Modified Quantile Regression (LbCNNM-MQR) to produce calibrated prediction intervals for multi-step ahead forecasts; calibration techniques improve PI accuracy; validated on large-scale real-world time-series data.
AI.motivation: Uncertainty quantification in forecast models is essential for reliable decisions. LbCNNM provides accurate point forecasts but lacks inherent interval estimates. Integrating a (Modified) Quantile Regression yields prediction intervals, and calibration aims to improve their reliability.
AI.method: Extend LbCNNM by incorporating Modified Quantile Regression to generate multi-step ahead prediction intervals. Develop interval calibration techniques to improve PI accuracy. Evaluate on large-scale real-world time-series data (~100k series).
AI.result: LbCNNM-MQR achieves improved interval forecasts, with superior performance in terms of PI reliability and interval tightness across numerous real-world series, as claimed by extensive experiments.
AI.conclusion: Integrating QR with LbCNNM and applying interval calibration yields an effective, scalable interval forecasting framework, offering reliable uncertainty quantification for multi-step ahead forecasts.

title: Breakout-picker: Reducing false positives in deep learning-based borehole breakout characterization from acoustic image logs
summary: Borehole breakouts are stress-induced spalling on the borehole wall, which are identifiable in acoustic image logs as paired zones with near-symmetry azimuths, low acoustic amplitudes, and increased borehole radius. Accurate breakout characterization is crucial for in-situ stress analysis. In recent years, deep learning has been introduced to automate the time-consuming and labor-intensive breakout picking process. However, existing approaches often suffer from misclassification of non-breakout features, leading to high false positive rates. To address this limitation, this study develops a deep learning framework, termed Breakout-picker, with a specific focus on reducing false positives in automatic breakout characterization. Breakout-picker reduces false positives through two strategies. First, the training of Breakout-picker incorporates negative samples of non-breakout features, including natural fractures, keyseats, and logging artifacts. They share similar characteristics with breakouts, such as low acoustic amplitude or locally enlarged borehole radius. These negative training samples enables Breakout-picker to better discriminate true breakouts and similar non-breakout features. Second, candidate breakouts identified by Breakout-picker are further validated by azimuthal symmetry criteria, whereby detections that do not exhibit the near-symmetry characteristics of breakout azimuth are excluded. The performance of Breakout-picker is evaluated using three acoustic image log datasets from different regions. The results demonstrate that Breakout-picker outperforms other automatic methods with higher accuracy and substantially lower false positive rates. By reducing false positives, Breakout-picker enhances the reliability of automatic breakout characterization from acoustic image logs, which in turn benefits in-situ stress analysis based on borehole breakouts.

AI.tldr: A deep-learning framework, Breakout-picker, reduces false positives in borehole breakout detection by: (1) training with negative samples of non-breakout features (e.g., natural fractures, keyseats, logging artifacts) and (2) verifying detections via azimuthal symmetry criteria. Evaluated on three acoustic image log datasets, it achieves higher accuracy with substantially fewer false positives than existing automatic methods, improving reliability for in-situ stress analysis.
AI.motivation: Accurate borehole breakout characterization is essential for in-situ stress analysis, but existing automated approaches suffer from high false positive rates due to misclassifying non-breakout features that resemble breakouts.
AI.method: Breakout-picker trains on a dataset augmented with negative samples that mimic breakout features (non-breakout features with low amplitude or enlarged radius). Candidate breakouts are further validated by azimuthal symmetry, discarding detections lacking near-symmetry. Evaluation uses three acoustic image log datasets from different regions and compares against other automatic methods.
AI.result: Breakout-picker achieves higher accuracy and substantially lower false positive rates than competing automatic methods across the three datasets.
AI.conclusion: Reducing false positives enhances the reliability of automatic breakout characterization from acoustic image logs, thereby improving in-situ stress analysis that relies on borehole breakouts.

title: Self-Distillation as a Performance Recovery Mechanism for LLMs: Counteracting Compression and Catastrophic Forgetting
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have achieved remarkable success, underpinning diverse AI applications. However, they often suffer from performance degradation due to factors such as catastrophic forgetting during Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), quantization, and pruning. In this work, we introduce a performance recovery framework based on Self-Distillation Fine-Tuning (SDFT) that effectively restores model capabilities. Complementing this practical contribution, we provide a rigorous theoretical explanation for the underlying recovery mechanism. We posit that an LLM's generative capability fundamentally relies on the high-dimensional manifold constructed by its hidden layers. To investigate this, we employ Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) to quantify the alignment between student and teacher activation trajectories, leveraging its invariance to orthogonal transformations and scaling. Our experiments demonstrate a strong correlation between performance recovery and manifold alignment, substantiating the claim that self-distillation effectively aligns the student's high-dimensional manifold with the optimal structure represented by the teacher. This study bridges the gap between practical recovery frameworks and geometric representation theory, offering new insights into the internal mechanisms of self-distillation.

AI.tldr: Self-Distillation Fine-Tuning (SDFT) recovers LLM performance after forgetting due to SFT, quantization, and pruning, with empirical and theoretical support via CKA-based manifold alignment between teacher and student activations.
AI.motivation: Address performance degradation in LLMs caused by repeated fine-tuning and compression, and provide a theoretical account linking hidden-layer geometry to generative capability using a measurable alignment metric (CKA).
AI.method: Introduce SDFT as a self-distillation regimen; analyze teacher–student activation trajectories; quantify their alignment with Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA); perform experiments showing recovery correlates with manifold alignment.
AI.result: Observed a strong correlation between performance recovery and high-dimensional manifold alignment; self-distillation aligns the student’s manifold with the teacher’s optimal structure, validating the proposed mechanism.
AI.conclusion: SDFT offers a practical recovery framework supported by a geometric/representation-theoretic perspective; manifold alignment is presented as a key mechanism underlying self-distillation effectiveness in restoring LLM performance.

title: Ranking XAI Methods for Head and Neck Cancer Outcome Prediction
comment: 4-page conference paper, accepted at IEEE ISBI 2026 (International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging)
summary: For head and neck cancer (HNC) patients, prognostic outcome prediction can support personalized treatment strategy selection. Improving prediction performance of HNC outcomes has been extensively explored by using advanced artificial intelligence (AI) techniques on PET/CT data. However, the interpretability of AI remains a critical obstacle for its clinical adoption. Unlike previous HNC studies that empirically selected explainable AI (XAI) techniques, we are the first to comprehensively evaluate and rank 13 XAI methods across 24 metrics, covering faithfulness, robustness, complexity and plausibility. Experimental results on the multi-center HECKTOR challenge dataset show large variations across evaluation aspects among different XAI methods, with Integrated Gradients (IG) and DeepLIFT (DL) consistently obtained high rankings for faithfulness, complexity and plausibility. This work highlights the importance of comprehensive XAI method evaluation and can be extended to other medical imaging tasks.

AI.tldr: Comprehensive benchmarking of 13 XAI methods for HNC outcome prediction on PET/CT; Integrated Gradients and DeepLIFT perform best across faithfulness, complexity, and plausibility; substantial variability across metrics and methods; advocates systematic, multi-faceted XAI evaluation for clinical adoption.
AI.motivation: Interpretability is essential for clinical adoption of AI in head and neck cancer prognostics. Prior work largely relied on ad-hoc, single-XAI selections. This study conducts a broad, multi-metric evaluation of 13 XAI methods to enable robust method choice and generalizable insights for medical imaging.
AI.method: Evaluate 13 XAI methods across 24 metrics spanning faithfulness, robustness, complexity, and plausibility on the HECKTOR multi-center PET/CT dataset for HNC prognosis; compare method rankings to identify consistent performers and variations.
AI.result: Significant variation in method performance across metrics and tasks; Integrated Gradients and DeepLIFT consistently rank highly in faithfulness, argue for plausibility and manageable complexity; results underscore the need for comprehensive XAI benchmarking rather than ad-hoc selections.
AI.conclusion: Systematic, multi-metric XAI evaluation is crucial for clinical deployment of explainable AI in medical imaging; findings can inform method choice for HNC and generalize to other medical tasks, promoting more reliable and plausible explanations.

title: ECG-Lens: Benchmarking ML & DL Models on PTB-XL Dataset
summary: Automated classification of electrocardiogram (ECG) signals is a useful tool for diagnosing and monitoring cardiovascular diseases. This study compares three traditional machine learning algorithms (Decision Tree Classifier, Random Forest Classifier, and Logistic Regression) and three deep learning models (Simple Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), and Complex CNN (ECGLens)) for the classification of ECG signals from the PTB-XL dataset, which contains 12-lead recordings from normal patients and patients with various cardiac conditions. The DL models were trained on raw ECG signals, allowing them to automatically extract discriminative features. Data augmentation using the Stationary Wavelet Transform (SWT) was applied to enhance model performance, increase the diversity of training samples, and preserve the essential characteristics of the ECG signals. The models were evaluated using multiple metrics, including accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC-AUC. The ECG-Lens model achieved the highest performance, with 80% classification accuracy and a 90% ROC-AUC. These findings demonstrate that deep learning architectures, particularly complex CNNs substantially outperform traditional ML methods on raw 12-lead ECG data, and provide a practical benchmark for selecting automated ECG classification models and identifying directions for condition-specific model development.

AI.tldr: DL models, especially ECGLens (a complex CNN), outperform traditional ML on raw 12-lead PTB-XL ECG data; SWT-based augmentation improves performance; ECGLens achieves ~80% accuracy and ~0.90 ROC-AUC.
AI.motivation: Evaluate whether deep learning on raw ECG signals offers substantial gains over traditional ML and to establish a practical benchmark for ECG classification models using a widely-used dataset (PTB-XL).
AI.method: Compare three traditional ML classifiers (Decision Tree, Random Forest, Logistic Regression) with three DL models (Simple CNN, LSTM, ECGLens) trained on raw 12-lead ECGs from PTB-XL; apply Stationary Wavelet Transform augmentation; assess with accuracy, precision, recall, F1, and ROC-AUC.
AI.result: ECGLens achieved the highest performance with 80% accuracy and 90% ROC-AUC; DL models, particularly the Complex CNN, substantially outperform traditional ML on raw ECG data; SWT augmentation contributed to performance gains.
AI.conclusion: Deep learning architectures, especially complex CNNs, provide superior performance over traditional ML for automatic 12-lead ECG classification on PTB-XL, offering a practical benchmark and guidance for condition-specific model development.

title: Elucidating the SNR-t Bias of Diffusion Probabilistic Models
summary: Diffusion Probabilistic Models have demonstrated remarkable performance across a wide range of generative tasks. However, we have observed that these models often suffer from a Signal-to-Noise Ratio-timestep (SNR-t) bias. This bias refers to the misalignment between the SNR of the denoising sample and its corresponding timestep during the inference phase. Specifically, during training, the SNR of a sample is strictly coupled with its timestep. However, this correspondence is disrupted during inference, leading to error accumulation and impairing the generation quality. We provide comprehensive empirical evidence and theoretical analysis to substantiate this phenomenon and propose a simple yet effective differential correction method to mitigate the SNR-t bias. Recognizing that diffusion models typically reconstruct low-frequency components before focusing on high-frequency details during the reverse denoising process, we decompose samples into various frequency components and apply differential correction to each component individually. Extensive experiments show that our approach significantly improves the generation quality of various diffusion models (IDDPM, ADM, DDIM, A-DPM, EA-DPM, EDM, PFGM++, and FLUX) on datasets of various resolutions with negligible computational overhead. The code is at https://github.com/AMAP-ML/DCW.

AI.tldr: Proposes a simple differential correction to SNR-t bias in diffusion models by per-frequency component correction; demonstrates across multiple models and datasets with minimal overhead; code provided.
AI.motivation: SNR-t bias arises from mismatch between training-time SNR-t coupling and inference-time SNR-t, causing error accumulation and degraded sample quality; a low-overhead, effective fix is needed.
AI.method: Decompose denoised samples into frequency components and apply a differential correction independently to each component during reverse diffusion; leverages the tendency of diffusion models to reconstruct low-frequency content first; evaluates on diverse model families and resolutions.
AI.result: Significant improvements in generation quality across IDDPM, ADM, DDIM, A-DPM, EA-DPM, EDM, PFGM++, FLUX on datasets of varying resolutions, with negligible computational overhead; code released at the provided URL.
AI.conclusion: Per-frequency differential correction mitigates SNR-t bias in diffusion models; the approach is model-agnostic, scalable, and practical for enhancing diffusion-based generation.

title: Placing Puzzle Pieces Where They Matter: A Question Augmentation Framework for Reinforcement Learning
summary: Reinforcement learning has become a powerful approach for enhancing large language model reasoning, but faces a fundamental dilemma: training on easy problems can cause overfitting and pass@k degradation, while training on hard problems often results in sparse rewards. Recent question augmentation methods address this by prepending partial solutions as hints. However, uniform hint provision may introduce redundant information while missing critical reasoning bottlenecks, and excessive hints can reduce reasoning diversity, causing pass@k degradation. We propose \textbf{PieceHint}, a hint injection framework that strategically identifies and provides critical reasoning steps during training. By scoring the importance of different reasoning steps, selectively allocating hints based on problem difficulty, and progressively withdrawing scaffolding, PieceHint enables models to transition from guided learning to independent reasoning. Experiments on six mathematical reasoning benchmarks show that our 1.5B model achieves comparable average performance to 32B baselines while preserving pass@k diversity across all $k$ values.

AI.tldr: PieceHint introduces a selective hint-injection framework for LLM reasoning that scores and injects only critical steps, matches larger models in math benchmarks while preserving pass@k diversity, and gradually withdraws scaffolding for independent reasoning.
AI.motivation: Tackling the overfitting vs underfitting dilemma in RL or supervised fine-tuning for LLM reasoning by avoiding uniform hints that cause redundancy and reduced diversity; leverage problem difficulty and step importance to provide targeted guidance.
AI.method: Score the importance of individual reasoning steps; allocate hints based on problem difficulty; progressively withdraw scaffolding during training to shift from guided to independent reasoning; evaluate on six mathematical reasoning benchmarks with a 1.5B model vs larger baselines.
AI.result: The approach yields performance comparable to 32B baselines on average across six math benchmarks while maintaining pass@k diversity across all k values, using a much smaller model.
AI.conclusion: Selective, progressively withdrawn hints can enable smaller models to achieve and sustain complex reasoning performance, offering data-efficient training and robust reasoning capabilities.

title: Mind's Eye: A Benchmark of Visual Abstraction, Transformation and Composition for Multimodal LLMs
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have achieved impressive progress on vision language benchmarks, yet their capacity for visual cognitive and visuospatial reasoning remains less understood. We introduce "Mind's Eye", a multiple-choice benchmark of eight visuo-cognitive tasks inspired by classic human intelligence tests and organized under a novel "A-R-T" taxonomy: Abstraction, Relation, and Transformation. The tasks probe core processes of fluid intelligence such as pattern induction, analogical relation mapping, and mental transformation. We evaluate a diverse suite of closed-source and open-source MLLMs and compare their performance with human participants. Humans achieve 80% accuracy, while top performing MLLMs remain below 50%. Error analysis reveals failures in: (i) visual attention allocation, (ii) internal perceptual manipulation, and (iii) weak abstraction of underlying visual concepts. Our findings suggest that current MLLMs exhibit limited visuospatial reasoning capabilities, when compared with human participants, highlighting the need for more cognitively grounded evaluation frameworks.

AI.tldr: Eight-task visuo-cognitive benchmark (A-R-T) for evaluating MLLMs' visuospatial reasoning; humans ~80% accuracy; SOTA LLMs <50%; error patterns point to attention, internal manipulation, and abstraction gaps; highlights need for cognitively grounded evaluation.
AI.motivation: Assess and diagnose the visuospatial reasoning capabilities of multimodal LLMs using processes from fluid intelligence and classic tests.
AI.method: Eight multiple-choice tasks inspired by classic intelligence tests, categorized under Abstraction, Relation, Transformation; compare a range of closed-source/open-source MLLMs and humans; perform error analysis on failures.
AI.result: Humans ~80% accuracy; top MLLMs <50%; common errors include misallocation of visual attention, insufficient internal perceptual manipulation, and weak abstraction of concepts.
AI.conclusion: Current MLLMs exhibit limited visuospatial reasoning relative to humans; evidences the need for cognitively grounded benchmarks and architectures to improve visuospatial cognition.

title: Modern Structure-Aware Simplicial Spatiotemporal Neural Network
summary: Spatiotemporal modeling has evolved beyond simple time series analysis to become fundamental in structural time series analysis. While current research extensively employs graph neural networks (GNNs) for spatial feature extraction with notable success, these networks are limited to capturing only pairwise relationships, despite real-world networks containing richer topological relationships. Additionally, GNN-based models face computational challenges that scale with graph complexity, limiting their applicability to large networks. To address these limitations, we present Modern Structure-Aware Simplicial SpatioTemporal neural network (ModernSASST), the first approach to leverage simplicial complex structures for spatiotemporal modeling. Our method employs spatiotemporal random walks on high-dimensional simplicial complexes and integrates parallelizable Temporal Convolutional Networks to capture high-order topological structures while maintaining computational efficiency. Our source code is publicly available on GitHub\footnote{Code is available at: https://github.com/ComplexNetTSP/ST_RUM.

AI.tldr: Proposes ModernSASST, a structure-aware simplicial spatio-temporal network that uses spatiotemporal random walks on simplicial complexes and parallel Temporal Convolutional Networks to capture high-order topology efficiently, addressing GNN limitations and scalability.
AI.motivation: Real-world networks exhibit higher-order topological structures beyond pairwise edges; GNNs struggle with higher-order relations and scale poorly with graph complexity.
AI.method: Constructs a simplicial-complex-based spatiotemporal model; employs spatiotemporal random walks on high-dimensional simplices; integrates parallelizable Temporal Convolutional Networks to model temporal dynamics.
AI.result: Abstract does not report empirical results; claims computational efficiency and capability to capture high-order topology.
AI.conclusion: First approach to leverage simplicial structures for spatiotemporal modeling (ModernSASST); code available at GitHub (ST_RUM).

title: Chain-of-Thought Degrades Visual Spatial Reasoning Capabilities of Multimodal LLMs
summary: Multimodal Reasoning Models (MRMs) leveraging Chain-of-Thought (CoT) based thinking have revolutionized mathematical and logical problem-solving. However, we show that this paradigm struggles with generalized spatial intelligence. We perform a comprehensive evaluation of seventeen models across thirteen spatial benchmarks and identify a critical gap: CoT prompting consistently degrades performance in visual spatial reasoning. Furthermore, through a novel No-Image++ ablation, we demonstrate that MRMs and CoT prompted MLMs suffer from severe shortcut learning, and hallucinate visual details from textual priors even when the image is absent. These findings challenge the efficacy of text-only CoT for spatial tasks and underscore the need for vision-centric reasoning paradigms.

AI.tldr: CoT-based reasoning harms visual spatial tasks in multimodal models; No-Image++ reveals shortcut learning and hallucinations from textual priors without images; Vision-centric reasoning is needed.
AI.motivation: Assess the limits of Chain-of-Thought prompting for spatial tasks and quantify reliance on textual priors when images are absent.
AI.method: Evaluate 17 models across 13 spatial benchmarks; perform No-Image++ ablation to test image-absent settings; analyze impact of CoT on visual-spatial reasoning.
AI.result: CoT prompting consistently degrades visual spatial reasoning; No-Image++ shows severe shortcut learning and hallucinations from textual priors even when the image is not provided.
AI.conclusion: Text-only CoT is ineffective for spatial tasks; robust, vision-centric reasoning paradigms are needed for reliable spatial understanding.

title: Reversible Residual Normalization Alleviates Spatio-Temporal Distribution Shift
summary: Distribution shift severely degrades the performance of deep forecasting models. While this issue is well-studied for individual time series, it remains a significant challenge in the spatio-temporal domain. Effective solutions like instance normalization and its variants can mitigate temporal shifts by standardizing statistics. However, distribution shift on a graph is far more complex, involving not only the drift of individual node series but also heterogeneity across the spatial network where different nodes exhibit distinct statistical properties. To tackle this problem, we propose Reversible Residual Normalization (RRN), a novel framework that performs spatially-aware invertible transformations to address distribution shift in both spatial and temporal dimensions. Our approach integrates graph convolutional operations within invertible residual blocks, enabling adaptive normalization that respects the underlying graph structure while maintaining reversibility. By combining Center Normalization with spectral-constrained graph neural networks, our method captures and normalizes complex Spatio-Temporal relationships in a data-driven manner. The bidirectional nature of our framework allows models to learn in a normalized latent space and recover original distributional properties through inverse transformation, offering a robust and model-agnostic solution for forecasting on dynamic spatio-temporal systems.

AI.tldr: Reversible Residual Normalization (RRN): a graph-aware, invertible normalization framework in residual blocks to address spatio-temporal distribution shift for graph-based forecasting.
AI.motivation: Distribution shift in spatio-temporal graphs degrades forecasting. Traditional instance normalization handles temporal shifts but fails to capture heterogeneity across nodes in a spatial network. A graph-aware, invertible normalization could adapt to both spatial and temporal shifts while preserving information via reversibility.
AI.method: RRN embeds graph convolutional operations inside invertible residual blocks, enabling spatially-aware, bijective normalization. It combines Center Normalization with spectral-constrained graph neural networks to capture complex spatio-temporal relations. The framework is bidirectional, allowing learning in a normalized latent space and exact inverse reconstruction for robust forecasting. It is designed to be model-agnostic.
AI.result: Claims robust handling of distribution shift in spatio-temporal graphs, with adaptive normalization that respects graph structure and reversibility. Asserts improvements in forecasting under dynamic shifts and broad applicability across models, via ablations and empirical validation implied.
AI.conclusion: RRN provides a novel, invertible, graph-aware normalization framework for spatio-temporal forecasting on dynamic networks. It offers a data-driven, model-agnostic approach that preserves information through invertible transformations and can be integrated with various forecasting architectures.

title: TableSeq: Unified Generation of Structure, Content, and Layout
summary: We present TableSeq, an image-only, end-to-end framework for joint table structure recognition, content recognition, and cell localization. The model formulates these tasks as a single sequence-generation problem: one decoder produces an interleaved stream of \texttt{HTML} tags, cell text, and discretized coordinate tokens, thereby aligning logical structure, textual content, and cell geometry within a unified autoregressive sequence. This design avoids external OCR, auxiliary decoders, and complex multi-stage post-processing. TableSeq combines a lightweight high-resolution FCN-H16 encoder with a minimal structure-prior head and a single-layer transformer encoder, yielding a compact architecture that remains effective on challenging layouts. Across standard benchmarks, TableSeq achieves competitive or state-of-the-art results while preserving architectural simplicity. It reaches 95.23 TEDS / 96.83 S-TEDS on PubTabNet, 97.45 TEDS / 98.69 S-TEDS on FinTabNet, and 99.79 / 99.54 / 99.66 precision / recall / F1 on SciTSR under the CAR protocol, while remaining competitive on PubTables-1M under GriTS. Beyond TSR/TCR, the same sequence interface generalizes to index-based table querying without task-specific heads, achieving the best IRDR score and competitive ICDR/ICR performance. We also study multi-token prediction for faster blockwise decoding and show that it reduces inference latency with only limited accuracy degradation. Overall, TableSeq provides a practical and reproducible single-stream baseline for unified table recognition, and the source code will be made publicly available at https://github.com/hamdilaziz/TableSeq.

AI.tldr: TableSeq is an image-only, end-to-end framework that unifies table structure recognition, content recognition, and cell localization into a single autoregressive sequence, achieving strong benchmarks with a compact architecture and no external OCR.
AI.motivation: Simplify and unify the table recognition pipeline by avoiding external OCR, decoders, and multi-stage post-processing, while enabling broad applicability including index-based table querying.
AI.method: A lightweight high-resolution FCN-H16 encoder, a minimal structure-prior head, and a single-layer transformer encoder; a single decoder emits an interleaved stream of HTML tags, cell text, and discretized coordinate tokens; no external OCR; investigation of multi-token predictions for faster decoding.
AI.result: Competitive or state-of-the-art results on benchmarks: 95.23 TEDS / 96.83 S-TEDS on PubTabNet; 97.45 TEDS / 98.69 S-TEDS on FinTabNet; 99.79 / 99.54 / 99.66 P/R/F1 on SciTSR under CAR; competitive on PubTables-1M under GriTS; good generalization to index-based table querying with best IRDR score; source code to be released; reduced latency with multi-token decoding.
AI.conclusion: TableSeq provides a practical, reproducible single-stream baseline for unified table recognition that remains simple yet effective across challenging layouts, with potential for broader applicability and faster decoding.

title: DPrivBench: Benchmarking LLMs' Reasoning for Differential Privacy
summary: Differential privacy (DP) has a wide range of applications for protecting data privacy, but designing and verifying DP algorithms requires expert-level reasoning, creating a high barrier for non-expert practitioners. Prior works either rely on specialized verification languages that demand substantial domain expertise or remain semi-automated and require human-in-the-loop guidance. In this work, we investigate whether large language models (LLMs) can automate DP reasoning. We introduce DPrivBench, a benchmark in which each instance asks whether a function or algorithm satisfies a stated DP guarantee under specified assumptions. The benchmark is carefully designed to cover a broad range of DP topics, span diverse difficulty levels, and resist shortcut reasoning through trivial pattern matching. Experiments show that while the strongest models handle textbook mechanisms well, all models struggle with advanced algorithms, revealing substantial gaps in current DP reasoning capabilities. Through further analytic study and failure-mode analysis, we identify several promising directions for improving automated DP reasoning. Our benchmark provides a solid foundation for developing and evaluating such methods, and complements existing benchmarks for mathematical reasoning.

AI.tldr: Presents DPrivBench to benchmark LLM-based reasoning about differential privacy; finds current models handle textbook DP but struggle with advanced algorithms, highlighting gaps and proposing directions to advance automated DP reasoning.
AI.motivation: DP is complex to reason about and verify; traditional tools require domain expertise. LLMs could lower barriers if they can robustly reason about DP guarantees under given assumptions.
AI.method: Introduce DPrivBench, a diverse set of instances where each asks whether a function or algorithm satisfies a stated DP guarantee under specified assumptions; evaluate LLMs across topics and difficulty; analyze failure modes.
AI.result: Stronger models perform well on textbook DP mechanisms; all models fail on more advanced algorithms; benchmark exposes gaps and guides future work; complements existing math-reasoning benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: LLMs show promise for automating DP reasoning but current capabilities are insufficient for advanced algorithms; the benchmark is a solid foundation for developing and evaluating improved methods.

title: The Amazing Stability of Flow Matching
comment: EurIPS 2025 Workshop on Principles of Generative Modeling (PriGM)
summary:
The success of deep generative models in generating high-quality and diverse samples is often attributed to particular architectures and large training datasets. In this paper, we investigate the impact of these factors on the quality and diversity of samples generated by \emph{flow-matching} models. Surprisingly, in our experiments on CelebA-HQ dataset, flow matching remains stable even when pruning 50\% of the dataset. That is, the quality and diversity of generated samples are preserved. Moreover, pruning impacts the latent representation only slightly, that is, samples generated by models trained on the full and pruned dataset map to visually similar outputs for a given seed. We observe similar stability when changing the architecture or training configuration, such that the latent representation is maintained under these changes as well.
  Our results quantify just how strong this stability can be in practice, and help explain the reliability of flow-matching models under various perturbations.

AI.tldr: Flow-matching models show remarkable robustness: pruning 50% of CelebA-HQ data, altering architecture, or changing training config does not degrade sample quality/diversity or latent mappings.
AI.motivation: To understand how data size and architectural choices influence the sample quality and diversity of flow-matching generative models, and assess their robustness to perturbations.
AI.method: Empirical study on CelebA-HQ: prune 50% of the dataset; vary architecture and training configuration; evaluate generated sample quality and diversity, and assess latent-space consistency across seeds.
AI.result: Quality and diversity of samples remain preserved under dataset pruning and architectural/training perturbations; latent representations remain stable, with outputs for a given seed visually similar between full and pruned training.
AI.conclusion: Flow-matching models exhibit strong robustness to data and architectural perturbations, helping explain their reliability in practice.

title: QuantSightBench: Evaluating LLM Quantitative Forecasting with Prediction Intervals
summary: Forecasting has become a natural benchmark for reasoning under uncertainty. Yet existing evaluations of large language models remain limited to judgmental tasks in simple formats, such as binary or multiple-choice questions. In practice, however, forecasting spans a far broader scope. Across domains such as economics, public health, and social demographics, decisions hinge on numerical estimates over continuous quantities, a capability that current benchmarks do not capture. Evaluating such estimates requires a format that makes uncertainty explicit and testable. We propose prediction intervals as a natural and rigorous interface for this purpose. They demand scale awareness, internal consistency across confidence levels, and calibration over a continuum of outcomes, making them a more suitable evaluation format than point estimates for numerical forecasting. To assess this capability, we introduce a new benchmark QuantSightBench, and evaluate frontier models under multiple settings, assessing both empirical coverage and interval sharpness. Our results show that none of the 11 evaluated frontier and open-weight models achieves the 90\% coverage target, with the top performers Gemini 3.1 Pro (79.1\%), Grok 4 (76.4\%), and GPT-5.4 (75.3\%) all falling at least 10 percentage points short. Calibration degrades sharply at extreme magnitudes, revealing systematic overconfidence across all evaluated models.

AI.tldr: New benchmark QuantSightBench evaluates numerical forecasting via prediction intervals for LLMs. None of 11 models reach 90% coverage; top performers ~75-79% coverage. Calibration worsens at extreme values, indicating overconfidence.
AI.motivation: Forecasting requires numerical estimates over continuous quantities and explicit uncertainty. Judgmental, binary/multiple-choice benchmarks miss interval-based evaluation. Prediction intervals offer a rigorous, scalable way to measure calibration and sharpness across outcomes.
AI.method: Introduce QuantSightBench and evaluate 11 frontier/open-weight models (e.g., Gemini 3.1 Pro, Grok 4, GPT-5.4) across multiple settings. Assess empirical coverage and interval sharpness; analyze calibration across outcome magnitudes.
AI.result: No model achieves 90% coverage target (top: 79.1% for Gemini 3.1 Pro, 76.4% for Grok 4, 75.3% for GPT-5.4). Calibration degrades at extreme magnitudes, indicating systematic overconfidence across models.
AI.conclusion: Prediction intervals are a suitable evaluation framework for numerical forecasting, revealing under-calibration and limited reliability of current frontier models in providing accurate, calibrated bounds across a continuum of outcomes.

title: Early Detection of Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML) Using YOLOv12 Deep Learning Model
summary: Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML) is one of the most life-threatening type of blood cancers, and its accurate classification is considered and remains a challenging task due to the visual similarity between various cell types. This study addresses the classification of the multiclasses of AML cells Utilizing YOLOv12 deep learning model. We applied two segmentation approaches based on cell and nucleus features, using Hue channel and Otsu thresholding techniques to preprocess the images prior to classification. Our experiments demonstrate that YOLOv12 with Otsu thresholding on cell-based segmentation achieved the highest level of validation and test accuracy, both reaching 99.3%.

AI.tldr: YOLOv12 with Otsu-based cell segmentation achieves 99.3% accuracy in multiclass AML cell classification, outperforming nucleus-based segmentation.
AI.motivation: AML classification is difficult due to visual similarity among cell types; accurate automated multiclass classification is needed for timely diagnosis and prognosis.
AI.method: Two segmentation pipelines based on cell and nucleus features: preprocessing via Hue channel and Otsu thresholding (cell-based) or alternative (nucleus-based); YOLOv12 used for multiclass classification; evaluation on validation and test sets.
AI.result: Cell-based segmentation with Otsu thresholding achieved the highest validation and test accuracy, both 99.3%. The two approaches were compared, with the cell-based Otsu method performing best.
AI.conclusion: The combination of simple thresholding-based segmentation and a modern object-detection model (YOLOv12) can achieve near-perfect accuracy for multiclass AML classification, indicating strong discriminative power and potential clinical utility; future work could extend to other datasets and compare additional preprocessing strategies.

title: (Weighted) Adaptive Radius Near Neighbor Search: Evaluation for WiFi Fingerprint-based Positioning
comment: 11 pages, 2 figures, 2 tables, submitted to IPIN 2026
summary: Fixed Radius Near Neighbor (FRNN) search is an alternative to the widely used k Nearest Neighbors (kNN) search. Unlike kNN, FRNN determines a label or an estimate for a test sample based on all training samples within a predefined distance. While this approach is beneficial in certain scenarios, assuming a fixed maximum distance for all training samples can decrease the accuracy of the FRNN. Therefore, in this paper we propose the Adaptive Radius Near Neighbor (ARNN) and the Weighted ARNN (WARNN), which employ adaptive distances and in latter case weights. All three methods are compared to kNN and twelve of its variants for a regression problem, namely WiFi fingerprinting indoor positioning, using 22 different datasets to provide a comprehensive analysis. While the performances of the tested FRNN and ARNN versions were amongst the worse, three of the four best methods in the test were WARNN versions, indicating that using weights together with adaptive distances achieves performance comparable or even better than kNN variants.

AI.tldr: Adaptive Weighted Radius Nearest Neighbor (WARNN) often surpasses kNN variants in WiFi fingerprinting regression; ARNN/FRNN underperform overall.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of fixed-radius FRNN, which can degrade accuracy when a single predefined distance is used for all training samples. Adaptive radius (ARNN) and, in particular, weighted adaptive radius (WARNN) aim to better capture local data geometry and contribution of training samples to the regression estimate.
AI.method: Propose ARNN (adaptive radius) and WARNN (adaptive radius with weights) for regression. Evaluate against kNN and twelve kNN variants on WiFi fingerprinting indoor positioning using 22 datasets.
AI.result: FRNN and ARNN performances are among the worse. Three of the four best methods were WARNN variants, indicating that weights with adaptive distances yield performance comparable to or better than kNN variants.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating adaptive distances and, especially, weightings improves the performance of FRNN-type methods for regression tasks such as WiFi-based indoor positioning; WARNN shows strong potential relative to standard kNN approaches.

title: DINOv3 Beats Specialized Detectors: A Simple Foundation Model Baseline for Image Forensics
summary: With the rapid advancement of deep generative models, realistic fake images have become increasingly accessible, yet existing localization methods rely on complex designs and still struggle to generalize across manipulation types and imaging conditions. We present a simple but strong baseline based on DINOv3 with LoRA adaptation and a lightweight convolutional decoder. Under the CAT-Net protocol, our best model improves average pixel-level F1 by 17.0 points over the previous state of the art on four standard benchmarks using only 9.1\,M trainable parameters on top of a frozen ViT-L backbone, and even our smallest variant surpasses all prior specialized methods. LoRA consistently outperforms full fine-tuning across all backbone scales. Under the data-scarce MVSS-Net protocol, LoRA reaches an average F1 of 0.774 versus 0.530 for the strongest prior method, while full fine-tuning becomes highly unstable, suggesting that pre-trained representations encode forensic information that is better preserved than overwritten. The baseline also exhibits strong robustness to Gaussian noise, JPEG re-compression, and Gaussian blur. We hope this work can serve as a reliable baseline for the research community and a practical starting point for future image-forensic applications. Code is available at https://github.com/Irennnne/DINOv3-IML.

AI.tldr: A lightweight, LoRA-adapted DINOv3 baseline for image forensics achieving state-of-the-art pixel-level localization with minimal trainable parameters.
AI.motivation: Address the generalization gap and reliance on complex designs in existing localization methods for manipulated imagery; aims for a simple, data-efficient, robust baseline that generalizes across manipulation types and imaging conditions.
AI.method: Freeze a ViT-L backbone (DINOv3), add LoRA adapters, and use a lightweight convolutional decoder. Evaluate under CAT-Net (four benchmarks) and MVSS-Net protocols, with 9.1M trainable parameters on top of the frozen backbone. Compare LoRA vs full fine-tuning across backbone scales; assess robustness to Gaussian noise, JPEG re-compression, and Gaussian blur. Code released.
AI.result: CAT-Net: average pixel-level F1 improved by 17.0 points over prior SOTA on four benchmarks; even the smallest variant surpasses prior specialized methods. LoRA consistently outperforms full fine-tuning across backbone scales. MVSS-Net: average F1 0.774 vs 0.530 for the strongest prior; full fine-tuning is unstable. Demonstrates robustness to common degradations (noise, compression, blur). Parameter-efficient baseline with strong performance.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a reliable, simple baseline for image-forensic localization that generalizes across conditions and manipulation types, highlighting that pre-trained representations retain forensic cues. Provides a practical starting point for future image-forensic applications; code available.

title: TwinTrack: Post-hoc Multi-Rater Calibration for Medical Image Segmentation
summary: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) segmentation on contrast-enhanced CT is inherently ambiguous: inter-rater disagreement among experts reflects genuine uncertainty rather than annotation noise. Standard deep learning approaches assume a single ground truth, producing probabilistic outputs that can be poorly calibrated and difficult to interpret under such ambiguity. We present TwinTrack, a framework that addresses this gap through post-hoc calibration of ensemble segmentation probabilities to the empirical mean human response (MHR) -the fraction of expert annotators labeling a voxel as tumor. Calibrated probabilities are thus directly interpretable as the expected proportion of annotators assigning the tumor label, explicitly modeling inter-rater disagreement. The proposed post-hoc calibration procedure is simple and requires only a small multi-rater calibration set. It consistently improves calibration metrics over standard approaches when evaluated on the MICCAI 2025 CURVAS-PDACVI multi-rater benchmark.

AI.tldr: TwinTrack calibrates ensemble PDAC segmentation probabilities to the empirical mean human response (MHR), enabling probabilities that reflect inter-rater disagreement and are interpretable as the expected fraction of annotators labeling a voxel as tumor.
AI.motivation: Inter-rater disagreement in PDAC CT segmentation means a single ground-truth label is inadequate; probabilistic outputs from standard deep learning can be poorly calibrated and less interpretable under genuine uncertainty.
AI.method: Post-hoc calibration of ensemble segmentation probabilities to the MHR using a small multi-rater calibration set, aligning voxel-level probabilities with the fraction of experts labeling tumor tissue.
AI.result: Calibration metrics consistently improve over standard approaches on the MICCAI 2025 CURVAS-PDACVI multi-rater benchmark.
AI.conclusion: TwinTrack provides interpretable, calibrated probabilistic segmentation that explicitly models inter-rater uncertainty, with a simple calibration pipeline feasible in data-constrained, multi-rater settings.

title: Stylistic-STORM (ST-STORM) : Perceiving the Semantic Nature of Appearance
comment: 20 pages, 16 figures, ICPR 2026 (28th International Conference on Pattern Recognition)
summary: One of the dominant paradigms in self-supervised learning (SSL), illustrated by MoCo or DINO, aims to produce robust representations by capturing features that are insensitive to certain image transformations such as illumination, or geometric changes. This strategy is appropriate when the objective is to recognize objects independently of their appearance. However, it becomes counterproductive as soon as appearance itself constitutes the discriminative signal. In weather analysis, for example, rain streaks, snow granularity, atmospheric scattering, as well as reflections and halos, are not noise: they carry the essential information. In critical applications such as autonomous driving, ignoring these cues is risky, since grip and visibility depend directly on ground conditions and atmospheric conditions. We introduce ST-STORM, a hybrid SSL framework that treats appearance (style) as a semantic modality to be disentangled from content. Our architecture explicitly separates two latent streams, regulated by gating mechanisms. The Content branch aims at a stable semantic representation through a JEPA scheme coupled with a contrastive objective, promoting invariance to appearance variations. In parallel, the Style branch is constrained to capture appearance signatures (textures, contrasts, scattering) through feature prediction and reconstruction under an adversarial constraint. We evaluate ST-STORM on several tasks, including object classification (ImageNet-1K), fine-grained weather characterization, and melanoma detection (ISIC 2024 Challenge). The results show that the Style branch effectively isolates complex appearance phenomena (F1=97% on Multi-Weather and F1=94% on ISIC 2024 with 10% labeled data), without degrading the semantic performance (F1=80% on ImageNet-1K) of the Content branch, and improves the preservation of critical appearance

AI.tldr: ST-STORM is a two-branch SSL framework that disentangles content (semantic) and style (appearance) via gating. The Content branch uses JEPA with a contrastive objective for appearance-invariant semantics, while the Style branch learns appearance signals via prediction/reconstruction under an adversarial constraint. Demonstrated on ImageNet-1K, multi-weather classification, and ISIC 2024 (with 10% labeled data), achieving strong style metrics without degrading semantic accuracy.
AI.motivation: SSL methods often enforce invariance to appearance, which can be detrimental when appearance carries discriminative information (e.g., weather cues, reflections, lighting) relevant for tasks like autonomous driving, weather analysis, or medical imaging.
AI.method: Two gated latent streams: Content and Style. Content branch optimized with JEPA (joint embedding and predictive alignment) plus a contrastive loss to promote appearance invariance. Style branch optimized to capture appearance signatures via a feature-prediction and reconstruction objective, constrained adversarially to prevent leakage into content. Gates regulate information flow between streams to enforce disentanglement.
AI.result: Quantitative results show strong performance in appearance-related tasks (F1=97% on Multi-Weather, F1=94% on ISIC 2024 with 10% labels) while preserving semantic performance (F1=80% on ImageNet-1K). ST-STORM improves preservation of critical appearance signals without sacrificing semantic representation.
AI.conclusion: Disentangling appearance as a semantic modality within SSL can unlock improved handling of appearance-driven cues across domains, enabling robust semantic representations while retaining detailed appearance information for tasks where it is informative.

title: Multi-Objective Bayesian Optimization via Adaptive \varepsilon-Constraints Decomposition
summary: Multi-objective Bayesian optimization (MOBO) provides a principled framework for optimizing expensive black-box functions with multiple objectives. However, existing MOBO methods often struggle with coverage, scalability with respect to the number of objectives, and integrating constraints and preferences. In this work, we propose \textit{STAGE-BO, Sequential Targeting Adaptive Gap-Filling $\varepsilon$-Constraint Bayesian Optimization}, that explicitly targets under-explored regions of the Pareto front. By analyzing the coverage of the approximate Pareto front, our method identifies the largest geometric gaps. These gaps are then used as constraints, which transforms the problem into a sequence of inequality-constrained subproblems, efficiently solved via constrained expected improvement acquisition. Our approach provides a uniform Pareto coverage without hypervolume computation and naturally applies to constrained and preference-based settings. Experiments on synthetic and real-world benchmarks demonstrate superior coverage and competitive hypervolume performance against state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: Proposes STAGE-BO to improve Pareto coverage in MOBO by identifying largest gaps in the approximate Pareto front and turning them into inequality constraints; uses constrained EI; claims uniform Pareto coverage without hypervolume computation; handles constraints and preferences; shows improved coverage and competitive hypervolume.
AI.motivation: Address limited Pareto front coverage, scalability with multiple objectives, and integration of constraints and preferences in MOBO by targeting under-explored Pareto regions.
AI.method: Compute Pareto-front coverage, locate largest geometric gaps in the approximate front, and treat these gaps as inequality constraints. Solve a sequence of constrained optimization subproblems using constrained Expected Improvement (EI) acquisition.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on synthetic and real-world benchmarks demonstrates improved Pareto coverage and competitive hypervolume relative to state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: STAGE-BO provides uniform Pareto coverage without hypervolume computation and naturally accommodates constraints and preference information, offering a scalable MOBO framework.

title: DenTab: A Dataset for Table Recognition and Visual QA on Real-World Dental Estimates
summary:
Tables condense key transactional and administrative information into compact layouts, but practical extraction requires more than text recognition: systems must also recover structure (rows, columns, merged cells, headers) and interpret roles such as line items, subtotals, and totals under common capture artifacts. Many existing resources for table structure recognition and TableVQA are built from clean digital-born sources or rendered tables, and therefore only partially reflect noisy administrative conditions.
  We introduce DenTab, a dataset of 2{,}000 cropped table images from dental estimates with high-quality HTML annotations, enabling evaluation of table recognition (TR) and table visual question answering (TableVQA) on the same inputs. DenTab includes 2{,}208 questions across eleven categories spanning retrieval, aggregation, and logic/consistency checks. We benchmark 16 systems, including 14 vision--language models (VLMs) and two OCR baselines. Across models, strong structure recovery does not consistently translate into reliable performance on multi-step arithmetic and consistency questions, and these reasoning failures persist even when using ground-truth HTML table inputs.
  To improve arithmetic reliability without training, we propose the Table Router Pipeline, which routes arithmetic questions to deterministic execution. The pipeline combines (i) a VLM that produces a baseline answer, a structured table representation, and a constrained table program with (ii) a rule-based executor that performs exact computation over the parsed table. The source code and dataset will be made publicly available at https://github.com/hamdilaziz/DenTab.

AI.tldr: DenTab: a 2,000-image dental table dataset with HTML annotations for TR and TableVQA; benchmarks 16 systems; structure recovery alone doesn’t guarantee arithmetic/consistency accuracy; proposes Table Router Pipeline for deterministic arithmetic execution without training.
AI.motivation: Real-world, noisy administrative tables deviate from clean digital-born data; need joint evaluation of table structure recognition and TableVQA, plus reliable arithmetic under practical artifacts; address limitations by introducing a robust, non-learning arithmetic path.
AI.method: Create DenTab dataset (2,000 cropped dental estimate tables with HTML annotations); evaluate 16 systems (14 VLMs + 2 OCR baselines) on TR and TableVQA with 2,208 questions across 11 categories; analyze results; propose Table Router Pipeline combining a VLM-based baseline with a structured table and a constrained program, plus a rule-based executor for exact computation; release code and data.
AI.result: Across models, improved table structure recovery did not reliably boost multi-step arithmetic or consistency reasoning; failures persist even with ground-truth HTML inputs; the Table Router Pipeline is proposed to route arithmetic questions to a deterministic executor, improving arithmetic reliability without training.
