AI.conclusion: DenTab provides a realistic benchmark for TR and TableVQA under noisy conditions and reveals limits of current VLMs in arithmetic reasoning; a non-learned arithmetic pathway (Table Router Pipeline) is proposed to enhance reliability; dataset and code will be publicly released for reproducibility and further research.

title: Evaluating quality in synthetic data generation for large tabular health datasets
summary: There is no consensus in the field of synthetic data on concise metrics for quality evaluations or benchmarks on large health datasets, such as historical epidemiological data. This study presents an evaluation of seven recent models from major machine learning families. The models were evaluated using four different datasets, each with a distinct scale. To ensure a fair comparison, we systematically tuned the hyperparameters of each model for each dataset. We propose a methodology for evaluating the fidelity of synthesized joint distributions, aligning metrics with visualization on a single plot. This method is applicable to any dataset and is complemented by a domain-specific analysis of the German Cancer Registries' epidemiological dataset. The analysis reveals the challenges models face in strictly adhering to the medical domain. We hope this approach will serve as a foundational framework for guiding the selection of synthesizers and remain accessible to all stakeholders involved in releasing synthetic datasets.

AI.tldr: Seven synthetic-data models are benchmarked across four health datasets; a single-plot fidelity evaluation method for joint distributions is proposed and demonstrated on the German Cancer Registries, revealing domain-adherence challenges and offering a framework for synthesizer selection.
AI.motivation: There is no consensus on concise metrics or benchmarks for synthetic data quality in large health datasets; a fair, parameter-tuned comparison is needed, aligning metrics with domain constraints.
AI.method: Evaluate seven models from major ML families on four datasets of varying scale; systematically tune hyperparameters per dataset; propose a fidelity evaluation methodology for joint distributions that can be visualized on a single plot; apply the approach to the German Cancer Registries epidemiological dataset and conduct a domain-specific analysis.
AI.result: Findings reveal challenges for models to strictly adhere to medical domain constraints; the proposed methodology serves as a foundational framework for selecting synthesizers and guiding stakeholders in releasing synthetic data; the approach is generalizable to other datasets.
AI.conclusion: The framework is generalizable across datasets and domains and aims to guide synthesizer selection and improve accessibility for stakeholders releasing synthetic datasets.

title: Polyglot: Multilingual Style Preserving Speech-Driven Facial Animation
comment: The project website is available at https://fedenoce.github.io/polyglot/
summary: Speech-Driven Facial Animation (SDFA) has gained significant attention due to its applications in movies, video games, and virtual reality. However, most existing models are trained on single-language data, limiting their effectiveness in real-world multilingual scenarios. In this work, we address multilingual SDFA, which is essential for realistic generation since language influences phonetics, rhythm, intonation, and facial expressions. Speaking style is also shaped by individual differences, not only by language. Existing methods typically rely on either language-specific or speaker-specific conditioning, but not both, limiting their ability to model their interaction. We introduce Polyglot, a unified diffusion-based architecture for personalized multilingual SDFA. Our method uses transcript embeddings to encode language information and style embeddings extracted from reference facial sequences to capture individual speaking characteristics. Polyglot does not require predefined language or speaker labels, enabling generalization across languages and speakers through self-supervised learning. By jointly conditioning on language and style, it captures expressive traits such as rhythm, articulation, and habitual facial movements, producing temporally coherent and realistic animations. Experiments show improved performance in both monolingual and multilingual settings, providing a unified framework for modeling language and personal style in SDFA.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-based, language- and speaker-conditioned SDFA model (Polyglot) for multilingual and personalized speech-driven facial animation, using transcript embeddings for language and reference-face style embeddings; self-supervised, no predefined labels; claims improved monolingual and multilingual performance.
AI.motivation: Need for multilingual SDFA due to language effects on phonetics, rhythm, intonation, and facial expressions; current methods are limited to language- or speaker-specific conditioning; desire joint modeling of language and personal style with no reliance on labeled data.
AI.method: A unified diffusion-based architecture that conditions on two signals: transcript embeddings encoding language information and style embeddings extracted from reference facial sequences to capture individual speaking characteristics. Operates without predefined language or speaker labels and learns via self-supervision, enabling generalization across languages and speakers.
AI.result: Experiments show improved performance in both monolingual and multilingual settings, producing temporally coherent and realistic animations; provides a unified framework for modeling language and personal style in SDFA.
AI.conclusion: Polyglot demonstrates effective joint modeling of language and personal style for multilingual SDFA, with self-supervised learning enabling label-free generalization across languages and speakers.

title: Impact of Nonlinear Power Amplifier on Massive MIMO: Machine Learning Prediction Under Realistic Radio Channel
comment: Accepted for publication in IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
summary: M-MIMO is one of the crucial technologies for increasing spectral and energy efficiency of wireless networks. Most of the current works assume that M-MIMO arrays are equipped with a linear front end. However, ongoing efforts to make wireless networks more energy-efficient push the hardware to the limits, where its nonlinear behavior appears. This is especially a common problem for the multicarrier systems, e.g., OFDM used in 4G, 5G, and possibly also in 6G, which is characterized by a high Peak-to-Average Power Ratio. While the impact of a nonlinear Power Amplifier (PA) on an OFDM signal is well characterized, it is a relatively new topic for the M-MIMO OFDM systems. Most of the recent works either neglect nonlinear effects or utilize simplified models proper for Rayleigh or LoS radio channel models. In this paper, we first theoretically characterize the nonlinear distortion in the M-MIMO system under commonly used radio channel models. Then, utilizing 3D-Ray Tracing (3D-RT) software, we demonstrate that these models are not very accurate. Instead, we propose two models: a statistical one and an ML-based one using 3D-RT results. The proposed statistical model utilizes the Generalized Extreme Value (GEV) distribution to model Signal to Distortion Ratio (SDR) for victim users, receiving nonlinear distortion, e.g., as interference from neighboring cells. The proposed ML model aims to predict SDR for a scheduled user (receiving nonlinear distortion along with the desired signal), based on the spatial characteristics of the radio channel and the operation point of each PA feeding at the M-MIMO antenna array. The predicted SDR can then be used to perform PA-aware per-user power allocation. The results show about 12% median gain in user throughput achieved by the proposed ML-based power allocation scheme over the state-of-the-art, fixed operating point scheme.

AI.tldr: Nonlinear PA distortion in M-MIMO-OFDM is modeled with two approaches (GEV SDR stat model and ML-based predictor) using 3D-RT calibration; enables PA-aware per-user power allocation with ~12% median throughput gain over fixed-point schemes.
AI.motivation: In M-MIMO-OFDM with high PAPR, nonlinear PAs distort signals; existing linear or simplified models fail to capture real distortion under realistic channels; improved distortion modeling and allocation strategies are needed to enhance spectral and energy efficiency.
AI.method: The paper theoretically characterizes nonlinear distortion under standard channel models and uses 3D-Ray Tracing to assess accuracy. It proposes two models: (1) a statistical Generalized Extreme Value (GEV) SDR model for victim users; (2) an ML-based SDR predictor using spatial channel features and PA operating point. Predicted SDR is used for PA-aware per-user power allocation.
AI.result: GEV-based SDR model for victim users; ML predictor enables SDR prediction for a scheduled user. The PA-aware allocation yields about 12% median gain in user throughput compared to a state-of-the-art fixed operating point scheme.
AI.conclusion: More accurate distortion modeling (statistical and ML-driven) combined with PA-aware power allocation can mitigate nonlinear distortion in M-MIMO-OFDM systems. 3D-RT-based validation highlights limitations of simplified models and motivates data-driven, channel-aware designs.

title: Towards In-Context Tone Style Transfer with A Large-Scale Triplet Dataset
summary: Tone style transfer for photo retouching aims to adapt the stylistic tone of the reference image to a given content image. However, the lack of high-quality large-scale triplet datasets with stylized ground truth forces existing methods to rely on self-supervised or proxy objectives, which limits model capability. To mitigate this gap, we design a data construction pipeline to build TST100K, a large-scale dataset of 100,000 content-reference-stylized triplets. At the core of this pipeline, we train a tone style scorer to ensure strict stylistic consistency for each triplet. In addition, existing methods typically extract content and reference features independently and then fuse them in a decoder, which may cause semantic loss and lead to inappropriate color transfer and degraded visual aesthetics. Instead, we propose ICTone, a diffusion-based framework that performs tone transfer in an in-context manner by jointly conditioning on both images, leveraging the semantic priors of generative models for semantic-aware transfer. Reward feedback learning using the tone style scorer is further incorporated to improve stylistic fidelity and visual quality. Experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of TST100K, and ICTone achieves state-of-the-art performance on both quantitative metrics and human evaluations.

AI.tldr: Proposes TST100K dataset and ICTone diffusion-based in-context tone style transfer with tone style scorer and reward feedback to achieve better semantic-aware tonal transfer and state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Address lack of large-scale, high-quality triplet data with stylized ground truth for tone style transfer, which hampers model capability; existing methods rely on self-supervised/proxy objectives.
AI.method: Build TST100K via data construction and train a tone style scorer to enforce stylistic consistency; introduce ICTone, a diffusion-based framework that conditions on content and reference jointly (in-context) using semantic priors; incorporate reward feedback learning from the scorer.
AI.result: Empirical results indicate TST100K is effective; ICTone achieves state-of-the-art performance on quantitative metrics and human evaluations.
AI.conclusion: Joint dataset construction and in-context diffusion-based tone transfer with reward feedback yields improved stylistic fidelity and visual quality, mitigating semantic loss seen in independent feature fusion methods.

title: Corner Reflector Array Jamming Discrimination Using Multi-Dimensional Micro-Motion Features with Frequency Agile Radar
summary: This paper introduces a robust discrimination method for distinguishing real ship targets from corner-reflector-array jamming with frequency-agile radar. The key idea is to exploit the multidimensional micro-motion signatures that separate rigid ships from non-rigid decoys. From Range-Velocity maps we derive two new hand-crafted descriptors-mean weighted residual (MWR) and complementary contrast factor (CCF) and fuse them with deep features learned by a lightweight CNN. An XGBoost classifier then gives the final decision. Extensive simulations show that the hybrid feature set consistently outperforms state-of-the-art alternatives, confirming the superiority of the proposed approach.

AI.tldr: A hybrid discrimination framework for frequency-agile radar ships vs jamming: handcrafted descriptors MWR and CCF from Range-Velocity maps are fused with CNN features and classified by XGBoost, achieving superior performance in simulations.
AI.motivation: Robustly distinguishing real ship targets from corner-reflector-array jamming when micro-motion signatures differ; existing methods are vulnerable to non-rigid decoys and radar clutter.
AI.method: Derive MWR and CCF descriptors from Range-Velocity maps; fuse with features from a lightweight CNN; train an XGBoost classifier for final decision.
AI.result: Extensive simulations show the hybrid feature set consistently outperforms state-of-the-art alternatives, indicating improved discrimination accuracy and robustness.
AI.conclusion: The proposed multi-descriptor fusion leverages multidimensional micro-motion signatures to enhance target discrimination in frequency-agile radar, with potential for real-time deployment due to computationally light CNN and efficient classifier.

title: From Articles to Canopies: Knowledge-Driven Pseudo-Labelling for Tree Species Classification using LLM Experts
summary: Hyperspectral tree species classification is challenging due to limited and imbalanced class labels, spectral mixing (overlapping light signatures from multiple species), and ecological heterogeneity (variability among ecological systems). Addressing these challenges requires methods that integrate biological and structural characteristics of vegetation, such as canopy architecture and interspecific interactions, rather than relying solely on spectral signatures. This paper presents a biologically informed, semi-supervised deep learning method that integrates multi-sensor Earth observation data, specifically hyperspectral imaging (HSI) and airborne laser scanning (ALS), with expert, ecological knowledge. The approach relies on biologically inspired pseudo-labelling over a precomputed canopy graph, yielding accurate classification at low training cost. In addition, ecological priors on species cohabitation are automatically derived from reliable sources using large language models (LLMs) and encoded as a cohabitation matrix with likelihoods of species occurring together. These priors are incorporated into the pseudo-labelling strategy, effectively introducing expert knowledge into the model. Experiments on a real-world forest dataset demonstrate 5.6% improvement over the best reference method. Expert evaluation of cohabitation priors reveals high accuracy with differences no larger than 15%.

AI.tldr: Biologically informed semi-supervised deep learning for forest species classification using hyperspectral imaging (HSI) and airborne laser scanning (ALS) with ecological priors derived from large language models (LLMs) to guide pseudo-labeling on a canopy graph, achieving improved accuracy with limited labels.
AI.motivation: Tackle hyperspectral tree species classification challenges: limited/imbalanced labels, spectral mixing, and ecological heterogeneity. Leverage biological/structural vegetation cues (canopy architecture, interspecific interactions) and expert ecological knowledge to improve accuracy with low labeling cost.
AI.method: Combine HSI and ALS data with a biologically inspired pseudo-labeling scheme on a precomputed canopy graph. Derive cohabitation priors from reliable sources using LLMs and encode as a cohabitation matrix; integrate these priors into pseudo-labeling to inject expert knowledge.
AI.result: Reported 5.6% improvement over the best reference method on a real-world forest dataset. Expert evaluation of cohabitation priors showed high accuracy with differences ≤15%.
AI.conclusion: Integrating ecological priors and canopy structure into semi-supervised DL enhances forest species classification with limited labeled data; LLM-derived priors can be effectively encoded into the learning process and yield practical improvements.

title: AEGIS: Anchor-Enforced Gradient Isolation for Knowledge-Preserving Vision-Language-Action Fine-Tuning
summary: Adapting pre-trained vision-language models (VLMs) for robotic control requires injecting high-magnitude continuous gradients from a flow-matching action expert into a backbone trained exclusively with cross-entropy. This cross-modal gradient asymmetry - the spectral dimensionality mismatch between low-rank MSE regression gradients and the high-dimensional semantic manifold sculpted by CE pre-training, causes rapid, severe erosion of the VLM's visual-question-answering (VQA) capability. Industry-standard defences either sever the gradient pathway entirely via stop gradient, discarding the rich continuous supervision, or restrict parameter capacity through low-rank adapters (LoRA) that constrain the rank of updates but not their direction, and thus still overwrite the pre-trained manifold. We introduce AEGIS (Anchor-Enforced Gradient Isolation System): a buffer-free, layer-wise orthogonal gradient projection framework that enables direct continuous MSE learning while preserving the pre-trained VQA manifold - without any co-training data or replay buffer. AEGIS pre-computes a static Gaussian reference anchor from masked VQA forward passes across all transformer layers, then at each training step constructs a Wasserstein-2 transport penalty that generates an anchor restoration gradient. A sequential dual-backward decomposes the task and anchor gradients; for each transformer layer, AEGIS applies a single Gram-Schmidt orthogonal projection that bends the task gradient away from the destructive direction while preserving its constructive content. The projection sheds less than 1% of gradient energy on average, yet eliminates the cumulative activation drift that drives severe forgetting.

AI.tldr: AEGIS is a gradient-projection technique that enables continuous MSE fine-tuning of pre-trained vision-language models for robotic control, while preserving the VQA manifold and avoiding replay buffers or stop-gradient/LoRA limitations.
AI.motivation: Mitigate cross-modal gradient asymmetry and catastrophic forgetting when injecting dense MSE supervision into CE-pre-trained VLMs; standard defenses (stop-gradient, LoRA) either block useful gradients or overwrite the learned manifold.
AI.method: Compute a static Gaussian reference anchor from masked VQA forward passes across all transformer layers. Use a Wasserstein-2 transport penalty to generate an anchor restoration gradient. Perform a sequential dual-backward pass and apply layer-wise Gram-Schmidt orthogonal projection to align the task gradient away from destructive directions while preserving constructive content; only a small fraction of gradient energy is discarded (<1%).
AI.result: The projection prevents cumulative activation drift and forgetting, achieving buffer-free, continual MSE learning without co-training data or replay buffers.
AI.conclusion: AEGIS offers a practical, minimal-gradient-energy method to preserve VLM manifolds during continuous gradient-based adaptation, enabling stable integration of MSE objectives into vision-language backbones for robotics.

title: Motion-Adapter: A Diffusion Model Adapter for Text-to-Motion Generation of Compound Actions
comment: 12 pages, 12 figures, Under review for publication in IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
summary: Recent advances in generative motion synthesis have enabled the production of realistic human motions from diverse input modalities. However, synthesizing compound actions from texts, which integrate multiple concurrent actions into coherent full-body sequences, remains a major challenge. We identify two key limitations in current text-to-motion diffusion models: (i) catastrophic neglect, where earlier actions are overwritten by later ones due to improper handling of temporal information, and (ii) attention collapse, which arises from excessive feature fusion in cross-attention mechanisms. As a result, existing approaches often depend on overly detailed textual descriptions (e.g., raising right hand), explicit body-part specifications (e.g., editing the upper body), or the use of large language models (LLMs) for body-part interpretation. These strategies lead to deficient semantic representations of physical structures and kinematic mechanisms, limiting the ability to incorporate natural behaviors such as greeting while walking. To address these issues, we propose the Motion-Adapter, a plug-and-play module that guides text-to-motion diffusion models in generating compound actions by computing decoupled cross-attention maps, which serve as structural masks during the denoising process. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method consistently produces more faithful and coherent compound motions across diverse textual prompts, surpassing state-of-the-art approaches.

AI.tldr: A plug-and-play module called Motion-Adapter guides text-to-motion diffusion models by generating decoupled cross-attention maps that act as structural masks during denoising, enabling faithful, coherent compound actions from text prompts.
AI.motivation: Current text-to-motion diffusion models suffer from (i) catastrophic neglect of earlier actions due to poor temporal handling, and (ii) attention collapse from excessive cross-attention fusion. Existing strategies rely on detailed descriptions or external LLMs for body-part interpretation, yielding weak semantic representations of kinematic structure.
AI.method: Introduce Motion-Adapter as a plug-and-play module that computes decoupled cross-attention maps to serve as structural masks during the denoising steps of diffusion models, guiding the generation of coherent compound actions across multiple concurrent motions.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show the method yields more faithful and coherent compound motions across diverse textual prompts, outperforming state-of-the-art approaches.
AI.conclusion: Motion-Adapter effectively mitigates temporal neglect and attention collapse in text-to-motion diffusion, enabling robust, semantically grounded generation of compound actions without heavy reliance on detailed prompts or external language models.

title: Prototype-Grounded Concept Models for Verifiable Concept Alignment
summary: Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) aim to improve interpretability in Deep Learning by structuring predictions through human-understandable concepts, but they provide no way to verify whether learned concepts align with the human's intended meaning, hurting interpretability. We introduce Prototype-Grounded Concept Models (PGCMs), which ground concepts in learned visual prototypes: image parts that serve as explicit evidence for the concepts. This grounding enables direct inspection of concept semantics and supports targeted human intervention at the prototype level to correct misalignments. Empirically, PGCMs match the predictive performance of state-of-the-art CBMs while substantially improving transparency, interpretability, and intervenability.

AI.tldr: Prototype-Grounded Concept Models (PGCMs) ground concepts in learned visual prototypes to provide explicit evidence for concepts, enabling inspection and targeted prototype-level intervention, while preserving predictive accuracy comparable to state-of-the-art CBMs.
AI.motivation: CBMs offer interpretability through a concept bottleneck but cannot verify alignment between learned concepts and human semantics, limiting transparency and actionable interpretability.
AI.method: Introduce PGCMs that ground each concept in learned visual prototypes (image parts) that serve as explicit evidence; enable inspection of concept semantics and targeted human intervention at the prototype level to correct misalignments.
AI.result: PGCMs achieve predictive performance on par with state-of-the-art CBMs and significantly improve transparency, interpretability, and intervenability.
AI.conclusion: Grounding concepts in visual prototypes yields interpretable, controllable, and performant models, addressing key limitations of traditional CBMs.

title: SWNet: A Cross-Spectral Network for Camouflaged Weed Detection
summary: This paper presents SWNet, a bimodal end-to-end cross-spectral network specifically engineered for the detection of camouflaged weeds in dense agricultural environments. Plant camouflage, characterized by homochromatic blending where invasive species mimic the phenotypic traits of primary crops, poses a significant challenge for traditional computer vision systems. To overcome these limitations, SWNet utilizes a Pyramid Vision Transformer v2 backbone to capture long-range dependencies and a Bimodal Gated Fusion Module to dynamically integrate Visible and Near-Infrared information. By leveraging the physiological differences in chlorophyll reflectance captured in the NIR spectrum, the proposed architecture effectively discriminates targets that are otherwise indistinguishable in the visible range. Furthermore, an Edge-Aware Refinement module is employed to produce sharper object boundaries and reduce structural ambiguity. Experimental results on the Weeds-Banana dataset indicate that SWNet outperforms ten state-of-the-art methods. The study demonstrates that the integration of cross-spectral data and boundary-guided refinement is essential for high segmentation accuracy in complex crop canopies. The code is available on GitHub: https://cod-espol.github.io/SWNet/

AI.tldr: SWNet is a bimodal end-to-end network for camouflaged weed detection using visible and NIR data, leveraging a Pyramid Vision Transformer v2 backbone, a Bimodal Gated Fusion Module, and an Edge-Aware Refinement to improve segmentation boundaries; achieves state-of-the-art results on the Weeds-Banana dataset and provides code on GitHub.
AI.motivation: Camouflage weeds blend with crops, making detection hard with single-spectrum vision; cross-spectral information (NIR) reveals chlorophyll differences; need effective fusion and boundary refinement for accurate segmentation in dense canopies.
AI.method: Architecture combines PVTV2 backbone for long-range dependencies; Bimodal Gated Fusion Module to fuse VIs and NIR; Edge-Aware Refinement module to sharpen boundaries; trained/evaluated on Weeds-Banana dataset; code available.
AI.result: Outperforms ten state-of-the-art methods on Weeds-Banana; demonstrates effectiveness of cross-spectral data fusion and boundary-guided refinement for high segmentation accuracy in complex canopies.
AI.conclusion: Cross-spectral fusion with boundary-focused refinement is essential for high-accuracy camouflaged-weed segmentation; approach contributes a practical pipeline and benchmark progress; code released.

title: Unveiling Stochasticity: Universal Multi-modal Probabilistic Modeling for Traffic Forecasting
summary: Traffic forecasting is a challenging spatio-temporal modeling task and a critical component of urban transportation management. Current studies mainly focus on deterministic predictions, with limited considerations on the uncertainty and stochasticity in traffic dynamics. Therefore, this paper proposes an elegant yet universal approach that transforms existing models into probabilistic predictors by replacing only the final output layer with a novel Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) layer. The modified model requires no changes to the training pipeline and can be trained using only the Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL) loss, without any auxiliary or regularization terms. Experiments on multiple traffic datasets show that our approach generalizes from classic to modern model architectures while preserving deterministic performance. Furthermore, we propose a systematic evaluation procedure based on cumulative distributions and confidence intervals, and demonstrate that our approach is considerably more accurate and informative than unimodal or deterministic baselines. Finally, a more detailed study on a real-world dense urban traffic network is presented to examine the impact of data quality on uncertainty quantification and to show the robustness of our approach under imperfect data conditions. Code available at https://github.com/Weijiang-Xiong/OpenSkyTraffic

AI.tldr: Transforms deterministic traffic predictors into probabilistic models by substituting the final layer with a Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) layer, enabling NLL-trained, uncertainty-aware forecasts without altering the training pipeline; works across architectures and datasets; supports rigorous probabilistic evaluation; code available.
AI.motivation: Current traffic forecasting largely yields deterministic outputs, with limited consideration of uncertainty and stochasticity in traffic dynamics. There is a need for simple, architecture-agnostic methods to obtain probabilistic forecasts and uncertainty quantification.
AI.method: Replace the final output layer with a Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) layer. Train the model with Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL) loss only; no changes to the training pipeline or additional regularization. The approach is model-agnostic and applies to both classic and modern architectures.
AI.result: Experiments show that the approach generalizes across architectures while preserving deterministic performance. It yields more accurate and informative probabilistic forecasts than unimodal or deterministic baselines. A systematic evaluation using cumulative distributions and confidence intervals is proposed. The method demonstrates robustness to imperfect data on a real dense urban network.
AI.conclusion: A simple, universal strategy to convert existing traffic predictors into probabilistic models without retraining or pipeline changes, providing accurate uncertainty quantification and robust performance across architectures and data conditions; code is available at the provided repository.

title: neuralCAD-Edit: An Expert Benchmark for Multimodal-Instructed 3D CAD Model Editing
summary: We introduce neuralCAD-Edit, the first benchmark for editing 3D CAD models collected from expert CAD engineers. Instead of text conditioning as in prior works, we collect realistic CAD editing requests by capturing videos of professional designers, interacting directly with CAD models in CAD software, while talking, pointing and drawing. We recruited ten consenting designers to contribute to this contained study. We benchmark leading foundation models against human CAD experts carrying out edits, and find a large performance gap in both automatic metrics and human evaluations. Even the best foundation model (GPT 5.2) scores 53% lower (absolute) than CAD experts in human acceptance trials, demonstrating the challenge of neuralCAD-Edit. We hope neuralCAD-Edit will provide a solid foundation against which 3D CAD editing approaches and foundation models can be developed. Code/data: https://autodeskailab.github.io/neuralCAD-Edit

AI.tldr: Introduces neuralCAD-Edit, a 3D CAD editing benchmark based on expert designers' interactions, showing a large gap between foundation models and human editors.
AI.motivation: Existing CAD editing benchmarks rely on text-conditioned prompts and lack realism; there is a need for benchmarks reflecting professional editing tasks in CAD workflows.
AI.method: Collected data by recording ten professional designers editing CAD models in real CAD software, capturing videos of their interactions while talking, pointing, and drawing; no text conditioning; benchmarked leading foundation models against human CAD experts.
AI.result: Substantial performance gap observed: even the best foundation model (GPT 5.2) scores 53% lower (absolute) than CAD experts in human acceptance trials; results are consistent across automatic metrics and human evaluations.
AI.conclusion: neuralCAD-Edit provides a solid foundation for developing 3D CAD editing approaches and foundation models; code/data available at the provided link.

title: The Harder Path: Last Iterate Convergence for Uncoupled Learning in Zero-Sum Games with Bandit Feedback
comment: Accepted at the 42nd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2025)
summary: We study the problem of learning in zero-sum matrix games with repeated play and bandit feedback. Specifically, we focus on developing uncoupled algorithms that guarantee, without communication between players, the convergence of the last-iterate to a Nash equilibrium. Although the non-bandit case has been studied extensively, this setting has only been explored recently, with a bound of $\mathcal{O}(T^{-1/8})$ on the exploitability gap. We show that, for uncoupled algorithms, guaranteeing convergence of the policy profiles to a Nash equilibrium is detrimental to the performance, with the best attainable rate being $Ω(T^{-1/4})$ in contrast to the usual $Ω(T^{-1/2})$ rate for convergence of the average iterates. We then propose two algorithms that achieve this optimal rate up to constant and logarithmic factors. The first algorithm leverages a straightforward trade-off between exploration and exploitation, while the second employs a regularization technique based on a two-step mirror descent approach.

AI.tldr: In zero-sum repeated games with bandit feedback and no communication, uncoupled algorithms cannot achieve last-iterate convergence faster than Ω(T^{-1/4}); the best rate for exploitability matches this lower bound; two near-optimal algorithms are proposed: one via an exploration-exploitation trade-off and another via a two-step mirror-descent regularization.
AI.motivation: To understand the fundamental limits of uncoupled, bandit-feedback learning for converging to Nash equilibria under last-iterate dynamics, and to identify whether last-iterate convergence is compatible with the optimal exploitability/regret trade-offs.
AI.method: 1) Derive a lower bound Ω(T^{-1/4}) on the exploitability gap for any uncoupled policy-learning algorithm in a repeated zero-sum matrix game with bandit feedback; show that enforcing last-iterate convergence hurts performance relative to average-iterate convergence. 2) Propose two algorithms achieving the optimal rate up to constants and log factors: (a) an exploration–exploitation trade-off approach; (b) a regularized two-step mirror-descent method; with theoretical guarantees matching the lower bound.
AI.result: Establishes a tight (up to constants/log factors) Ω(T^{-1/4}) rate for last-iterate convergence in uncoupled bandit-zero-sum play; demonstrates that forcing last-iterate convergence is inherently costly and provides two practical algorithms achieving near-optimal rates.
AI.conclusion: The work reveals a fundamental limit for uncoupled learning in zero-sum bandit games and offers near-optimal algorithms to reach the optimal rate. It suggests further research on relaxing the uncoupled assumption, exploring faster rates under alternative feedback or communication models, and extending to broader game classes.

title: Winner of CVPR2026 NTIRE Challenge on Image Shadow Removal: Semantic and Geometric Guidance for Shadow Removal via Cascaded Refinement
comment: 10 pages, 4 figures, 5 tables, accepted at the CVPR 2026 Workshops (NTIRE 2026 Image Shadow Removal Challenge). Code and materials are available at https://github.com/AIT-Assistive-Autonomous-Systems/SGCR-SR
summary: We present a three-stage progressive shadow-removal pipeline for the CVPR2026 NTIRE WSRD+ challenge. Built on OmniSR, our method treats deshadowing as iterative direct refinement, where later stages correct residual artefacts left by earlier predictions. The model combines RGB appearance with frozen DINOv2 semantic guidance and geometric cues from monocular depth and surface normals, reused across all stages. To stabilise multi-stage optimisation, we introduce a contraction-constrained objective that encourages non-increasing reconstruction error across the cascade. A staged training pipeline transfers from earlier WSRD pretraining to WSRD+ supervision and final WSRD+ 2026 adaptation with cosine-annealed checkpoint ensembling. On the official WSRD+ 2026 hidden test set, our final ensemble achieved 26.680 PSNR, 0.8740 SSIM, 0.0578 LPIPS, and 26.135 FID, ranked first overall, and won the NTIRE 2026 Image Shadow Removal Challenge. The strong performance of the proposed model is further validated on the ISTD+ and UAV-SC+ datasets.

AI.tldr: Three-stage progressive shadow-removal method built on OmniSR; uses RGB with frozen DINOv2 guidance and monocular depth/normals; contraction-constrained objective; staged training with WSRD pretraining to WSRD+ adaptation with cosine-annealed checkpoint ensembling; achieves state-of-the-art on NTIRE 2026 WSRD+ challenge.
AI.motivation: Address robust shadow removal under WSRD+ by leveraging semantic guidance and geometry, while stabilizing multi-stage optimization through a contraction constraint.
AI.method: Three-stage progressive shadow-removal pipeline built on OmniSR; integrates RGB appearance with frozen DINOv2 semantic guidance and monocular depth and surface normals; uses a contraction-constrained objective to enforce non-increasing reconstruction error across stages; staged training from WSRD pretraining to WSRD+ supervision and final WSRD+ 2026 adaptation with cosine-annealed checkpoint ensembling.
AI.result: On the official WSRD+ 2026 hidden test set, the final ensemble achieves 26.680 PSNR, 0.8740 SSIM, 0.0578 LPIPS, and 26.135 FID; ranked first overall and won the NTIRE 2026 Image Shadow Removal Challenge; validated on ISTD+ and UAV-SC+ datasets.
AI.conclusion: The proposed multi-stage, geometry- and semantics-guided approach, coupled with a contraction-based stability objective and staged training with ensemble, yields state-of-the-art shadow removal performance on WSRD+2026 and strong generalization.

title: Sample Complexity Bounds for Stochastic Shortest Path with a Generative Model
comment: Accepted at the 32nd International Conference on Algorithmic Learning Theory (ALT 2021)
summary: We study the sample complexity of learning an $ε$-optimal policy in the Stochastic Shortest Path (SSP) problem. We first derive sample complexity bounds when the learner has access to a generative model. We show that there exists a worst-case SSP instance with $S$ states, $A$ actions, minimum cost $c_{\min}$, and maximum expected cost of the optimal policy over all states $B_{\star}$, where any algorithm requires at least $Ω(SAB_{\star}^3/(c_{\min}ε^2))$ samples to return an $ε$-optimal policy with high probability. Surprisingly, this implies that whenever $c_{\min} = 0$ an SSP problem may not be learnable, thus revealing that learning in SSPs is strictly harder than in the finite-horizon and discounted settings. We complement this lower bound with an algorithm that matches it, up to logarithmic factors, in the general case, and an algorithm that matches it up to logarithmic factors even when $c_{\min} = 0$, but only under the condition that the optimal policy has a bounded hitting time to the goal state.

AI.tldr: Lower bounds and near-matching algorithms for SSP sample complexity with a generative model; shows Ω(S A B_*^3 /(c_min ε^2)) samples are necessary; c_min=0 leads to non-learnability; provides algorithms achieving near-optimal upper bounds (with/without bounded hitting time).
AI.motivation: Clarify fundamental sample complexity of learning ε-optimal policies in SSP, reveal hardness relative to finite-horizon and discounted settings, and identify conditions (e.g., bounded hitting time) under which SSP is learnable.
AI.method: Derive worst-case lower bound by constructing SSP instances; develop upper-bound algorithms that match the bound up to logarithmic factors; treat the c_min=0 case with and without a bounded hitting-time assumption.
AI.result: Proves a tight (up to log factors) sample complexity bound for SSP with a generative model; shows non-learnability when c_min=0 in general; provides two near-optimal algorithms under different conditions.
AI.conclusion: Learning in SSP can be strictly harder than finite-horizon/discounted settings; zero minimum cost introduces inherent hardness; feasible learnability relies on structural assumptions such as bounded hitting time.

title: Saturation-Aware Space-Variant Blind Image Deblurring
summary: This paper presents a novel saturation aware space variant blind image deblurring framework designed to address challenges posed by saturated pixels in deblurring under high dynamic range and low light conditions. The proposed approach effectively segments the image based on blur intensity and proximity to saturation, leveraging a pre estimated Light Spread Function to mitigate stray light effects. By accurately estimating the true radiance of saturated regions using the dark channel prior, our method enhances the deblurring process without introducing artifacts like ringing. Experimental evaluations on both synthetic and real world datasets demonstrate that the framework improves deblurring outcomes across various scenarios showcasing superior performance compared to state of the art saturation-aware and general purpose methods. This adaptability highlights the framework potential integration with existing and emerging blind image deblurring techniques.

AI.tldr: A saturation-aware, space-variant blind deblurring framework for HDR/low-light images that segments by blur and saturation proximity, uses a pre-estimated Light Spread Function to reduce stray light, and dark channel prior to recover radiance, achieving artifact-free deblurring and outperforming state-of-the-art methods.
AI.motivation: Saturated pixels and stray light under high dynamic range and low-light conditions hinder blind image deblurring, causing artifacts such as ringing and degraded radiance recovery; a robust approach is needed that preserves details while suppressing artifacts.
AI.method: Proposes a saturation-aware, space-variant blind deblurring pipeline. It segments the image by blur intensity and proximity to saturation, leverages a pre-estimated Light Spread Function to mitigate stray light effects, and uses the dark channel prior to estimate true radiance in saturated regions. The approach aims to reduce ringing and can be integrated with existing blind deblurring techniques.
AI.result: Experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets show improved deblurring across various scenarios, with superior performance compared to state-of-the-art saturation-aware and general-purpose methods.
AI.conclusion: The framework is adaptable and can be integrated with existing and emerging blind image deblurring techniques, broadening applicability in challenging HDR/low-light deblurring tasks.

title: SCRIPT: Implementing an Intelligent Tutoring System for Programming in a German University Context
comment: In: Cristea, A.I., Walker, E., Lu, Y., Santos, O.C., Isotani, S. (eds) Artificial Intelligence in Education. Posters and Late Breaking Results, Workshops and Tutorials, Industry and Innovation Tracks, Practitioners, Doctoral Consortium, Blue Sky, and WideAIED. AIED 2025. Communications in Computer and Information Science, vol 2590 . Springer, Cham
summary: Practice and extensive exercises are essential in programming education. Intelligent tutoring systems (ITSs) are a viable option to provide individualized hints and advice to programming students even when human tutors are not available. However, prior ITS for programming rarely support the Python programming language, mostly focus on introductory programming, and rarely take recent developments in generative models into account. We aim to establish a novel ITS for Python programming that is highly adaptable, serves both as a teaching and research platform, provides interfaces to plug in hint mechanisms (e.g.\ via large language models), and works inside the particularly challenging regulatory environment of Germany, that is, conforming to the European data protection regulation, the European AI act, and ethical framework of the German Research Foundation. In this paper, we present the description of the current state of the ITS along with future development directions, as well as discuss the challenges and opportunities for improving the system.

AI.tldr: Proposes an adaptable Python ITS with interfaces for hint mechanisms (e.g., LLMs), emphasizing regulatory compliance in Germany/Europe and outlining current state and future development directions.
AI.motivation: Address gaps in Python-focused ITS, support advanced programming education, and enable research with plug-in hint mechanisms while complying with stringent data protection and AI governance frameworks.
AI.method: System description of an ITS architecture, interfaces to plug hint mechanisms (including LLMs), and discussion of regulatory/ethical considerations; plan for future development.
AI.result: Descriptive description of the current state of the ITS and a roadmap for future work; no empirical evaluation reported in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: The work identifies challenges and opportunities in building a regulation-compliant, adaptable Python ITS that can serve teaching and research, leveraging generative models.

title: AIFIND: Artifact-Aware Interpreting Fine-Grained Alignment for Incremental Face Forgery Detection
summary: As forgery types continue to emerge consistently, Incremental Face Forgery Detection (IFFD) has become a crucial paradigm. However, existing methods typically rely on data replay or coarse binary supervision, which fails to explicitly constrain the feature space, leading to severe feature drift and catastrophic forgetting. To address this, we propose AIFIND, Artifact-Aware Interpreting Fine-Grained Alignment for Incremental Face Forgery Detection, which leverages semantic anchors to stabilize incremental learning. We design the Artifact-Driven Semantic Prior Generator to instantiate invariant semantic anchors, establishing a fixed coordinate system from low-level artifact cues. These anchors are injected into the image encoder via Artifact-Probe Attention, which explicitly constrains volatile visual features to align with stable semantic anchors. Adaptive Decision Harmonizer harmonizes the classifiers by preserving angular relationships of semantic anchors, maintaining geometric consistency across tasks. Extensive experiments on multiple incremental protocols validate the superiority of AIFIND.

AI.tldr: AIFIND introduces artifact-driven, semantic anchors to stabilize incremental face forgery detection, addressing feature drift and forgetting by constraining features and harmonizing decision boundaries across tasks.
AI.motivation: To mitigate feature drift and catastrophic forgetting in Incremental Face Forgery Detection by stabilizing the feature space with invariant, semantic anchors derived from low-level artifacts.
AI.method: 1) Artifact-Driven Semantic Prior Generator creates fixed semantic anchors from artifact cues; 2) Artifact-Probe Attention injects anchors into the image encoder to constrain features toward stable semantics; 3) Adaptive Decision Harmonizer preserves angular relationships of anchors to maintain geometric consistency across tasks.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on multiple incremental protocols show that AIFIND achieves superior performance and stability compared with baselines, validating the effectiveness of semantic anchors and the proposed modules.
AI.conclusion: Semantic anchors provide a stable, interpretable coordinate system for incremental learning in face forgery detection, enabling better retention of previous knowledge while adapting to new forgery types.

title: Univariate Channel Fusion for Multivariate Time Series Classification
summary: Multivariate time series classification (MTSC) plays a crucial role in various domains, including biomedical signal analysis and motion monitoring. However, existing approaches, particularly deep learning models, often require high computational resources, making them unsuitable for real-time applications or deployment on low-cost hardware, such as IoT devices and wearable systems. In this paper, we propose the Univariate Channel Fusion (UCF) method to deal with MTSC efficiently. UCF transforms multivariate time series into a univariate representation through simple channel fusion strategies such as the mean, median, or dynamic time warping barycenter. This transformation enables the use of any classifier originally designed for univariate time series, providing a flexible and computationally lightweight alternative to complex models. We evaluate UCF in five case studies covering diverse application domains, including chemical monitoring, brain-computer interfaces, and human activity analysis. The results demonstrate that UCF often outperforms baseline methods and state-of-the-art algorithms tailored for MTSC, while achieving substantial gains in computational efficiency, being particularly effective in problems with high inter-channel correlation.

AI.tldr: Transforms multivariate time series into a univariate representation via simple channel fusion (mean, median, or DTW barycenter), enabling use of univariate classifiers for MTSC with improved efficiency; achieves strong performance across five diverse domains, especially when channels are highly correlated.
AI.motivation: MTSC methods, particularly deep learning models, are computationally intensive and ill-suited for real-time or edge deployment on IoT/wearables. A lightweight, flexible approach that leverages existing univariate models is desirable.
AI.method: Convert multivariate time series into a univariate sequence using channel fusion strategies (mean, median, DTW barycenter). The resulting univariate series can then be classified with any classifier designed for univariate time series.
AI.result: Five case studies spanning chemical monitoring, brain–computer interfaces, and human activity analysis show that UCF often surpasses baselines and MTSC SOTA methods while delivering substantial computational gains; performance advantages are pronounced in problems with high inter-channel correlation.
AI.conclusion: UCF offers a flexible and efficient alternative for MTSC by exploiting univariate classifiers, making it suitable for resource-constrained settings without sacrificing accuracy, particularly when inter-channel correlation is high.

title: Training Time Prediction for Mixed Precision-based Distributed Training
summary: Accurate prediction of training time in distributed deep learning is crucial for resource allocation, cost estimation, and job scheduling. We observe that the floating-point precision setting is a key determinant of training time, leading to training time variations of ~2.4x over its minimum. However, existing studies on distributed training time prediction rely on static model computation graphs that do not capture precision variations, including mixed precision. According to our experiments, training time prediction without considering precision results in significant prediction errors - reaching up to 147.85% in mean absolute percentage error (MAPE). To address this issue, we propose a precision-aware distributed training time predictor that achieves robust accuracy across diverse precision settings, including mixed precision, with 9.8% MAPE.

AI.tldr: Precision-aware distributed DL training time prediction reduces error from precision-agnostic baselines (MAPE up to ~147.85%) to 9.8% across diverse precision settings, including mixed precision.
AI.motivation: Training time varies significantly with floating-point precision; static computation-graph-based predictors fail to capture precision effects, leading to large errors. A precision-aware model is needed for accurate resource planning and scheduling in mixed-precision environments.
AI.method: Introduce a precision-aware training time predictor that explicitly models and incorporates precision settings (e.g., mixed precision configurations) into the prediction process, ensuring robustness across a range of precision configurations.
AI.result: The proposed predictor achieves 9.8% MAPE across diverse precision settings, including mixed precision, substantially outperforming precision-agnostic approaches that can incur up to 147.85% MAPE.
AI.conclusion: Accounting for precision variations is essential for accurate training time prediction in distributed DL; the proposed approach enables more reliable resource allocation, cost estimation, and job scheduling in mixed-precision environments.

title: GAViD: A Large-Scale Multimodal Dataset for Context-Aware Group Affect Recognition from Videos
summary: Understanding affective dynamics in real-world social systems is fundamental to modeling and analyzing human-human interactions in complex environments. Group affect emerges from intertwined human-human interactions, contextual influences, and behavioral cues, making its quantitative modeling a challenging computational social systems problem. However, computational modeling of group affect in in-the-wild scenarios remains challenging due to limited large-scale annotated datasets and the inherent complexity of multimodal social interactions shaped by contextual and behavioral variability. The lack of comprehensive datasets annotated with multimodal and contextual information further limits advances in the field. To address this, we introduce the Group Affect from ViDeos (GAViD) dataset, comprising 5091 video clips with multimodal data (video, audio and context), annotated with ternary valence and discrete emotion labels and enriched with VideoGPT-generated contextual metadata and human-annotated action cues. We also present Context-Aware Group Affect Recognition Network (CAGNet) for multimodal context-aware group affect recognition. CAGNet achieves 63.20\% test accuracy on GAViD, comparable to state-of-the-art performance. The dataset and code are available at github.com/deepakkumar-iitr/GAViD.

AI.tldr: Introduces GAViD, a large multimodal dataset for group affect in-the-wild and a context-aware recognition model (CAGNet); achieves 63.20% test accuracy on GAViD, with code and data available.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of large-scale, multimodal, context-rich datasets for in-the-wild group affect and establish a baseline for context-aware multimodal recognition.
AI.method: Create 5091 video clips with multimodal data (video, audio, context); annotate with ternary valence and discrete emotion labels; enrich with VideoGPT-generated contextual metadata and human action cues; propose Context-Aware Group Affect Recognition Network (CAGNet) for multimodal fusion of context features.
AI.result: CAGNet achieves 63.20% test accuracy on GAViD, comparable to state-of-the-art; dataset and code are publicly available at GitHub.
AI.conclusion: The GAViD dataset enables benchmarking of context-aware group affect recognition and demonstrates feasibility of multimodal, context-informed modeling in real-world social scenes; invites further research and improvements.

title: Tabular foundation models for in-context prediction of molecular properties
summary: Accurate molecular property prediction is central to drug discovery, catalysis, and process design, yet real-world applications are often limited by small datasets. Molecular foundation models provide a promising direction by learning transferable molecular representations; however, they typically involve task-specific fine-tuning, require machine learning expertise, and often fail to outperform classical baselines. Tabular foundation models (TFMs) offer a fundamentally different paradigm: they perform predictions through in-context learning, enabling inference without task-specific training. Here, we evaluate TFMs in the low- to medium-data regime across both standardized pharmaceutical benchmarks and chemical engineering datasets. We evaluate both frozen molecular foundation model representations, as well as classical descriptors and fingerprints. Across the benchmarks, the approach shows excellent predictive performance while reducing computational cost, compared to fine-tuning, with these advantages also transferring to practical engineering data settings. In particular, combining TFMs with CheMeleon embeddings yields up to 100\% win rates on 30 MoleculeACE tasks, while compact RDKit2d and Mordred descriptors provide strong descriptor-based alternatives. Molecular representation emerges as a key determinant in TFM performance, with molecular foundation model embeddings and 2D descriptor sets both providing substantial gains over classic molecular fingerprints on many tasks. These results suggest that in-context learning with TFMs provides a highly accurate and cost-efficient alternative for property prediction in practical applications.

AI.tldr: Tabular foundation models enable in-context learning for molecular property prediction in low-to-medium data regimes, achieving competitive accuracy with lower cost; performance strongly depends on molecular representation; CheMeleon embeddings boost performance with high win rates; RDKit2d/Mordred descriptors serve as solid descriptor-based alternatives.
AI.motivation: Address data scarcity and the limitations of task-specific fine-tuning in molecular foundation models. Evaluate tabular foundation models for chemistry, comparing frozen embeddings and classical descriptors/fingerprints, across pharmaceutical and chemical-engineering benchmarks.
AI.method: Benchmark TFMs on low- to medium-data regimes across standardized pharmaceutical benchmarks and chemical engineering datasets. Compare frozen molecular foundation model representations with classical descriptors and fingerprints. Evaluate CheMeleon embeddings and 2D descriptors (RDKit2d, Mordred) as alternatives. Assess predictive performance and computational cost.
AI.result: TFMs show excellent predictive performance with reduced computational cost compared with fine-tuning. CheMeleon embeddings yield up to 100% win rates on 30 MoleculeACE tasks. RDKit2d and Mordred descriptors provide strong descriptor-based alternatives. Molecular representation is a key determinant of TFM performance; both molecular foundation-model embeddings and 2D descriptor sets outperform classic molecular fingerprints on many tasks.
AI.conclusion: In-context learning with TFMs offers a highly accurate and cost-efficient alternative for molecular property prediction in practical applications. The choice of molecular representation is crucial for maximizing TFM performance, with embeddings and 2D descriptors providing substantial gains over fingerprints in many tasks.

title: Dental Panoramic Radiograph Analysis Using YOLO26 From Tooth Detection to Disease Diagnosis
summary: Panoramic radiography is a fundamental diagnostic tool in dentistry, offering a comprehensive view of the entire dentition with minimal radiation exposure. However, manual interpretation is time-consuming and prone to errors, especially in high-volume clinical settings. This creates a pressing need for efficient automated solutions. This study presents the first application of YOLOv26 for automated tooth detection, FDI-based numbering, and dental disease segmentation in panoramic radiographs. The DENTEX dataset was preprocessed using Roboflow for format conversion and augmentation, yielding 1,082 images for tooth enumeration and 1,040 images for disease segmentation across four pathology classes. Five YOLOv26-seg variants were trained on Google Colab using transfer learning at a resolution of 800x800. Results demonstrate that the YOLOv26m-seg model achieved the best performance for tooth enumeration, with a precision of 0.976, recall of 0.970, and box mAP50 of 0.976. It outperformed the YOLOv8x baseline by 4.9% in precision and 3.3% in mAP50, while also enabling high-quality mask-level segmentation (mask mAP50 = 0.970). For disease segmentation, the YOLOv26l-seg model attained a box mAP50 of 0.591 and a mask mAP50 of 0.547. Impacted teeth showed the highest per-class average precision (0.943), indicating that visual distinctiveness influences detection performance more than annotation quantity. Overall, these findings demonstrate that YOLOv26-based models offer a robust and accurate framework for automated dental image analysis, with strong potential to enhance diagnostic efficiency and consistency in clinical practice.

AI.tldr: First application of YOLOv26 for automated tooth detection, FDI numbering, and disease segmentation in panoramic radiographs; strong performance and segmentation quality on DENTEX dataset; per-class insights; potential clinical impact.
AI.motivation: Automate panoramic radiograph analysis to improve diagnostic efficiency and consistency in high-volume dental clinics.
AI.method: Preprocessing with Roboflow; DENTEX dataset: 1,082 enumeration images, 1,040 segmentation images across four pathology classes; five YOLOv26-seg variants trained on Google Colab with transfer learning at 800x800; evaluation on box mAP50 and mask mAP50; comparison against YOLOv8x baseline.
AI.result: TOO: YOLOv26m-seg achieved best tooth enumeration metrics: precision 0.976, recall 0.970, box mAP50 0.976; outperformed YOLOv8x by 4.9% precision and 3.3% mAP50; mask mAP50 0.970. Disease: YOLOv26l-seg box mAP50 0.591, mask mAP50 0.547. Per-class AP: impacted teeth 0.943.
AI.conclusion: YOLOv26-based models provide robust automated dental image analysis with high accuracy and segmentation quality, promising improved diagnostic efficiency and consistency; further validation on broader data is needed.

title: JumpLoRA: Sparse Adapters for Continual Learning in Large Language Models
summary: Adapter-based methods have become a cost-effective approach to continual learning (CL) for Large Language Models (LLMs), by sequentially learning a low-rank update matrix for each task. To mitigate catastrophic forgetting, state-of-the-art approaches impose constraints on new adapters with respect to the previous ones, by targeting either subspace or coordinate-wise interference. In this paper, we propose JumpLoRA, a novel framework to adaptively induce sparsity in the Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) blocks through the use of JumpReLU gating. The method achieves dynamic parameter isolation, which helps prevent task interference. We demonstrate that our method is highly modular and compatible with LoRA-based CL approaches. Specifically, it significantly boosts the performance of IncLoRA and outperforms the leading state-of-the-art CL method, ELLA.

AI.tldr: JumpLoRA introduces JumpReLU-based gating to induce adaptive sparsity in LoRA blocks for continual learning in LLMs, enabling dynamic parameter isolation and improving IncLoRA while beating ELLA.
AI.motivation: Address catastrophic forgetting and cross-task interference in adapter-based continual learning for large language models by enabling selective, sparse, task-specific updates within LoRA blocks.
AI.method: Apply JumpReLU gating to LoRA blocks to dynamically prune/update parameters, achieving per-task isolation. The approach is designed to be modular and compatible with existing LoRA-based CL frameworks, notably IncLoRA, and is claimed to outperform the SOTA method ELLA.
AI.result: The method is reported to significantly boost the performance of IncLoRA and to outperform ELLA, indicating stronger interference mitigation and effective reuse of shared knowledge.
AI.conclusion: JumpLoRA offers a novel, gating-based sparsity mechanism that enables dynamic parameter isolation in LoRA-based continual learning, presenting a practical plug-in improvement with compatibility to existing CL approaches; further details and broader benchmarking would help validate its generality.

title: A Two-Stage, Object-Centric Deep Learning Framework for Robust Exam Cheating Detection
comment: 7 pages, 5 figures. Accepted at the FISU Joint Conference on Artificial Intelligence (FJCAI 2026), Vietnam
summary: Academic integrity continues to face the persistent challenge of examination cheating. Traditional invigilation relies on human observation, which is inefficient, costly, and prone to errors at scale. Although some existing AI-powered monitoring systems have been deployed and trusted, many lack transparency or require multi-layered architectures to achieve the desired performance. To overcome these challenges, we propose an improvement over a simple two-stage framework for exam cheating detection that integrates object detection and behavioral analysis using well-known technologies. First, the state-of-the-art YOLOv8n model is used to localize students in exam-room images. Each detected region is cropped and preprocessed, then classified by a fine-tuned RexNet-150 model as either normal or cheating behavior. The system is trained on a dataset compiled from 10 independent sources with a total of 273,897 samples, achieving 0.95 accuracy, 0.94 recall, 0.96 precision, and 0.95 F1-score - a 13\% increase over a baseline accuracy of 0.82 in video-based cheating detection. In addition, with an average inference time of 13.9 ms per sample, the proposed approach demonstrates robustness and scalability for deployment in large-scale environments. Beyond the technical contribution, the AI-assisted monitoring system also addresses ethical concerns by ensuring that final outcomes are delivered privately to individual students after the examination, for example, via personal email. This prevents public exposure or shaming and offers students an opportunity to reflect on their behavior. For further improvement, it is possible to incorporate additional factors, such as audio data and consecutive frames, to achieve greater accuracy. This study provides a foundation for developing real-time, scalable, ethical, and open-source solutions.

AI.tldr: A two-stage AI system for exam cheating detection using YOLOv8n for localization and RexNet-150 for behavior classification, trained on 273,897 samples from 10 sources, achieving ~0.95 accuracy with low latency; includes privacy-preserving reporting and notes potential for audio/frame enhancements.
AI.motivation: Address inefficiencies and errors of human invigilation while delivering transparent, scalable, and privacy-conscious monitoring. The approach aims to provide real-time, open-source solutions for exam integrity.
AI.method: Use YOLOv8n to localize students in exam-room images, crop detected regions, and classify as normal or cheating with a fine-tuned RexNet-150. Trained on a dataset compiled from 10 sources (n=273,897). Evaluated with accuracy, recall, precision, F1, and inference time (13.9 ms/sample).
AI.result: Performance: 0.95 accuracy, 0.94 recall, 0.96 precision, 0.95 F1; 13.9 ms per sample; 13% improvement over a 0.82 baseline in video-based cheating detection. Privacy-preserving delivery of results to students after exams (e.g., via email).
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of a real-time, scalable exam-monitoring pipeline with ethical reporting safeguards. Establishes a foundation for open-source solutions and indicates directions for enhancement (e.g., audio data, consecutive frames).

title: Synthetic data in cryptocurrencies using generative models
summary: Data plays a fundamental role in consolidating markets, services, and products in the digital financial ecosystem. However, the use of real data, especially in the financial context, can lead to privacy risks and access restrictions, affecting institutions, research, and modeling processes. Although not all financial datasets present such limitations, this work proposes the use of deep learning techniques for generating synthetic data applied to cryptocurrency price time series. The approach is based on Conditional Generative Adversarial Networks (CGANs), combining an LSTM-type recurrent generator and an MLP discriminator to produce statistically consistent synthetic data. The experiments consider different crypto-assets and demonstrate that the model is capable of reproducing relevant temporal patterns, preserving market trends and dynamics. The generation of synthetic series through GANs is an efficient alternative for simulating financial data, showing potential for applications such as market behavior analysis and anomaly detection, with lower computational cost compared to more complex generative approaches.

AI.tldr: CGAN-based synthetic data for crypto price time series using an LSTM generator and an MLP discriminator; preserves temporal patterns and market dynamics, enabling privacy-preserving data generation and cheaper simulation for market analysis and anomaly detection.
AI.motivation: Privacy risks and access restrictions in real financial data motivate synthetic data generation. The work targets cryptocurrency price series to enable research, modeling, and monitoring without exposing sensitive data.
AI.method: A conditional GAN where a recurrent LSTM-type generator produces synthetic price sequences and an MLP discriminator assesses realism. The CGAN is applied to cryptocurrency assets to learn temporal dependencies and generate statistically consistent synthetic time series.
AI.result: The model reproduces key temporal patterns and preserves market trends across different crypto assets. Generated series can be used for market behavior analysis and anomaly detection, with lower computational cost than more complex generative models.
AI.conclusion: CGAN-based synthetic generation is effective for financial time series, offering privacy-preserving data augmentation and a computationally efficient tool for analysis and anomaly detection in crypto markets.

title: CollideNet: Hierarchical Multi-scale Video Representation Learning with Disentanglement for Time-To-Collision Forecasting
summary: Time-to-Collision (TTC) forecasting is a critical task in collision prevention, requiring precise temporal prediction and comprehending both local and global patterns encapsulated in a video, both spatially and temporally. To address the multi-scale nature of video, we introduce a novel spatiotemporal hierarchical transformer-based architecture called CollideNet, specifically catered for effective TTC forecasting. In the spatial stream, CollideNet aggregates information for each video frame simultaneously at multiple resolutions. In the temporal stream, along with multi-scale feature encoding, CollideNet also disentangles the non-stationarity, trend, and seasonality components. Our method achieves state-of-the-art performance in comparison to prior works on three commonly used public datasets, setting a new state-of-the-art by a considerable margin. We conduct cross-dataset evaluations to analyze the generalization capabilities of our method, and visualize the effects of disentanglement of the trend and seasonality components of the video data. We release our code at https://github.com/DeSinister/CollideNet/.

AI.tldr: CollideNet proposes a spatiotemporal hierarchical transformer for time-to-collision (TTC) forecasting, combining multi-resolution spatial aggregation with multi-scale temporal encoding and disentanglement of non-stationarity (trend and seasonality); it achieves state-of-the-art results on three public datasets, shows cross-dataset generalization, and releases code.
AI.motivation: TTC forecasting demands precise temporal prediction and robust understanding of both local and global spatiotemporal video patterns. Videos exhibit non-stationarity (trend, seasonality) and multi-scale structure, requiring architectures that capture multi-scale context and disentangle non-stationary components for reliable forecasting. The goal is to improve accuracy and generalization over existing methods.
AI.method: A spatiotemporal hierarchical transformer-based architecture (CollideNet) with two streams: a spatial stream that aggregates frame information at multiple resolutions per frame, and a temporal stream that performs multi-scale feature encoding and disentangles non-stationarity into trend and seasonality components, leveraging hierarchical attention mechanisms to capture local/global patterns.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance on three public TTC datasets; evidence of generalization via cross-dataset evaluation; visualization of the disentangled trend and seasonality components; code released at GitHub.
AI.conclusion: CollideNet effectively handles multi-scale spatiotemporal patterns and non-stationarity in video for TTC forecasting, delivering superior accuracy and generalization, with open-source code to facilitate adoption and further research.

title: Find, Fix, Reason: Context Repair for Video Reasoning
summary: Reinforcement learning has advanced video reasoning in large multi-modal models, yet dominant pipelines either rely on on-policy self-exploration, which plateaus at the model's knowledge boundary, or hybrid replay that mixes policies and demands careful regularization. Dynamic context methods zoom into focused evidence but often require curated pretraining and two-stage tuning, and their context remains bounded by a small model's capability. In contrast, larger models excel at instruction following and multi-modal understanding, can supply richer context to smaller models, and rapidly zoom in on target regions via simple tools. Building on this capability, we introduce an observation-level intervention: a frozen, tool-integrated teacher identifies the missing spatiotemporal dependency and provides a minimal evidence patch (e.g., timestamps, regions etc.) from the original video while the question remains unchanged. The student answers again with the added context, and training updates with a chosen-rollout scheme integrated into Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). We further propose a Robust Improvement Reward (RIR) that aligns optimization with two goals: outcome validity through correct answers and dependency alignment through rationales that reflect the cited evidence. Advantages are group-normalized across the batch, preserving on-policy exploration while directing it along causally meaningful directions with minimal changes to the training stack. Experiments on various related benchmarks show consistent accuracy gains and strong generalization. Web page and source code will be available at https://github.com/JethroJames/FFR.git.

AI.tldr: Observation-level intervention: a frozen, tool-integrated teacher supplies minimal evidence patches from the video to guide a student; training uses a chosen-rollout GRPO with a Robust Improvement Reward, yielding on-policy exploration directed by causal dependencies and improved accuracy/generalization in video reasoning.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of on-policy self-exploration, hybrid replay, and bounded-context dynamic methods; capitalize on larger models' ability to provide richer context and targeted evidence to smaller models to improve dependency alignment and reasoning.
AI.method: Introduce a frozen teacher with tool integration that identifies missing spatiotemporal dependencies and returns minimal evidence patches (e.g., timestamps, regions) without changing the question; train the student with a chosen-rollout scheme embedded in Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO); define Robust Improvement Reward (RIR) to reward correct answers and dependency-aligned rationales; apply group-normalization to preserve on-policy exploration.
AI.result: Experiments on related benchmarks show consistent accuracy gains and strong generalization.
AI.conclusion: Shows that minimal, evidence-grounded interventions plus a causality-aligned reward can steer on-policy learning in large multimodal settings, enabling better video reasoning; code and web resources will be released.

title: Sketching the Readout of Large Language Models for Scalable Data Attribution and Valuation
summary: Data attribution and valuation are critical for understanding data-model synergy for Large Language Models (LLMs), yet existing gradient-based methods suffer from scalability challenges on LLMs. Inspired by human cognition, where decision making relies on a focused readout of relevant memories rather than replaying all pathways, we introduce RISE (Readout Influence Sketching Estimator). Instead of computing and indexing gradients across the entire LLM, RISE focuses on influence hotspots at the output layer, where influence signals concentrate, and the gradient admits a decomposed outer-product form. This enables a dual-channel representation combining a lexical residual channel (RH) and a semantic projected-error channel (GH). Applying CountSketch projections to these channels achieves strong compression while maintaining accurate attribution. Across the OLMo (1B-32B) and Pythia (14M-6.9B) families, RISE reduces index storage by up to 112$\times$ compared to RapidIn and scales to 32B parameters LLM, where gradient-based baselines such as RapidIn and ZO-Inf become memory-infeasible. We evaluate RISE on two paradigms: (1) retrospective attribution, retrieving influential training examples for specific predictions, and (2) prospective valuation, scoring candidate data utility zero-shot. We validate RISE on three tasks: Howdy backdoor data detection, Finance-Medical domain separation, and Brain Rot high-quality data selection. In a closed-loop Brain Rot study, continued pretraining on RISE-selected data yields consistent downstream improvements. Overall, RISE provides a practical and scalable primitive for influence analysis and training-data selection in modern large language models.

AI.tldr: RISE is a scalable, gradient-free influence estimator for LLM data attribution and valuation that uses an output-layer readout focus with dual channels (lexical residual RH and semantic projected-error GH) and CountSketch compression, enabling efficient training-data selection across large models with substantial storage reductions.
AI.motivation: Gradient-based attribution methods do not scale well to large language models; a cognitively inspired approach that focuses on influential readouts can enable practical data attribution/valuation.
AI.method: RISE exploits an outer-product decomposition of gradients at the output layer, introducing two channels (RH: lexical residual; GH: semantic projected error). It applies CountSketch projections to compress these channels, producing a dual-channel representation that preserves attribution quality while reducing storage. It supports retrospective attribution and prospective data-valuation across LLM families (OLMo and Pythia).
AI.result: RISE achieves up to 112× reduction in index storage versus RapidIn and scales to 32B-parameter LLMs, where traditional gradient-based baselines (RapidIn, ZO-Inf) become memory-infeasible. It is validated on tasks including Howdy backdoor data detection, Finance-Medical domain separation, and Brain Rot data selection, with Brain Rot showing downstream improvements when trained on RISE-selected data.
AI.conclusion: RISE offers a practical, scalable primitive for influence analysis and training-data selection in modern LLMs, enabling efficient attribution and valuation without full gradient indexing.

title: Neuro-Symbolic ODE Discovery with Latent Grammar Flow
summary:
